Posted: November 24
Maybe my favorite FromSoft title (in competition with Bloodborne), Elden Ring is the most ambitious of the Soulsborne games and it's an ambition that pays off. The world of the Lands Between is an addicting place and the sheer freedom and variety of combat and builds alone would mark this as one of my favorites. In my opinion, boss design is the best it's ever been in Elden Ring with all of the main boss fights (and a lot of the optional) being incredibly memorable. Lore wise, Elden Ring has surpassed even Dark Souls with its complexity and depth and I love how it has it's own unique feel and tone just like the original Dark Souls.

Now that I'm (mostly) done praising it, here are some issues I have. ER reuses a lot of boss assets for optional bosses, to a point where it's very noticeable and feels a bit lazy. This might be a byproduct of this being FromSoft's first open world game and maybe they underestimated the amount of unique bosses they would need. Additionally, ER has this weird obsession of making enemies jump around a corner to attack you. Like a lot, to the point where I always check corners in dungeons and usually find an enemy waiting to spook me. Kind of annoying, not a dealbreaker imo.

I'm not gonna bother going to Shadow of the Erdtree's actual store page so I'll talk about it here: It is a beautiful, expansive area that is way overtuned in difficulty to the point of being unenjoyable. I've tried on two separate occasions to get through it and there's just something about the pacing and enemies that doesn't make it fun. Even defeating bosses (like Messmer) isn't even satisfying, you're just glad it's over. I don't know what exactly makes the DLC feel this way but the base game just feels better in almost every respect. Boss design and spectacle is great but the time investment for how hard it is just makes it not worth it.

That aside, TL;DR Elden Ring is amazing and you should get it.

Posted: November 23
Well butter my biscuits, Elden Ring.
Where do I even start? There’s so much here that I don’t know which end of this review to grab first. In short: this game is a masterpiece and, speaking personally, FromSoftware’s greatest work to date.

A stunning open world offering HUNDREDS (yes, literally hundreds) of hours of exploration. And that’s not even counting the DLC, which has almost as much content as the base game itself. Hundreds of bosses to defeat, mountains of gear to collect, countless builds to try, hidden passages everywhere, secrets behind every corner.

The game isn’t quite as punishing as some other titles from the studio (yes, Sekiro, I’m looking at you), but you’ll still "hate" this game in certain spots (Haligtree, my beloved…).

Boss design is absolutely top-tier. Every fight is a challenge - sometimes mild, sometimes brutal. (Yes, I got my butt handed to me the first time I fought Morgott.) The beauty of it all lies in learning their patterns (*coughMaleniacough*) and reacting properly… or just dumping all your points into Strength and going for the classic "bonk" build. There’s something for everyone.

I simply can’t not recommend this game. I cannot wrap my head around it, but there is something about this game, that itch, which makes you just play it again and again and again. Whether you’re a Souls veteran or someone touching the series for the first time: don’t get discouraged by the early stumbles. It’s truly worth it.

Practice makes perfect, so RISE NOW, YE TARNISHED! YE DEAD, WHO YET LIVE!

Posted: November 23
If you care about good story telling or a compelling narrative this is not the game for you.

Not because the story is convoluted, or because it's hard to follow, but because it is utterly and absolutely meaningless. Nothing you do matters in the end. Characters act as if nothing changed, literally, they act like you didn't actually do anything, likr they didn't even notice it, despite you supposedly having just completely turned the world on its head. Nope, not even some cheap repetitive filler comment from the very few characters left hanging around.

They say these games are punishing, the gameplay is fine, the most punishing part is how little care was put into having any sort of closure to a somewhat decent adventure.

Having finished the game more than once, and being disappointed more than once, I cannot recommend this game, based on *my* perspective.

If you want a challenging game, with interesting (but completely meaningless) lore, it's a decent game.

Posted: November 23
TL;DR: Game is big and beautiful, my first full playthrough was over 100 hours. It's hard to enjoy this game if you aren't willing to take time to explore and strengthen your character. Bosses are tough, some are really, really tough and can be super frustrating, but nothing is impossible and there are plenty of ways to make things easier if you want. If you're new to these games, I wouldn't recommend Elden Ring first, since it might be overwhelming, or because it might make the older games feel too bad to play if you end up going back to try them.

The first time I played this game, I came away from it thinking I hated it. I had played about 30 or so hours and just was not having a good time. I ended up not wanting to take time exploring since most of the early areas that I did look into didn't have much else that seemed worthwhile. This led to me not having many upgrades or level ups as I stubbornly plowed forward, which in turn made me enjoy the combat and bosses even less because I was underpowered and didn't have room to make a lot of mistakes. I ended up in Altus Plateau, which is right around the middle of the game, having only defeated one big boss, feeling weak and frustrated, and I just decided to stop playing for a while.

Some time later, I wanted to give the game another chance. I still knew very little about it outside of what I had played before. And my actual 'first' playthrough ended up taking about 106 hours. In that time, I explored as much as I could, beat every major boss, and cleared almost every marked location in the game (this was before the DLC was even announced). After that, and much more time and many more playthroughs, and collecting I think every single item in the whole game on one character, I now consider this to be my second favorite of the FromSoft games that I've played. Sekiro is still my #1 because I enjoy that combat system the most.

Elden Ring is massive and there are dozens of places you can go to as soon as you get out of the starter zone. Run into a troublesome boss? Find a place where the regular enemies kill you in 1-2 hits? Pop a marker on your map and go somewhere else, there are guaranteed to be plenty of other spots to check out and clear out to get your character a bit stronger. Come back some other time. If Dark Souls is a river and its tributaries, Elden Ring is more like the Great Lakes or something. There's no set and distinct path, everything is spread out, and you will miss a ton of stuff if you only beeline in one direction. Now, that's not the end of the world, really, because for as big as the world is, only small parts are strictly necessary for progression. And the items you do find might not be relevant to your build. But you do risk missing vital upgrades to scale your character and weapons if you don't look around and fight stuff. Another side effect of the huge world is that FromSoft's obsession with vague and confusing NPC questlines is even harder to grapple with because it's harder to find people when they move. I think those are the only things I looked up during my first playthrough because I just couldn't figure out where some people went and what to do for some of them.

The game just looks really nice when you're exploring, though. FromSoft has never been on the cutting edge of graphics tech but their art direction is very, very good, and there are some vistas and spectacles in this game that genuinely impressed me. They've never been great at optimization either, though, so do some research about performance if you've got dated hardware.

Now, the difficulty. This game is hard. If you're stubborn like me and don't want any help or overpowered tools, then I think Elden Ring is probably the hardest game in the lineage. The enemy and boss design is overall much more complex and challenging to learn than any of the previous games, and the toughest fights top anything that came before. The game also scales up quite sharply later on, with enemy health and damage getting kind of crazy into the endgame. If you want to get really good at this game without relying on powerful items or summons, you'll need to focus on learning the patterns of each boss or elite enemy. Many of them are designed to trick your natural reactions and expectations, so you just have pay attention and try until you find the right moves to make. How to position yourself, which direction to dodge, what can and can't be blocked/parried, when to attack and when not to, when it's safe to heal, which attacks chain into which other attacks, etc.

If you're more willing to use whatever the game gives you, however, then I think it can potentially be one of the easiest in the series. Elden Ring allows the player to become much more powerful than ever before. Lots of enemy groups and bosses around means lots of runes over time, so you can get lots of levels, and many stats scale higher than they did in previous games. Get a crafting kit early on and you can make lots of consumable items to help yourself if you can find recipes and gather materials. Most ordinary weapons can be made quite powerful with different Ashes of War (special moves that you can swap out) and damage affinities, and there are lots of very powerful unique weapons, some of which can be obtained very early. With enough stat investment, magic can yield devastating damage while keeping yourself away from harm. Solo players now have access to helpers without actual multiplayer, in the form of Spirit Ashes. And again, you can almost always back off from a tough area or boss and come back later after clearing out more manageable locations and getting a bit stronger.

This is FromSoft's most popular game to date, and I think that Elden Ring occupies this weird space where it is, in some ways, more accessible and beginner-friendly than previous Souls games. At least it seems to be with the open-world design and the greater freedom that it gives to the player. Even the base player mechanics feel, in my opinion, the best they've ever been in a Souls game, to the point where going back to the older games can feel pretty rough. But I feel like the size and scope of this game could just as easily be too much to take in for some people. If you're the kind of player who prefers to have a clear goal and way forward, with more natural progression that simply unfolds as you go, you might not like how the world is designed. If you want great and exciting treasure around every corner, you may find yourself disappointed more often than not. You might enjoy roaming around, only to have your spirit crushed whenever you run into a boss that makes you eat dirt. There's a fair bit about this game that I don't totally enjoy, but the high points outweigh the lows for me, I've gotten used to it all and just take it as it is. It's been climbing up my list of most played games and threatens to take the top spot eventually.

Posted: November 21
Overview
As someone new to the souls-like game format, I found Elden Ring to be a wonderful change of pace in my usual gaming routine. There is so much to love about the game, but at the same time, there are various areas for improvement.

Gameplay & Systems
If you’re like me, the combat and movement will be a challenge to initially pick up. Basic actions like dodging and strafing will be awkward at first, and parrying will be out of the question until you have practiced against some basic enemies. However, overtime I noticed and felt a real progression that made every consecutive dodge and parry a satisfying celebration. Progression feels earned, and although most bosses have a “cheese” strategy, defeating them through skill alone makes for an exciting experience unique to your character build. In addition, progression through stats, gear, and spells feels rewarding. That being said, there are plenty of options to customize your character and really get into your desired build. The gameplay loop feels repetitive at times, especially with catacombs and dungeons. While most bosses have a unique moveset/strengths/weaknesses- it is easy to fall into the same couple moves each fight when they seem to get the job done. That did not bother me, it added to my experience, but is something to keep in mind if you prefer to mix things up.

Narrative & Worldbuilding
The worldbuilding is phenomenal. I found myself searching online in forums for answers to pieces of lore I missed out on, and fell in love with the world with each new discussion. There are bits of lore thrown throughout and a beautiful cast of characters to uncover. The world feels consistent, I never felt as though the worldbuilding was unfinished or dull. Each piece of gear you find has lore that fills in the blanks, which just added so much to my experience. Contrastingly, I did find the NPC’s to be annoying to keep up with. There are no quest markers and it is very easy to overlook a step in a quest. I found myself following a guide on my second playthrough to ensure I followed everyone’s story to the very end. Throughout that experience, I kept remarking on how impossible these steps would feel for a newer player. Even an experienced player would have trouble recalling all the steps necessary for the many interconnected unmarked quests. On a positive note, the NPC’s have fairly flushed out stories and personalities. They don’t all feel “alive” but I found myself getting attached to quite a few.

Visual & Audio Presentation
I won’t lie. At first, I did not find the graphics to be impressive. That was until I stopped hoping for a photorealistic game and instead looked at the game with their unique artstyle in mind. If you are a fan of their at-times surreal atmosphere, you will find it breathtaking. I certainly enjoyed all the many cliffs and ledges that allowed me to just take the whole scenery in. Walking around the world just feels so encapsulating. At times, I forgot where my screen ended and the game began. However, certain textures left much to be desired (i.e. the two dimensional doors to the mausoleum that just seem to contrast with a rather detailed game.) Bosses are for the most part beautifully designed, especially the first few main bosses. As for the soundtrack, I enjoyed it but have nothing impactful to say about it. It was fitting for the game, but the only personal standout were the horns and trumpets that certain enemies play.

Performance & Technical Stability
I experienced the occasional freeze and on rare occasions a crash. Otherwise, the game ran smoothly. That being said, I have a stronger than average PC. I could see certain clustered areas causing major drops in frames if your PC can’t handle the overwhelming movements and attack special effects. There were a few bugs that interrupted my game. Of course, I enjoy finding glitches for fun and still found a few that could “cheese” certain bosses.

Difficulty & Accessibility
This is a difficult game for beginners. However, with the number of builds and optimizations you can make for your character, anyone can find a way to beat the game. If you do find yourself stuck, you can always ask for help. Each enemy has a weakness and can be exploited to make your life easy. I personally enjoyed using the most overpowered spells and attacks to otherwise destroy difficult enemies. The tutorial teaches you the essentials, but more advanced skills will require a lot of experimentation or a quick search online to find a more indepth tutorial. Parrying for example, requires a lot of both. If you practice, you will get better. Don’t let the constant stream of “skill issues” tear you down. I have very little skill and still found a way to beat every boss in the game. Worst comes to worst, summon an ally or another player. It is a lot of fun to beat down on a boss with someone at your side to pull aggro.

Areas for Improvement
The main areas of improvement for Elden Ring are the poor implementation of platforming and the lack of guidance with NPC related quests. Platforming is the absolute worst part of this game. The only blessing is that it is not too common in the game unless you are chasing a platinum. I am the worst at platforming in general and most of my deaths came from fall damage on failed jumps. Platforming is horrendous in this game, especially on the Torrent (Spectral Steed/Horse). I almost gave a poor review just for how gut wrenchingly awful the platforming is. That aside, a simple quest log for NPCs would be a huge improvement to the absent approach they currently have at play.

Conclusion
TLDR: Difficult to get into for new players but rewarding overtime. Has amazing progression and replay value with lots of opportunity for customization. Worldbuilding is exceptional and graphics will either be a hit or miss depending on your taste. Performs well on higher end PC’s but may have some issues on a weaker rig. If you are a fan of soul-like gameplay you will enjoy it.

CONSENSUS 🍅
Mechanically brilliant and offering a freedom rivaling "Tears of the Kingdom," Elden Ring is a triumph of open-world design, though its relentlessly grim atmosphere and claustrophobic dungeons may alienate players seeking a sense of hope or comfort.


FULL REVIEW 📝
Score: 87% (Fresh) 🌿

I am not a "Dark Souls" fan. In fact, I usually dislike the tight, punishing corridors of FromSoftware games. Surprisingly, I liked Elden Ring—but with a major caveat.

The open world here is incredible 🌍. It gave me that same sense of wonder I felt in , allowing me to simply ride off and explore whenever I hit a wall. This freedom is why I stuck around as long as I did.

However, the "Legacy Dungeons" (like Stormveil 🏰) felt like a step backward for me. They forced me back into that classic, claustrophobic "Souls" design I usually try to avoid.

But my biggest hurdle wasn't the difficulty—it was the vibe. The world is breathtaking, but it is 🌑. Unlike the adventurous spirit of Zelda, this world is oppressive, decaying, and genuinely uneasy to exist in. It didn't just make me feel challenged; it often made me feel anxious and sad.

It is a mechanically perfect game, but it’s a heavy one to carry.


A masterpiece of exploration trapped inside a nightmare. If you love open worlds but hate feeling constantly on edge or depressed by your surroundings, this 87% comes with a heavy warning. ⚠️

This review is a bunch of nitpicking but the more I go back and play the older From games the more I'm disappointed with this game:

The open world itself is forgettable since it poses zero challenge to the player. You can just turn your brain off and run past every enemy with zero punishment. People still reminisce about their first time in Blight Town or facing those two guys down the well in Bloodborne. There is literally nothing of sort in Elden Ring.

The small dungeons spread out in the open world sometimes have interesting gimmicks but usually end with you killing a reused boss and being rewarded something you'll never end up using. Bloodborne chalices had random layouts, a decent amount of enemies/bosses not in the regular game and gave you a reason to do them in Blood gems. All that and most people still skipped them. Ubisoft level bloat for people who don't realize more playtime doesn't make a game better.

Legacy dungeons, you don't even want to explore because after doing a few you realize the only decent content the game has are the bosses. DS1 was praised for the inteconnected world, DS2 and 3 still had shortcuts you could discover by exploring. The level design once again devolved with ER, with a grace around every corner the shortcuts and the relief from finally finding one are mostly gone.

Bosses, I've died to Malenia, Messmer and Radahn dozens of times yet none of them gave me the same dopamine hit from killing Gael or Orphan in maybe one fifth of the tries. It feels like 90% of the challenge with all these big bad bosses are their one or two crazy moves. You'll die over and over to Waterfowl or the big snake combo Messmer has until you figure out when and which way to roll or run. You'll never get that adrenaline rush from getting a boss to 5-10% your first try because these moves solely exist to make older players not whine about everything else by giving the illusion of difficulty while still letting the new "broader audience" tank half the combos with their summon. This contrast in difficulty between the world and the bosses also makes the game feel like a boss rush rather than an adventure like the older games.

Narrative, they don't leave things to imagination much compared to the older games. Ranni probably blogs more about the story than the entirety of DS1 dialogue. Around the same amount of NPC questlines in a much bigger world makes the world feel even more barren. Through 3 playthroughs I haven't noticed a Solaire, Lucatiel or Anri. Someone on a similar journey, living their own life you come across multiple times. Maybe they exist but the world isn't interesting enough for me to go find them.

Multiplayer, covenants are gone, invasions are opt-in. All the fun sucked out of it because god forbid the people who already look up everything online would have to look up how to turn invasions off too.

It's still an okay game but I'm worried where FromSoft is heading. I don't care if some guy who didn't have the dedication to beat the older games now gets to have fun beating bosses with spells/summons/weapon arts as long as it doesn't end up making the game a worse experience but the multiple steps backwards taken in level/world design, narrative, multiplayer are clearly an attempt to make the game more appealing to them, and it affects everyone.
Even the content people create has gone downhill, from meme videos, challenges and invasions to weapon/spell/x rankings, how to cheese x boss and loreslop (♥♥♥♥ VAATI)

Rating: ★★★★★ / A masterpiece that stays with you long after you close the game

Elden Ring didn’t just “impress” me. It changed me in that weird way some games do when they’re so beautiful, so heavy, and so painfully human beneath all the monsters that you can’t help but carry them with you.

FromSoftware didn’t give me a story.
They handed me a world that remembers, even when the people inside it can’t.

Every step feels like wandering through the ghost of a civilization, touching places that used to matter to someone. You don’t get exposition you get ruins, broken statues, forgotten prayers, a sword at a grave, a melody hummed by someone who’s already gone. The game trusts you to feel your way through its grief.

And the bosses?
God. Even the most terrifying ones feel like tragedies wearing armor.

No giant screaming monster ever felt like “just a fight.”
They all felt like someone who had already lost long before you arrived someone reaching for what little they had left. You don’t just dodge attacks; you survive moments that feel like elegies.

Combat is brutal, but fair.
Beautiful, but cruel.
And when you finally overcome something impossible, it doesn’t feel like triumph.
It feels like release.

Exploring the world is intoxicating.
Sometimes you wander into somewhere breathtaking.
Sometimes you wander into somewhere that hates you personally.
Sometimes you stand on a cliff alone, watching a sunset bleed across a world that refuses to die.

Elden Ring isn’t comforting.
But it is honest in a way that very few games ever dare to be.

And when the credits rolled, I didn’t feel done.
I felt… changed.
Like I had lived through something ancient and sad and beautiful, and I was lucky to have seen it at all.

If you want an adventure that respects you, challenges you, breaks you, and makes you grateful for every victory… Elden Ring is that rare, once-in-a-generation masterpiece.

This is one of the most well made games I've seen in all my history of playing games as a whole. There is so much content and most of it doesn't seem to get old. This game has a show and don't tell form of storytelling. This means if you're interested in the game's lore, you have to either write down most of the dialogue communicated through the NPCs or you have to look up lore discussion videos. The lore is extremely scattered and if you want to learn about one thing, you usually have to search for consistencies in dialogue from one NPC to another. Some NPCs will openly lie to you while others will border the line of insanity and coherency. It's up to you as the player to determine who's intel is reliable or not.
Pros:
- Vast open world.
- Great combat mechanics. It's not the best I've ever seen, but it's the boss fights you play the game for.
- Deep lore rich story.
- Beautifully crafted environments.
- Good graphics.
- Extremely replayable with different build and story options.
Cons:
- The game needs a radial menu to select spells and items. Cycling through items is too inefficient.
- Lore is difficult to engage.
- After playing Monster Hunter games, the normal weapon attack options feels limited.

Before buying the steam version, I already had 2 full playthroughs of this game on xbox, totaling about 160 hours. Currently on my fourth playthrough (second one with expansion).

I can now confidently claim this is the best singleplayer game I have ever played. It is my comfort zone: My happy place when I'm stressed. The game that makes me wish I could forget it all and experience it for the first time again. It's an open world game where you explore out of curiousity and wonder, not because you have a shopping list of objectives to complete. You can build your character in completely different ways, offering a completely different experience every time.

It's also the fromsoft game that offers you the most flexibility in how easy or how hard you want to make things for yourself. Hitting a difficulty wall? Go do something else in the game, because there is so, so much to discover, and this is something that sets Elden Ring apart from other fromsoft games: Come back when you are level appropriate, or come back overpowered. Still difficulties with a fight? Summon some people and cheese the hell out of it.

In a way, Elden Ring serves as a tutorial in how to play fromsoft games. After my first playthrough, I wanted more and tried Dark Souls again, which I tried and gave up several times before. And this time, it all clicked: Playing through Elden Ring primes you for 'understanding' the souls series, and for the first time ever, I absolutely enjoyed Dark Souls and it's quirks. I understand why it's one of the best games ever made, and played through the entire souls series from beginning to end: After that, I went back for a new Elden Ring playthrough: That is how much I adore these games now, while previously I would dismiss them as "too hard just for the sake of it".

FromSoft games are not particularly hard (let's ignore Sekiro): Your mother could probably finish Dark Souls and Elden Ring: they are meant to be experienced, fully immersed in, learn from your mistakes. There is no handholding: The player's agency is deeply respected. Elden Ring is like the super version of that: "Here is a huge world. We won't tell you how to play this game, go figure it out". There is no list of quests for you to complete or items to collect. There are storylines you can progress in, but they are entirely optional. You might accidently stumble upon them, or you can make a conscious effort to follow up on them. The game doesn't really care: It's your journey. No really, play how you want. Do you want twitchy "pro gamer" gameplay? Get a parry shield and master the unreasonably strict parry windows for each fight. Want to just stand there and block instead? Get a 100% block shield, and just keep your shield up when the enemy attacks. Don't want to deal with shields at all? Just go 2-handed (EVERY weapon can be 2-handed for more damage) and just dodge and circle around the enemy as needed, because the dodge window is much more generous than the parry window. You feel like even dodging is too much for you? Go for a ranged build. Want a bow, crossbow, magic, pyromancy? You name it. There is even more variation than that, and the expansion greatly expands on that still. Experiment! See what works. Again, curiousity gets greatly rewarded.
There is all sorts of crazy items in the game, and weapons can even be expanded with special skills using "ashes of war". Don't sleep on these: Innocent looking ones can be crazy strong to use, but it's so easy to forget about them and never use them at all. The game isn't going to remind you. It's not going to tell you anything at all about damage types either, though observing and learning about them -will- make fights a lot easier.

The game doesn't assume you are an idiot, like almost every other open world rpg does, and I believe that is one of the biggest aspects that makes Elden Ring as satisfying as it is.

After well over 150 hours across platforms — including a full playthrough on Xbox and 85+ hours on Steam — I can confidently say that Elden Ring earns its reputation. FromSoftware took everything they’ve learned over the years and built a world that rewards curiosity, patience, and experimentation more than any of their previous titles.

The combat is demanding but fair, giving you the freedom to approach encounters with whatever playstyle you gravitate toward. Heavy weapon bruiser, nimble bleed build, magic artillery, hybrid experiments — the game genuinely supports it all. Bosses hit hard, but they’re memorable fights that feel like puzzles you solve through mastery rather than luck. When you finally win, the sense of accomplishment is unmatched.

Exploration is where Elden Ring truly shines. The open world feels handcrafted rather than procedural, packed with secrets, ruins, optional dungeons, and unexpected discoveries that make wandering off the beaten path irresistible. Even after dozens of hours, I kept stumbling into areas I had completely missed. It’s one of the few open worlds where exploration feels consistently meaningful.

The story is cryptic but rich, and while the narrative is presented in a minimalistic way, the atmosphere and environmental storytelling more than make up for it. The art direction, music, and overall sense of place create a world that’s as haunting as it is beautiful.

This is a game that respects your time, challenges your skill, and rewards your curiosity. If you enjoy action RPGs, deep combat systems, memorable boss design, or open-world exploration, Elden Ring remains one of the best experiences available. A must-play.

Posted: November 17
The most overrated game of all time.

Let me be clear: I don’t mind difficulty. In fact, I’ve loved challenging games since childhood. I enjoy learning from my mistakes, improving, dying, trying again, and beating bosses. I defeated 30–40 bosses in Elden Ring without giving up, raging, or complaining about the difficulty.

My issue is not difficulty, it’s everything else.

Story & Worldbuilding

Yes, the world of Elden Ring can be huge, rich, and deep. But a world that big shouldn’t be this vague. The “story” basically consists of a guy shouting at the beginning and a handful of cryptic NPCs who talk like they’re solving riddles. That’s simply not for me.

I want to engage with a story.
I want an actual script, a main quest that makes sense, something to pull me into the world.
I shouldn’t have to check item descriptions or chase random weird NPCs across a massive map just to understand the basics of the lore. (Lore of the world, not the story)

Some players enjoy that style and that’s completely fine, but I don’t.

Gameplay.....

This is where the game completely loses me.

Elden Ring still uses the same old formula from 2007: slow, clunky, roll–dodge–hit combat that becomes extremely repetitive after a while. After a masterpiece like Sekiro, I genuinely thought FromSoftware would push their combat systems forward. Instead, they went backward.

Sure, there are tons of builds and creative options. But at the end of the day, every boss fight ends up feeling like you're just a rolling Pokémon waiting for openings. It’s uninteresting and dull.

Visuals & Exploration

I’ll give credit where it’s due: visually and structurally, the world is stunning. I normally hate getting lost in video games, but Elden Ring’s worldbuilding makes exploration rewarding, every corner has something to discover, and that’s a huge positive.

But in the end, amazing exploration doesn’t mean much when:

1)The story gives you nothing to hold onto, and

2)The combat system feels boring and outdated.

Conclusion

I respect people who love this game, everyone has different tastes. But I genuinely cannot see the appeal. After 42 hours of boredom, I have to drop it midway.

A generous 5/10.

The game is also quite replayable with how many different weapons and possible builds you can make BUTTTTT the game is so long that I find myself kinda dreading having to play it again for different endings or trying out different weapons. The weapon thing can be especially annoying since upgrading weapons costs a limited finite resource and if you upgrade a weapon there's no way to unupgrade it if you decide you actually want to change your build. Can sometimes make you feel like you are "stuck" with a certain build.
Also there's a good chance you've probably heard people wanting a "difficulty slider" and while I don't want that, it should be noted that certain builds are absolutely just easier than others. If you want to play this game on easy mode, use a dex-bleed or full magic build. (Which again this can kinda suck if you accidentally build your character to a build that is harder BUT that you find more fun. Of course there is an argument to be made that you should just play what is more fun, but players will almost always use the path of least resistance one they've learned of it.. even if it's less fun!).

Anyway to wrap this up if you want some extra fun with this game mods are a thing, and multiplayer mods are especially fun. Though it should be noted this isn't "officially supported", so ya know, risk and all that.

Elden Ring is widely regarded as a masterpiece because it blends FromSoftware’s signature challenging combat with a vast, beautifully crafted open world that constantly rewards curiosity and exploration. The Lands Between feel alive with mystery, from haunting ruins and eerie forests to massive legacy dungeons filled with memorable bosses that test your skill without feeling unfair. The game gives you enormous freedom—if a fight is too tough, you can wander off, discover new areas, find better gear, or level up, making progression feel organic rather than forced. Its deep build variety lets you shape your playstyle however you like, whether you prefer brutal melee, powerful sorcery, or a hybrid approach, and the storytelling—subtle, cryptic, and atmospheric—invites you to piece together a rich lore at your own pace. Combined with stunning art direction, emotional highs from hard-won victories, and a world overflowing with secrets, Elden Ring offers an experience that feels both epic and personal, earning its reputation as one of the best games of its generation.

Posted: November 16
I beat Elden Ring, and I think this game is just exhausting.

I've played Dark Souls 1-3 and I think, comparatively, Elden Ring is just not a similar enough experience.
Boss design feels too fast for responding to, leaving you to just gamble on dodging correctly or leaning into just blocking most of the fight. The attacks aren't big OR fast, they're big AND fast.
Navigating detracts from combat. I ended up just racing around with my horse and getting all of the possible waypoints around the map, which seems to be par for the course, but it felt like a waste of my time. Same for walking into a level 40 area at the start, grinding there for a while, and then coming back to all of the bosses dying in 4 hits. Not to mention mounted combat, which has been interesting in concept, and frustrating in practice.
Speaking of which, the balance is suboptimal. As a Strength Barbarian, I was fascinated by the stun/knockdown system, but it didn't feel rewarding because you have to keep attacking for so long back-to-back. This incentivized just gambling on charged heavy attacks and being rewarded for the boss to by chance just use the worst attack on you. It felt gross. I bet other builds like Magic are a lot faster and feel better to play, but I definitely didn't play that build so that's moot to me.
So yeah, I can't really recommend it. I wish I could. The game just left me feeling pretty empty and regretful. I think FromSoftware made an okay game, I just don't think it can be justified to recommend others for spending the hours to beat it. If you do end up getting it though, maybe don't play a Strength build like I did.

I don’t enjoy open world games, so when I saw that Fromsoft was making “open world” dark souls, I feared the worst. My fears were not only met, but surpassed. I don’t foresee myself playing any new title from Fromsoft if they keep trying to take their aging combat system and continue to duct tape Devil May Cry bosses into it. My issues with this game are legion, but I’ll just list off the most poignant ones.

- Enemies can and WILL input read you, and retaliate with frame perfect precision.
- Enemies can and WILL be put around corners to deal cheap chip damage.
-Enemies can and WILL do combos that dole out multi hit flurries of attacks that take upwards of 5-8 seconds, that you absolutely CAN’T punish afterwards as the combo actually had another 2-3 hits if you stood near them.
- Enemies can and WILL use that “ONE” attack, again and again until you die.
- Quest progression is basically impossible to keep track of without a notepad handy as the game lacks a basic quest log.
- Progress feels like it resets at the mid-end game due to enemy attacks dealing exponentially more damage.
- Certain rewards in dungeons can be completely useless for your build (Thanks open world game design!).

This applies to the DLC as well, but cranked up to 20 as you’ll need to get your Scat-du blessing up to level 20 if you intend to have any fun with this DLC. Gotta love arbitrary difficulty tied directly to world exploration that gets hindered by said enemies blocking your exploration, but hey! Game design is hard.

Best way to play this game is to look up any “How to get OP fast” guide that just tells you where all the OP equipment is and just abuse the broken Ashes of War because, honestly, they’re better than most of the spells you get.

elden ring is probably the most complete and well executed game ive ever witnessed, it has probably more content in its first area of the game than some triple a games have entirely. ive played for 300 hours before i bought the dlc, and still havent finished everything in it yet, because it just has so many things you can do, and each and every one is interesting and rewarding, and somehow the amount of content is not overwhelming, as it gradually comes and introduces the missions within your progression. it is very polished, and has nearly no bugs whatsoever, at least not that i encountered in all my 300 hours. the music is not insane, but it is good and builds the atmosphere very well, on top of the already insane atmospheres it has. the world design, textures, and the design of every creature in this game is nothing but perfection. the architecture is also one of the greatest ive ever seen, with castles and cathedrals that truly are breathtaking. and not to mention that the map is one of the biggest and most diverse and content filled maps i ever played. it is also the most cinematic game ive ever seen, with so many beautiful views, and incredibly cinematic boss fights. on top of that, theres also over 300 weapons of so many classes like great swords, bows, colossal weapons, axes and just enough to give you freedom to play in your own style. you can also ELDEN RING - A Journey That Changed Me:

I've spent over 3,000 hours in ELDEN RING (1,100 on PC and 3,000 on Xbox) and somehow that number still doesn't capture what this world has meant to me. It feels strange to call it "just a game" when it has been a place I've lived in, grown inside escaped to, and returned to again and again. The Lands Between pulled me in slowly... and then refused to let me go - and I'm grateful for that.

When I first stepped into Limgrave, I didn't know that I was about to begin a journey that would become part of my life. But every river valley, every ruined chapel and whispering forest path offered something more then a challenge - it offered a feeling.
A feeling of curiosity.
A feeling of fear.
A feeling of wonder.
And over time, that wonder turned into a deep connection.

A world that feels like home
There's something indescribable about a world that you can wander for thousands of hours and still be surprised by. Even now, after all that time, I'll stop on a cliff in Altus Plateau and catch a view I've somehow never seen before. Or I'll notice a piece of environmental storytelling that suddenly makes everything click. ELDEN RING is alive in a way most open worlds only pretend to be.

It's a world that listens.
It's a world that remembers.
And somehow, in a strange way, it's a world that cares - not by coddling you, but by pushing you to grow.

The Combat: A Dance I'll Never Master (But I Love Trying To)

I've fought certain bosses so many times they feel like old friends - the kind who still beat you senseless but cheer for you when you finally stand tall again. Nothing in gaming compares to the feeling of learning a boss's rhythm, of finding your own way to face them, of finally landing that finishing blow after a dozen or a hundred attempts.
With each new build I try Whether it is Strength or Dex Intelligence maybe Faith or Arcane, or some chaotic blend - the game transforms. The combat is a conversation between you and the world, and after 3,000 hours, I'm still learning new words in its language.

A Story Told in Whispers

What amazes me is how FromSoftware trusts the player. The game never spoon-feeds you meaning, but gently nudges you toward discovery. Every item description, every ruined statue, every ghostly figure murmuring its final words adds another thread to a tapestry woven with tragedy, hope, ambition, and mystery.
Some games tell stories.
ELDEN RING invites you to uncover one.

Why I Can't Stop Playing:

People sometimes ask how I can spend 3,000 hours in one game. the truth is simple:
Because it doesn't stop giving.
There's always another build to test, another boss to rematch, another hidden area to explore, another theory to dig into. The game grows with you. It challenges you in different ways depending on who you are when you return.
some days i go in for the thrill of battle.
Other days i wander for the peace of exploration.
And sometimes... I step into the Lands Between simply because it feels comforting, familiar - like sitting by a warm fire after a long day.

A Masterpiece I'll Carry With Me:

After 3,000 hours, one would think i'd be done. But I'm not. Not even close. There is no "end" for me here - just another path to walk, another weapon to master, another reason to fall in love with the game all over again.

ELDEN RING isn't just a masterpiece.
It's a part of my life now - a place where I've grown, struggled, triumphed, and found joy in ways I never expected. And I'll keep playing it for years to come.

Please Please Please, let ELDEN RING take you on your own journey though the piece of art that is ELDEN RING. Good luck Fellow Tarnished.

Immersive, beautiful world. Ton of exploration, wonderfully bleak world and lore. Second time I'm beating the game, and it's still as enjoyable.
Also, even though I'm playing fully solo, not engaging in multiplayer, there's a lot of replay value, due to sheer variety of items, build archetypes, enemies, bosses, areas etc.
It's not as curated experience as say Bloodborne, or other souls games, but the open world has it's own advantages, in it that you can approach the game in multiple ways, and you can (to a degree) choose you difficulty, not only with more world exploration which can lead to a better leveled and equipped character, but also in terms of summoning signs, and ash summons, which for most boss fight, can make the experience a lot more new player friendly. Or you can forego those, and approach it solo. Freedom's the name here.

Posted: November 13
It's been quite a while since this launched and while the ship has sailed and everyone knows how good this game is; I still thought I'd leave my two cents from the perspective of someone who has enjoyed past Fromsoft games immensely.

First and foremost:
It's a successful experiment of open world. Fromsoft have delivered a vast rich world with epic battles and vistas. They have made it fun to explore with most side paths rewarding you for your curiosity. Time and time again I followed a secluded path and was rewarded, which encouraged me to explore more. The sheer size of the world makes for an epic adventure. I was only disappointed on a handful of occasions when boss was reused that I remembered fighting distinctly. Reusing Godrick (a great rune bearer) as Godefroy did not work for me.

Secondly:
Dungeons. I think I would have tired of the open world eventually. it's an issue alot of open world games face, every area has the depth of a puddle because the map is as wide as a ocean. Lucky Fromsoft played to their strengths and strategically placed dungeons and legacy dungeons to vary the experience and craft dense interconnected challenging levels. It changes the pace so well and so exceptionally. Elden ring could have released with no open world all together with simple paths between these legacy dungeons and would still be an excellent game.

Thirdly:
Mechanics. Darks souls combat is here and better than it's ever been, weapons are bombastic and exciting, powerstance duelwielding. With the added option of Ashes of war expanding on weapon arts gives customisation you will constantly be tinkering with trying to make weapons your own. And that's to say nothing of magic and miracles which are powerful beyond anything in previous games. The addition of jumping and designing maps around that mechanic is scary and complex but they pull it off. The legacy of Sekiro learned verticality plays so well and adds depth to exploration decisions and combat.

Pedantic notes:
Mounts 😈 I think if I had one desire it would be to remove Torrent and shave a third off the map size. I spent most of my playthough coop in the open world with a friend and so experienced most of the map without torrent, running very far in many open stretches of land with not much in it. In general I've not got much love for mounts as they feel like a design fix for worlds designed too large. I'd argue if you were to design Elden rings map without Torrent in mind, you could get more interesting experiences out of it. I would much rather see mobility in the form of magics, miracles, or equipment that teleports, transforms, or speeds up the player character.

The DLC:
Worse than the base game in all honesty. The map was far too large with really not enough in it. Many times I explored corners of the map fully only to not be rewarded or find anything of interest, really killing my curiosity (e.g. Cerulean fields of f*** all). There were also alot of strange parkour sections with torrent which felt like some of the worse paths in the game (the climb up to Bayle could have been so much more interesting on foot with a mountain legacy dungeon and I really didn't feel excited killing my 6th dragon in a row). Though the enemies are damn challenging, and it makes it worthwhile to think on your build, weapon, and who you're fighting to get an edge and the bosses are exceptional.
It's still fun and worth playing as everything else is spectacular, but a step backwards in open world design it feels like.

I enjoyed the game immensely (~230 hours for completed first playthough, though I did invade alot).
(☞ﾟヮﾟ)☞

Posted: November 11
Visuals: 10/10. Detailed and varied vistas, the kind of things that stick in your mind. Gritty, dark fantasy world with its own post apocalyptic beauty.

Story 5/10, you have to work to know what the heck is going on. Most of the story is hidden in obscure places, item descriptions, and notes to be pieced together, though that's not out of place for "souls" games. What story there is can be quite interesting, but you're better off having a YouTuber who studied this like it was their doctorate explain it to you.

Gameplay: 8/10, if you like this style of brutal, unforgiving combat where timing and knowing your weapon and foe is everything, you're in the right place. Sometimes you get hit when it really feels like you shouldn't have and some encounters are infuriating, but that's par for the course for this type of game. Many of the weapons and spells are very satisfying to use.

Sound: 9/10. Pretty thematic music, haunting but beautiful. Fits the world and scenery really well, though no particular tracks ever really stood out to me. General sound effects were what they needed to be.

Difficulty: it's a souls game. You're gonna have a steep learning curve, especially if you go in blind. With a detailed guide the game gets much less infuriating, and in some ways can even get too easy if you know how to min/max everything. In many ways the difficulty is all up to what a player wants it to be. Want it easy? There's ways to do that. Want it excruciatingly difficult? There's no end to challenge runs you can set up.

Play the Co-op mod! It's a ton of fun with a friend!

Posted: November 11
My first Souls game. Very highly recommended.

The positives:
- Exploration, story, and open world design is an absolute master class in "show don't tell". There are so many secrets to find. It's so rewarding just to check every corner of the map and leave no stone unturned.
- The combat and character customization rewards different play-styles and experimentation.
- The horse. I love that as soon as you whistle, it spawns immediately and you are already mounted. Riding around slashing enemies is fun.
- Feeling like a genetically hyper-optimized supremium alpha warrior after defeating bosses.
- I also really appreciate the small file size (for a modern AAA game) and the performance. It's very common nowadays for minimum specs to require a supercomputer, 160GB of space, and DLSS or FSR just to maybe hit 60fps.

My nitpicks:
- The lock-on can be frustrating and will often snap onto weak minor enemies instead of the extremely terrifying giant running towards you. It will also fail to lock on if an enemy is only slightly obscured by a wall or a tree or a column, and is charging up an attack that will invariably hit you through those trees, walls, and columns.
- The lock-on button is also the same as the recenter camera button, meaning attempted lock-ons while strafing will often snap the camera away from the enemy and leave you blind and open for damage.
- The camera also has a tough time capturing some of the massive bosses. So you'll be unable to see or react to attacks and telegraphs.
- The upgrading resources could use adjectives instead of numbers to help differentiate them. I didn't realize Smithing Stone [4] is not the same thing as 4 Smithing Stones. It would be better if they were instead categorized on a scale from "very weak" to "very strong".
- The music is also pretty forgettable. A few of the themes sound like they were thrown together with Kontakt in a day. Kind of baffling considering there are 7 composers credited.

Posted: November 9
A spectacular experience the first time, and a good one in subsequent playthroughs. Elden Ring is a masterful demonstration of From Software's formula crafted into the open world. Although, this game is no masterpiece, like any other game, it holds its own significant flaws. To call it a masterpiece detracts from its problems, giving its developers the illusion that they need not make improvements. It is regressive to consider any game perfect but I dare say Elden Ring approaches perfection. However, the game does suffer from major problems.

The multiplayer component is lackluster. The implementation of limiting gameplay with friends is a mistake. Ideally, cooperative gameplay should be seamless in the exploration of this beautiful world, without the loops and hurdles required to do so. And yes, there is a great mod for that but modders are not responsible for fixing your bad implementation (I'm looking at you Bethesda).

The weapon upgrade system has confusing dynamics. Often times upgrading base weapons with several smithing stones, in several tiers, is more challenging than upgrading unique weapons with somber smithing stones. Upgrading base weapons takes a total of 12 smithing stones per tier compared to the 1 somber smithing stone for unique weapons. Finding and grinding smithing stones can be its own difficulty, made even easier for unique weapons considering you only need 1. The system is backwards in my opinion and should be reversed. Simply put, the base weapons should be easier to upgrade than the unique ones.

The most gigantic issue I have with this games design is its restrained style story telling. Giving the player little instruction creates a magnificently quiet atmosphere, unlike any other game, but it's not without trade-offs. While the quite can be breathtaking, it leaves so much to be desired for quest lines. Most of the time you'll find yourself using external resources for quest progression, item locations, weapon locations, etc. While some of those features are fine, its disastrous for story telling. You are nigh-on forced to look up quest progression beyond blind luck which has the effect of utterly ripping you out the experience. You'll be sooner be checking off a guided list of instructions on a YouTube guide or Wiki page than naturally progression the story. The problem is compounded when you need to complete these vague quest lines for different ending. While solutions are something I cannot easily provide, From should look to compromising between the baby mode of Rockstar missions and the quiet restraint that From is known for.

Overall, disagree with my points as you like, but we need to be more critical of games. It's easily to clown on utter failures, but its far worse to relentlessly praise a great game. We cannot give From Software the idea that they are infallible. It's happened before, and now look at Bethesda's output. We should recognize and voice our issues without strictly praising a great game. Consequently, From Software will continue to improve and we will continue to play their great games. I don't want to continue in a market suffocated by underachievement and disappointment. A market that From Software has stood above, time and time again.

⭐ A Journey Worth Taking

If you're drawn to Souls games for their world-building, exploration, and sense of adventure, Elden Ring is a masterpiece. The Lands Between is vast, mysterious, and brimming with secrets. Every corner invites curiosity, and the thrill of discovery is unmatched.

The game doesn't just give you a map—it gives you a world. From haunting ruins to majestic castles, each location feels handcrafted and alive. The freedom to explore at your own pace makes even the smallest detour feel rewarding.

Combat? It's solid. Bosses are memorable, and classes offer variety. But if you're here only for the challenge of boss fights and tight combat loops, you might find the pacing a bit meandering. The game isn't a boss rush—it’s a pilgrimage.

And yes, the runbacks exist. But they’re rarely soul-crushing. Often, they’re scenic, filled with tension and beauty. You’re not just retrying a fight—you’re re-entering a world that wants to tell you something.

If you crave immersion, mystery, and the joy of getting lost, Elden Ring is everything you hoped for.

This was my first full introduction to souls-borne/souls-like games. And it was pretty good. I get why so many people like it. Can't really compare it to other titles, but I think this is probably the best introduction into the genre.

Combat
Combat is really good. You have so many options to choose and you can be basically everything you want. It can get frustrating from time to time, but sometimes it can also be forgiving. Sometimes some ordinary enemies can be much harder than some bosses.

I really like the freedom exploring the world. You can go basically everywhere whenever you want. So the game let's you explore and get powerful even before you fight your first boss. But sometimes it can be quite overwhelming and you can't decide where to go exactly and you can easily end up in the endgame area within first hours of your playthrough.

The story doesn't tell you much and story of the world is mainly told through hidden messages, item descriptions or mysterious NPC conversations. This style of storytelling is definitely not for everyone. Maybe I'm spoiled from big AAA RPGs, like The Witcher, Cyberpunk or so on, but even the ending wasn't that satisfying as I thought it would be. Maybe I'm not used to that formula yet.

It was fun, interesting and sometimes frustrating journey I enjoyed. Right now I want to dive into the DLC, so maybe my opinion will change. Also what helped get through this game is to look at it as big open-world RPG, not souls-borne or FromSoftware game.

In the 69 hours that it took me to beat Elden Ring, I was put through an absolute wringer of emotion: Anger as I was beaten down by its toughest challenges, exhilaration when I finally overcame them, and a fair amount of sorrow for the mountains of exp I lost along the way to some of the toughest boss encounters FromSoftware has ever conceived. But more than anything else I was in near-constant awe – from the many absolutely jaw-dropping vistas, the sheer scope of an absolutely enormous world, the frequently harrowing enemies, and the way in which Elden Ring nearly always rewarded my curiosity with either an interesting encounter, a valuable reward, or something even greater. FromSoftware takes the ball that The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild got rolling and runs with it, creating a fascinating and dense open world about freedom and exploration above all else, while also somehow managing to seamlessly weave a full-on Dark Souls game into the middle of it. It shouldn’t be a surprise to anyone that Elden Ring ended up as one of the most unforgettable gaming experiences I’ve ever had.

To set the stage, all you know from the outset is that you play as a “Tarnished” of no renown, blessed by grace, and are compelled to make the journey to The Lands Between and become an Elden Lord. What that actually means, how one might go about doing that, and what the deal is with that giant glowing golden tree are all things that you have to discover yourself. Like other FromSoft games, the grand story is hard to fully digest on a first playthrough, especially because there’s no in-game journal to refresh you on the events, characters, or unique terms you encounter across dozens of hours. There really should be, but it is a story I nonetheless enjoyed trying to piece together for myself. I look forward to supplementing that knowledge with the inevitable painstakingly detailed lore videos that emerge from the community later.

Unplayable. (I'll change review once it gets fixed)

The game has a glitch that freezes your PC, not the game, the entire PC. IDK how this happens, but has been happening since the launch and devs don't fix it. It's not a performance issue as all components are under 60% load when it freezes.

If you are getting freezes every 10-30 seconds in moments where it makes no sense, you have the bug. It can be "solved" every time it happens if you minimize the game in any way, like pressing Alt+Tab or Windows button. Sometimes it doesn't get fixed the first time you press it, so what i do to quickly fix it (not always works, sometimes it gets full freeze and you got to wait it for minutes) is mash the windows button until i see the game moving again.

If you have this issue and just bought the game, I recommend you return the game soon because it won't get fixed. 2 DLCs since the bug and still no solutions, so yeah, no fix on sight.

Just as a note, no other From Software games gave me this issue. Also no other game I've played in this PC gave me that kind of glitch.

Posted: November 7
It really is superb. The combat system is great, the customisation and control you have over your character is like nothing else I've played. The visual design is incredible and the world is so enjoyable to play in. The bosses look great and are mostly very fun to fight.

I deliberately went in as blind as possible. I knew everyone said this game was something else and I'd only played and completed Wukong as a similar game to this. The first 10-20 hours of me figuring out little bits at a time that improved my ability to play was so satisfying. I love the upgrade system and the flasks, the way the wondrous physick works is really good and can change your approach to a boss so quickly without loads of thought or time.

With me going in blind, I didn't really experiment with summons, incantations, spells and crafting. I wouldn't do this again and I'd recommend new players try to figure this out early and maybe even restart with a new character once you've got the hang of it. The game doesn't hold your hand and I think some of this stuff would be best looked up online rather than just trying to brute force your way through the game. That said, I'm looking forward to playing through again some time and taking much more advantage of this the next time round.



There are some issues with the game from my perspective. Some of the boss mechanics are extremely frustrating and it can take so long to get to that point of the fight after you die that it can be really hard to learn the tells or timing. Wukong had some bosses (not all) that you would just start on phase 2 if you got that far previously and I liked that. Long runs back to bosses weren't that common but that was pretty frustrating, also being subjected to cut scenes every time that you had to skip was frustrating. It would be good if you had a setting that could turn that off after the first attempt.

Fighting large bosses was quite often a pain from a camera perspective, and while not staying perma-locked on does help it's really a scale issue. You really need a second right thumb to do everything you need in a timely fashion.

I also found the critical hits inconsistent to land. Most of the time it's not a major issue but I didn't feel like I was the problem there.


I will 100% play through this again and I'm looking forward to focusing on the bits of the game that I realised much later I'd missed out, rather than going back and doing them overleveled. It was a great experience and I'd recommend it to anyone that hasn't played this type of game before, they just have to be willing to commit some time to getting over that early learning curve.

I had my doubts about this one. I'm a keyboard and mouse purist and not very into soulslikes.

Surprisingly the game isn't half bad without a controller, and even if youtube content creators will try to sell you the opposite, the original controls feel intuitive and fine as they are.

The world is gorgeous and even though I don't understand half of the lore, as a whole Elden Ring manages to create a very attractive gloomy atmosphere. The only thing is that it can feel just like that, gloomy, and empty. What the game does it does masterfully and even if you may get tired of fighting one boss or exploring one area you can just decide to head some other place, but in the end there's not much else on the menu than hacking and slashing.

A legendary open-world masterpiece.

Elden Ring isn’t simply a game — it’s an epic journey through a hauntingly beautiful world filled with danger, mystery, and unforgettable triumphs. FromSoftware delivers storytelling through atmosphere, exploration, and discovery, letting the world speak without needing words. Every ruined castle, every lonely field, every towering boss tells a story.

The combat is deep and thrilling — brutal at times, but endlessly rewarding. Whether you're wielding colossal weapons, casting ancient sorceries, or riding into battle on Torrent, every encounter feels meaningful and earned. Victory isn’t given — it’s forged through determination, learning, and courage.

The Lands Between is breathtaking in scale and detail. A world so immersive you can lose yourself for hours just exploring its secrets. No markers, no hand-holding — just pure adventure and freedom.

If you love games that challenge you, inspire you, and stay in your mind long after you stop playing, Elden Ring is a must-play. It raises the bar for open-world design and player agency.

10/10 — died a hundred times, loved every second.

Pros
* a lot of content (I haven't started a new game+ yet)
* there are optional summons and co-op in boss battles, so you can complete the game even if you're not very good at it (like I did)
* the world is beautiful

Cons
* without using the guides it will take inordinate amount of time to discover everything
* a lot of quests are easy to miss or to break by progressing main story too far
* it's hard and sometimes grindy

All-in-all, it's a long journey, so if you prefer something short it might not be to your liking, but it doesn't get boring most of the time.

Posted: November 2
I've played through this twice, once on Xbox, and then on Steam Deck/PC and both times I put well over 100 hours into it. It performed admirably on the Deck, although on a dedicated PC, it performs better. There are some technical issues with framerate and stuttering. However, gameplay wise, Elden Ring takes everything that other Souls games have done and does them almost universally better. Not everything is totally perfect, but it's so close that you can't even really see it as anything other than. It's difficult, sometimes frustrating and not for everyone. But it's a modern masterpiece. While I still want to see FromSoftware return to the Dark Souls style of world traversal one day, I couldn't fault them for doing open world games like this. They put several other companies to shame with this one.

OVERALL RATING: ◆◆◆◆◇
FINAL VERDICT: This game is great and I would say the price is not that high. Exploring locations, finding new items and enemies is good - but sometimes repetitive, due to reused bosses and enemies. The fights are great, but sometimes the timing of enemy attacks is annoying. The story is also nice and provides multiple endings. Using the SeamlessCoop mode, we can play with our friends without being disturbed by other players. I definitely recommend the game, especially for those who like a little challenge.

Elden Ring – A Modern Masterpiece

Elden Ring is FromSoftware at its absolute best — a stunning fusion of open-world freedom, rich lore, and brutal yet rewarding gameplay. The Lands Between is a vast, hauntingly beautiful world that encourages exploration and discovery without holding your hand.

Combat is deep and flexible, offering countless playstyles through weapons, sorceries, and Ashes of War. Every victory feels earned, every defeat a lesson. Visually and musically, it’s breathtaking — a world that feels ancient, alive, and endlessly mysterious.

Blending Hidetaka Miyazaki’s design genius with George R. R. Martin’s mythic storytelling, Elden Ring isn’t just a great game — it’s a landmark achievement in gaming history.

Verdict: 10/10 — A timeless masterpiece of challenge and wonder.

This game represents the peak of the Soul franchise, brimming with promise right from day one. Not just in gameplay, but in the art direction—it shows the full potential of what video game visuals can be.
Yet, the open world (or "open field") feels disappointingly sparse. The game focuses heavily on exploration, but the rewards are underwhelming. Roughly a 1/4 of the content, like dungeons and bosses, is reused, which actively tempts you to quit and forget the final reward.
However, the boss design is pure genius and the peak of action gaming. Defeats require focus and patience, yet the challenge is balanced and fun, often letting players discover clever ways to win. It is a 10/10 fun and memorable experience and the peak of modern gaming.

I picked up Elden Ring a while ago, and it completely blew me away.
The world is massive, beautifully crafted, and full of secrets that reward curiosity.
Combat is challenging but fair, giving a true sense of accomplishment with every victory.
The variety of weapons, spells, and builds lets you create a playstyle that feels completely your own.
Exploration is endlessly rewarding — every hidden path or dungeon can lead to epic loot or a tough boss fight.
The bosses are memorable and intimidating, and defeating them is genuinely thrilling.
NPCs and lore are woven into the world in a way that makes every encounter meaningful.
Multiplayer features like co-op and PvP add even more depth and replayability.
The game encourages experimentation and creativity, whether in combat, exploration, or character builds.
If you enjoy challenging, immersive worlds where every step feels earned, Elden Ring is an absolute must-play.

Elden Ring is not just a game; it is the pinnacle of human achievement in interactive art. Dark Souls 1, 2, and 3? Mere warm-ups, clumsy sketches compared to the towering genius of this masterpiece. Lies of P? A pathetic, clumsy echo, utterly incapable of reaching the soaring brilliance of FromSoftware’s vision.

What once might have been friction or tedium in lesser games is here transformed into a seamless, exhilarating flow, where every action and decision hums with brilliance. The world itself anticipates the player, shaping challenges and opportunities with audacious foresight and daring ambition.

Elden Ring doesn’t just surpass the old Souls games - it obliterates them with effortless, godlike mastery, a shining monument to what games can be.

This is the undisputed apex of human game design. Pack it up. Go home. Give up. Nothing else can even compete. Nothing else will ever approach this level of divine perfection.

Elden Ring just blew me away. The world’s huge, gorgeous, and everywhere you turn, there’s something new to stumble into. Every area feels alive, whether you’re creeping through shadowy caves or wandering into towering castles that make you stop and stare.

The combat? It’s brutal, sure, but it always feels fair. Boss fights are wild — you’ll probably curse a lot, but beating one actually feels like you accomplished something. The best part, though, is how you can explore any way you want. No hand-holding, no annoying arrows telling you where to go. Just real, honest adventure.

Yeah, sometimes it’s tough. But that challenge makes every discovery and victory mean more. The music, the world, the whole vibe — it just grabs you. Elden Ring isn’t just another game. It’s a trip you won’t forget.

Elden Ring is the rare open world that trusts you. Curiosity is the best stat here—wander off the road and you’ll stumble into dungeons, dragons, and secrets that reshape your build and playstyle. Combat is elastic and expressive: from spellblades and bleed monsters to chunky greatshield tanks, the build variety is wild without feeling gimmicky. The art direction swings from melancholy ruins to nightmare majesty, and it’s unforgettable.

Exploration and initiative are constantly rewarded—knowledge, not just numbers, is your power curve. It’s challenging, yes, but fair in that FromSoftware way that turns setbacks into lore and routing lessons. Simply put: the most generous, surprising, and cohesive take on Souls design to date.

Verdict: The best Souls game ever—vast, intoxicating, and endlessly replayable.

Truthfully, I'm not too sure what I could add that hasn't already been said.
Elden Ring is truly a gem of a product, and one that will keep bringing you make for hours upon hours.

I do think the game can be a bit large for many people. In many other open worlds, I wouldn't think this is a problem, but Elden Ring has so much in it's world. But not only that, since it still can be a bit cryptic with how you moving quest lines along or how to get to certain areas, it can absolutely feel daunting.

This is my second playthrough and I feel much better about this now then my first playthrough. Not only more comfortable with the gameplay and bosses, but being able to traverse the world better. While I didn't know where everything was, it was much easier being able to gauge where things were since I'd played this before.

With that said, it will only effect a small group of people. The rest, well, you have an unbelievably incredible product here. One that you can keep coming back to again-and-again for more. There truly isn't much left to say when talking about this game. Once you play it, you will be entranced and pulled into the world From Software has created once again.

Overall Score: 7/10
Story: 5/10 Story is probably decent, but it never caught my attention, and i half speed ran the start of the game to play with friends missing out of character development etc.
Gameplay: 9/10 Super solid and consistent gameplay, high difficulty, high challenge but also high rewards.
Graphics: 9/10 The environment, characters and just everything is pretty and looks great.
Solo VS Online: 5/10 Playing this game solo is fun, if you have the mimic tear to assist with boss fights. It's a shame like all souls games the coop portion is pretty terrible. You have to use consumables, stand in specific areas and can wait up to 5 minutes for the signs to spawn in. Just janky, if they can improve it to be similar to the Remnant games etc.
Achievements: 7/10 Complete the game and do all bosses, then collectables towards the end. Score drops a couple of times as you have to complete the game multiple times for different endings.

Horrendous game. Stylish and pretty with some of the most horrid mechanics of ANY game I've played. Every Single Enemy has AOE's that are either A. Unavoidable or last an arbitrary amount of time unable for you do deal damage to, Flying enemies unable to be hit unless you're using a specific weapon ( in which case this goes against EVERYTHING ) every other Souls game did where you should and could use EVERY weapon and it be viable. Not here. Want to use Fist weapons? you'll never land a hit on anything that flies at any point. Want to try do some roll playing and sneaking around to do some cheap critical hits? have fun during boss fights. Want to challenge yourself and use ONLY consumables? can't do that either since you can only hold so many of 1 item at a time. Buffs don't last over 60 seconds where fights with bosses and demi-bosses can last over 5x - 10x that amount of time. add in the AOE fiasco that every enemy can do and maneuver during their "I'm going to enrage so don't come near me for 20 seconds ) makes them feel underwhelming and useless. Many late game bosses have this issue not just the early game. The copying and pasting of bosses in the game can make some sense to a degree, but there are literal areas just filled with what you fought somewhere 1 on 1 and now you have to 1 v 6 them. it's lazy, uninteresting and no-one goes out of their way to fight them. This game is literally marathon simulator, run past everything go to the next area to get your upgrade materials then go back since leveling your actual stats outside of Vigor, Endurance and Mind have little on how much damage you deal. Oh you wanted to maybe change your build mid way through then change it back? well you have to play though an entire area first unlike in DS2 you can do it immediately at the start of the game. I've never heard anyone say they enjoy fighting enemies like the Ulcerated Tree Spirits, Fighting 2 or more Crystallid Warriors or Crucible Knights ( even though if you just blaze through the game with max upgraded weapons or as far as you can get them they're not horrible ). Bosses should be just that "Bosses" not just something you copy and paste like 15 other enemies in the game and just be like here you go, we ran out of ideas just stick something in it. As someone who's put 1000+ hours into DS1 and DS2 and a couple of hundred in DS3 back on Xbox 360 this game is exactly what every bad thing about all of those games was and put into this one. Poise means nothing and weight means everything. Heavy armor is worthless and serves no purpose but to slow you down. Got headbutted by a 3 foot tall plant? Literally wearing stone as armor enjoy being stun locked and losing 20% of your health since damage reduction is % based you can't tell me that 1.6% of 1500 damage has any difference than 3.2% of 1500 damage means you're going to live or die in a boss fight that's going to take 10 minutes anyway ( i'll do the math here for you, it's less than 50 overall damage that you'd receive ) ( every dragon in the game follows this principle ). People that have praised this game are deluded in game and world design. Beat the game and i'll never touch it again dropped it a year ago back in august and picked it up after some tweaks were made came back ran a train on everything then you get to the last 5 - 6 bosses you can encounter where it's a 10 - 15 minute kite festival trying to squeeze in a hit without getting punished for it. Save your money and your sanity. stay far away it. The best description i can find for this game is and pun intended a Shattered Masterpiece.

I didn't end up hooked on the PvP in this game the way I did with the various Dark Souls iterations, but the world, bosses, build variety, etc., still made for an outstanding single-player adventure. Don't get me wrong, I enjoyed the PvP for a while, but perhaps because of the larger playerbase and deluge of content created about the game, it seemed like flavor-of-the-week meta builds were all too common and dueling got stale more quickly. That may be less of an issue now that the player count has shrunk and the content mills have moved on to other fodder. Regardless, Elden Ring is an excellent entry in FromSoft's catalogue that retains their signature design pattern of punishing you until you learn and adapt. Or rather, it does if you go through the game solo, once you call in other players to help it can trivialize several of the fights. I think this is a fine compromise for people who may not have the patience/reflexes/time/whatever to grind through a tough fight on their own to enjoy the game, but I'd still recommend trying each fight by yourself at least once before summoning help.
