Its definitely an incredibly good game with a wide variety of gameplay and weaponry letting you play however you like and in whatever way suits you. The game is refined to the utmost and features incredible story and views.

Some advice: The game is challenging especially if you are new to the soulsborne genre. I recommend you beat the first two story bosses before even considering quiting. The game is a bit grindy, if you want it to be more managable, you need to fight some enemies to level up. That way the game feels more fair and more rewarding. It will punish you for taking the easy way.
Some more advice: Explore everything before moving on. If you see the boss early and feel like you haven't seen at least most of the area. Go Back! After that you that you can beat the boss and go finish any business remaining in that area and only then should you proceed. That is how the game is meant to be played and will make it easier and more fun. It takes some getting used to. But if you can do that you will find yourself enjoying the game more, it will feel more natural in progression and difficulty and you will find your style and weapons faster.
Also stories and quests do not progress themselves. You need to find and talk to NPC's, alot! keep talking to them until they repeat lines. Then go sit at a grace and come back and talk to them again. If they are gone or still repeat lines you can move on. The story is not revealed easily. You have to do questlines and you have to talk to NPC's. You DO have to listen to what they say as it will help you keep it going and find them again.

Final piece of advice, GOOGLE ♥♥♥♥. How to level up, or how to make a build. How to dodge this one attack you can't seem to dodge. WE HAVE ALL DONE IT! (Water foul dance). I want to preface this review by saying that I've played Dark Souls 1 and 3 which I thought were pretty good, and I also played Sekiro, which I enjoyed very much. I also enjoyed playing Elden Ring, at least for around the first 75% of the game. The problem is what came after.

Once you reach a certain point, level design, enemy design, and just game design more broadly starts to go downhill very quickly. Late-game regions (especially one in particular) start to just become enemy spam, throwing a bunch of things you've already seen together in a room, but with way over-tuned health and damage. Bosses become a mess of AOE, janky hitboxes, janky attack timings, and extremely long combos, making the game more about memorizing the input sequences to avoid each boss' attacks rather than actually applying the skills you've developed over the course of the game. It turns into pure trial-and-error, or into a lottery where you just hope you get enough favorable patterns in a row to eke out a win.

It's really disappointing, because if I could just review the early- and mid-game, I would absolutely recommend Elden Ring. The exploration is fantastic and the boss fights are generally tuned appropriately so that you can either fight them as you are, or go off and do some other stuff so you can come back later better equipped. But to get so close to the end only for the game to suddenly devolve into an un-fun mess that you have to spend at least another dozen hours to slog through is just a horrible experience, and not one I would wish on others. If you're the kind of person who is happy to play a game while it's fun and then drop it once it's not fun anymore, Elden Ring is a great game to choose. However, if you're like me and you want to play games through to completion, it's sadly really hard to recommend it.

It's a good souls game, but I still think its the most overrated one in the series of DS, Dark soul 1-3, and Sekiro. I'd honestly just put Elden Ring just above DS2. This is game is exactly why I wish Steam had and a neutral rating instead of just a thumbs up and thumbs down system.

There's allot of love that put into this game and there's no denying that. The gameplay, music, boss design, and world design is beautiful and they've put allot of effort into it. Judging Elden Ring as just a game and not the whole package makes it a great game, but what this fails to deliver is an actual story worth investing into. I've beaten this game three-times just to get all the achievements and despite all of that it still doesn't make any sense as to what is happening. I do this with every souls game because I enjoy that challenges that certain achievements bring, but what this game fails to do like all its previous installments is coming up with a decent coherent story.

Out of all the times I played this game not once does it ever click in terms what is happening. You can watch all the lore videos and read all the lore explanations on online forums, but they usually just go off into their own "theory's." These fan theory's can basically be summed up like this "Yes this happened because this random knife description said it happened so now I'm going to write my own fan-fictions as to why that happened and have people think its real canon." Here's a couple gripes I have with the game in terms of writing. Spoiler warning:



Look I get it, its fine to speculate on the actions of characters and attempting to fill the blanks in between of the events that happened in the Lands Between. But its been years of this rehashed formula in souls games where they don't tell the player anything and rather have them just find the most random of items and reading their description for more lore to dig into that rarely has anything substantial in terms of adding a puzzle piece to an already confusing story. And before anyone says anything, yes I even know why Miyazaki does it the way he does. For those that don't know Miyazaki would read books in different languages he wouldn't really fully understand, so when he got to areas in books where he couldn't properly grasp what was happening due to the language barrier he filled in the blanks with his own head canon. But after 10+ years of this formula constantly being used it just feel so lazy now. Sekiro is the only one where there was an actual story that you were able to follow, but that's because Miyazaki had little influence in the story and just chose to direct it.

The quest designs are just terrible, there was an instance in progressing through some side content I was sent of by this mage to go find another mage and kill them. Simple right? Go from point A to point B and smash this mage face in with my big hammer right? Nope, you go to the area where she tells you and there's a thing in sight that gives away the hideout. I decided to look it up and in order to find this mage. What transpired after wards was just dumb, I had to hit this random side of a clif to reveal a hidden hide out. There's not a single conversation, item, or even hint that someone could reasonably find that tells yous to hit this specific clif at a certain location. I mean props for realism in being a tricky mage hiding, but lets be real what kind of garbage quest design is that? This is just one of many instances of doing the most random things to progress quest lines. You'll need the wiki pulled up with all the information on quest to even progress them.

If I'm being real here, yes its a good game, why wouldn't I give it a thumbs up? But if steam had a neutral rating it'll fit more in line as to what I think about this game. Elden Ring is lacking in any real substance in terms of writing... oh wait George RR Martin helped write this game? Ahhhh no wonder this story is half-assed. The old man still can't finish his own series so he had give us the equivalent of season 8 of GoT with this for games.

Its a solid game, but I just wished they'd ditch this lazy writing approach.

Infuriating. Take one step into water and immediately die, all loot removed from inventory and returned to the environment. You don't gain XP; you have to hold on to XP and hope you don't die before you can spend it.

This game does not respect the player's time.

Slow movement, cliffy starting environment, NPC's mouth not moving during dialogue, mushrooms are unimaginatively named "mushrooms."

The physics are hardly such. The game feels like one of the first four Saints Row games. I was expecting something more like Mad Max or one of the Arkham games.

The default controller controls are not intuitive: the trigger and bumper functions should be swapped. This is not possible in the game settings, and Steam controller configuration was no help (I had to End Task out of Big Picture Mode twice because BPM was registering my commands without responding to them). I gave up and switched to my mouse and keyboard, and because there is no first-person option, this makes combat feel awkward. Camera sensitivity is not high enough at maximum (10). It needs to be about 12, and I generally prefer lower mouse sensitivity.

Only for masochists who don't value their time and don't mind repetition and constantly losing progress.

Rating: 5/5
Elden Ring is one of the most immersive and well-crafted games I’ve ever played. After spending over 1700 hours across console and PC, I can say it offers a rare kind of freedom—letting you explore, fight, and shape your own path without forcing anything.
The world is huge and full of detail. Every area feels different, and the game doesn’t hold your hand, which makes every discovery feel earned. The combat is challenging but fair, and there’s a lot of room to experiment with builds and playstyles.
I play solo, and that’s how I prefer it. The game respects that. Whether you’re exploring quietly or taking on tough bosses, it gives you space to play your way.
FromSoftware didn’t just make a great game—they built a world that feels alive, mysterious, and worth returning to. If you value depth, atmosphere, and a game that lets you take your time, Elden Ring is absolutely worth it.

Posted: October 22
Wow. After about 120 hours of playing and about 1000 hours of meta-gaming, I cleared the credits. This game is a masterpiece. For those undecided who are intrigued but fear its difficulty or its learning curve - don't. Those ideas are all notional. This game can be played easily or hard. That's it's greatest attribute. The artistry of the world is masterful and the music and sounds and vistas all make it an incredible dark fantasy experience. Play as a wizard or a warrior or anything in between. Knowledge is much more important than anything else; specifically: where the secrets are. I recommend putting potions on Y+left and Y+right and the physick on Y+down - this way you'll never scroll for your heals (down then x can then be for all the items like ashes, lantern, boluses &c.).

The game rewards builds and the stacking of boons, &c. It rewards attention and testing. There is so much build variety. The combat is not as divine as Sekiro but it is still a lot of fun and infinitely more varied. Parrying can be entirely avoided but it is also a tool that rewards mastery. The absence of a pause is stupid. and is the game's sole blemish. There is likely something around that corner waiting to ambush you. Lock on is optional and not locking on can be a great choice in many instances. Experiment and Enjoy!

к сожалению не затянуло, НО Elden Ring и DLC Shadow of the Erdtree — это просто восторг! Я честно не ожидал такого уровня, но игра снова удивила меня и подарила незабываемые эмоции. Мир огромный, будто живой — хочется исследовать каждую трещинку, искать секреты и выяснять историю древних королей и богов. DLC — отдельный шедевр: столько новых локаций, врагов, механик! Иногда казалось, будто я снова начал своё первое прохождение — настолько всё свежо и интересно.

А теперь главное. Рени — мой абсолютный фаворит среди всех игровых персонажей. Её сюжетная линия захватила меня с первых диалогов: в ней есть и тайна, и философия, и ощущение чего-то настоящего, большого. С Рени сюжет становится глубже — её выборы и мотивация реально заставляют переживать и думать о смысле всего происходящего. Именно благодаря ей история Elden Ring обретает душу, и мне лично хотелось бы оказаться её учеником и союзником в любой вселенной.

Для меня Elden Ring — это не просто игра, а настоящее приключение, а DLC и встреча с Рени только усилили это ощущение и сделали любимую героиню ещё ближе. Если вы цените глубокие истории, красивые миры и запоминающихся персонажей — Elden Ring и DLC вас точно не разочаруют!
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Unfortunately, it didn’t fully capture me, BUT Elden Ring and the Shadow of the Erdtree DLC are absolutely amazing! Honestly, I didn’t expect this level, but the game surprised me again and gave me unforgettable emotions. The world is huge, almost alive—you want to explore every corner, search for secrets, and uncover the history of ancient kings and gods. The DLC is a masterpiece on its own: so many new locations, enemies, and mechanics! Sometimes it felt like I was starting my very first playthrough again—everything felt so fresh and interesting.

And now, the main point. Ranni is my absolute favorite among all game characters. Her story grabbed me from the very first dialogues: there’s mystery, philosophy, and the feeling of something real and grand. With Ranni, the plot becomes deeper—her choices and motivations genuinely make you care and think about the meaning of everything going on. Thanks to her, Elden Ring’s story truly gets a soul, and personally, I’d love to be her student and ally in any universe.

For me, Elden Ring isn’t just a game, but a true adventure, and the DLC plus meeting Ranni only strengthened that feeling and made my favorite heroine even closer. If you appreciate deep stories, beautiful worlds, and memorable characters—Elden Ring and the DLC definitely won’t disappoint you!
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I simply lost interest in this game. Got fairly close to the end (I guess, as I burned the Tree), but the impulse to just open it again and try to play is just not there. I really dislike the mechanic that in order to defeat a boss you have to die 5-10 times before you learn their combos. Sometimes you manage to get an early kill, other times it can be 20 deaths before you kill a certain boss. And after you learn their moves it seems like a choreographed dancing session. Jump, jump, roll, hit, roll, bla, bla. It's just not my thing. The only good aspects are the overall feeling of the world. The visual art and music are nice, but the fact that the story really lacks (IMO) just hurts the overall feeling of the world. I mean the story does not lack, it's just a fever dream. Most lore becomes speculative - i.e. that guy probably is related to that other guy. Could that guy be the other guy? It's just confusing and the fact that it all comes in riddles through different NPC's dialogues really does not help.
There are other bad aspects. IMO the hit boxes are just absolute nonsense. There is no quest design - not that I want to be guided towards the 'objective', but give me at least some quest log so I can keep track of what's going on. No. It's all just based on your memory, that's all. For example, when I got to burn the tree I lost whatever quests I had in relation to a certain place that gets burned alongside with the tree. There's no warning that might happen, and no possibility to load. Some bosses feel quite hard (it all depends on the order you engage them), but as you level up and get back, some are just too easy.
I don't know why so many people like it, but as they do, that's probably my problem. It's just not my thing. I have it 100 h in it, I really wanted to finish it, but it just gets repetitive really fast for me as in most places there are incentives to not even engage the mobs, but just run straight to the boss. It can become a running and avoiding simulator.
Maybe it's not a bad game considering how many people love it, but I'm gonna say it's just not my thing. However, if you're a glutton for punishment it could be yours.

It's OK. Like Skyrim, this is the 2022's version of baby's first action RPG that's entirely infallible and perfect in every single way. In reality, this game's pretty mediocre, has a lot of annoying and objectively bad design choices, an overdone and derivative narrative, and a handful of good boss fights in a sea full of terrible fights. Still, quantity can sometimes be a quality of its own, and there's a lot of game in this one. Not as good as other action RPGs, but it's good enough if you want something simple and superficially difficult. Keep bashing your head until you memorize the rhythm and roll well, hey, it's a "hard" game after all, right, so you're a "hardcore gamer" now.
Randomizer mod is genuinely great fun. I recommend playing that if you get this game. Gives you a reason to actually explore since you wont just find useless crafting materials you'll never use in the farthest corners of the world.

My favourite game of all time without a doubt in my mind.

This is the only game I've shouted at and yet never been angry with it. The fighting is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ awesome, the boss fights are unbelievably fun, and the open world is beautiful.
In my opinion, the lore is kinda confusing, but the base story is nice and easy to follow.

One thing I'll warn you about is the DLC. I personally never had any problems with the DLC - the size of it was insane, the boss fights are somehow even better, and the new weapons are ridiculously fun.
But I've seen a lot of people complaining that it's 'empty', as in a lot of areas don't have a lot to do. If you think this might be a problem for you, I'd advise you look into it more so you aren't disappointed when you buy it, but for me it was absolutely peak.

It's infinitely re-playable if you want it to be - you can run different, more obscure builds, you can do challenge runs, and there are even some pretty awesome mods on Nexus that I always have tons of fun with.

Absolute peak, 10/10 game for me.

Posted: October 18
This is an open world fantasy game which is not dissimilar in some ways to Breath of the Wild-- but it's old school Nintendo hard. You will die, a lot. You will not have a mini map. You will not have a quest log or waypoints marked on your world map. This causes you to invest your attention in the game. You should take notes on interactions with npcs like in the old days (or google/chatGPT when stuck), memorize the map and find your shortcuts etc. Unlike other souls type games, for particularly tough battles you can summon a spirit helper or a mimic, which you find in the wild and will level up like upgrading equipment. The environment is incredibly immersive and just plain gorgeous. This is not a purchase you will regret, as long as you walk into the game with the right expectations of the gameplay.

It's a fantastic game, i will list some pro's and con's
Pros:
1. Great art: the game is beautiful the fedility is not much but art direction goes too good

2. Fun combat: the combat is so good with all builds and spell, you get insane replaybility

3. Lore: The backstory written by george martin goes hard,

4. Exploration: i will say this is the best part of game the map is big also alive

5. Accessible: the game is not artificialy difficult it's fun and game gives you enough tool to make you op and never be stuck on a boss

Minor Cons:
1. Performance on pc: the game has stutters due to eac and engine, which can only be fixed when you disable eac and use process lasso to fix most of stutters, also the game lacks any new tech like upscalers.

2. Bad quest design: first of all game lacks any journal to ser what quests you are even doing and some quests are too random that you need to pick up a guide

3. Minor wepon upgrade problem: the base wepon upgrade is way too tedious conpared to rare somber weapons.

Conclusion:
Overall the game is 9.5/10 and is only held back by some issue to get 10/10

And yes it can give you easy 100+hr if you want.

Wow. Just Wow. Elden Ring was my first experience with any souls game. and let me tell you, it was not the last haha. A world so rich, filled with so much lore, yet does not force the player into watching cutscenes, players are given the freedom to explore and approach the game difficulty however they like, and the story is pieced together by item descriptions and questlines. The graphics although not amazing are good, it is the art of the game that really stands out. As for the bosses, it's hard to not call some of the bosses perfect, you would understand if you fought the Elden Lord. This is undoubtedly one of the best games in the last 10 years, I would dare to call it the best of all time. It offers so much, once you finish the game, you are left with a desire for more, try a new weapon with a new build, fight some bosses you didn't fight, or maybe go for a challenge run. This game offers you SOO much. For people like me who have gone into NG+ this game is endless as there is always something new you can try or a way to make things more difficulty. If after reading all this I have not convinced you this is a good game and worth your money, then I don't know what else to say. Just play Elden Ring, you won't regret.

Posted: October 18
It's like Dark Souls just worst in my opinion, I don't like the direction it went towards for the following reasons:

- it's too easy but instead of creating some interesting bosses they just gave them some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ combos and placed the spawn point closer. Even the toughest boss battle only took 3 or 4 attempts to defeat.
- recycled bosses and dungeons, over and over again has burned me out, I've grown sick and tired slapping around the same ♥♥♥♥ again and again or going through the same ♥♥♥♥♥♥ dungeon again and again, wasting between 30 minutes to 1 hour and for what? a spell that I can't use? at least in Dark Souls when you went off the beaten path and found ♥♥♥♥ you didn't need it only took like 5 minutes, here I need to put time and sometimes even effort if it's the stupid one shot kill dungeons and for what? too much work, energy and especially TIME for too little reward or even enjoyment
- huge empty maps, if I wanted pretty vistas I'd play a walking simulator, it's pretty no doubt about it but I didn't come here for looking at pretty ♥♥♥♥, I came to kill stuff and for meaningful exploration
- crafting is useless imo, and I normally LOVE crafting in games but here? why should I bother going out of my way collecting rocks, plants and killing 10 wildlife for 10 arrows when i can get more of those by buying them with runes by simply killing a normal footsoldier? too much work for too little reward, there is no incentive to do it except at the start when the novelty of it hasn't died out yet

I really liked it at the start but I started getting burned out after 60+ hours and I tried to push through just with the idea of finishing it and getting it done but I don't even have the incentive to do that anymore, the game took the joy right out of it.

FromSoftware if you're listening I'd say stick with what you know and drop the open world in your games, or do better please. Your game managed to kill my joy and I haven't even managed to reach the end of it.

I know some people still like this game, a large amount and to that I will say, good for them. I'm glad people enjoy it but as someone who's played DS, DS2, DS3 and Sekiro I will be honest and state that the game disappointed me, it's not what I expected from a Souls game.

I guess it opened the door for more people with these design choices but it's just not for me, for anyone enjoying it though. I wish you all the best and hope you don't burn out like I did, honestly that is one of the worst feelings to receive from a game series you really enjoyed.

Elden Ring isn’t just a game... it’s an experience you survive, piece together, and eventually fall in love with.

The story? Absolutely wild. But here's the thing: it doesn't hand it to you. There's no long exposition dump, no glowing markers telling you where to go next. You want to know what's going on? You better explore, talk to every NPC, read item descriptions, and listen. Miss something? Too bad. This game rewards curiosity and absolutely punishes ignorance.

The world is massive and stunning, sometimes peaceful, sometimes terrifying. And the bosses… man, some of them gave me straight-up goosebumps just from the cutscenes. It's not just the challenge, it’s the atmosphere. Every fight feels important, like it has weight, history, meaning.

One of the absolute highlights for me was Ranni’s quest. Not only did I get to learn more about her, but it also led me to uncover who Blaidd truly was. That moment of realization? Honestly, it broke me a bit. Finishing her storyline felt like closing a chapter in a book I didn’t want to end.

The NPCs aren’t just filler! Their quests shape the world, the endings, and your understanding of what’s actually happening. You grow attached, even if the game never really promises a happy ending.

I platinumed it, and I’d do it all over again. Elden Ring doesn't beg you to like it. It makes you work for it... and in doing so, it earns your respect. Maybe even a little of your soul.

"A dogged contender… refuses to die."

And that’s you, Tarnished. Good luck!!! ^^

TL;DR: The Magnum Opus of Open-World Design (Warts and All)

Elden Ring is not just a game; it is a legendary digital pilgrimage that redefines the open-world RPG by demanding your respect and richly rewarding your persistence. It is a masterpiece of world-building fused with FromSoftware's signature, challenging combat. The sheer scope of The Lands Between, combined with unparalleled build customization and a sense of genuine discovery, makes this an essential purchase. It successfully transitions the traditionally linear 'Souls' experience into a non-linear, exploratory sandbox where every new horizon conceals a secret, not a rote checklist icon.

While the game is technically demanding both in terms of skill and, historically, on PC performance (micro-stutters and a 60 FPS cap are still realities), the value proposition is enormous. This title offers hundreds of hours of content, from complex boss fights to hidden optional dungeons. If you are prepared for a steep, but fair, difficulty curve and can tolerate the few remaining technical quirks, this is a clear and unequivocal recommendation.




The greatest strength of Elden Ring is its seamless, non-directive open world. Unlike modern, icon-heavy map designs, the Lands Between is a pure invitation to exploration. The game provides only vague clues, trusting the player to ride their spectral steed, Torrent, toward points of interest. This organic discovery is paramount. For example, stumbling upon a massive underground biome like the Siofra River well by chance, rather than by following a waypoint, is the true core gameplay loop. Exploration itself is the primary mechanism for progression, mitigating the traditional 'Souls-brick wall' frustration.


The combat retains the strategic stamina management and precision-dodging of previous titles, but the addition of is a game-changer. These abilities fundamentally shift the build landscape, allowing for dynamic, specialized playstyles. Using the 'Bloodhound's Step' Ash of War on a heavy, slow-hitting weapon, for instance, allows a traditionally tanky build to incorporate hyper-mobile evasiveness. The staggering variety of viable paths—from a pure Sorcerer to a status-effect Arcane bleed build—ensures that the difficulty curve is manageable because it can be entirely customized to your strengths.


It must be noted that the PC version, while patched and significantly improved since its launch, is not technically flawless. The game still suffers from occasional, intermittent which is tied to the loading of new assets and zones. Furthermore, a hard 60 FPS frame rate cap and a lack of native ultrawide support are historical FromSoftware technical quirks that persist. These issues are annoying, especially for those with high-end rigs, but they do not break the core experience. For most players, the game runs well, and it is "Verified" for the Steam Deck.













Elden Ring is a genre-defining title. It is the culmination of a decade of FromSoftware's design philosophy, marrying their legendary combat and mystique with an open world that genuinely respects the player's intelligence. While the PC port's technical baggage (stutters, 60 FPS limit) prevents it from being flawless, the core experience—the exploration, the combat, the overwhelming sense of discovering a shattered myth—is so powerful that these technical issues become footnotes. The Lands Between will claim hundreds of hours of your life, and you will thank it for every single one.

Elden Ring – A Brutal, Beautiful Open-World Tarnished Souls-like experience

Elden Ring is everything I love about FromSoftware games turned up to 11 — and then dropped into a massive, breathtaking open world that begs to be explored. It’s challenging, atmospheric, mysterious, and so ridiculously big that every time I thought I’d seen it all, I’d stumble into another gigantic dungeon or secret boss hiding in a swamp.

The combat is tight and satisfying, with endless ways to build your character and fight. And the bosses? Oh, they’re glorious nightmares. From Malenia, the infamous blade who’ll ruin your ego in seconds, to Radahn, a literal meteor-riding demigod, every major fight is a mix of frustration, adrenaline, and absolute joy when you finally win.

One of my favorite additions is the Spectral Steed (aka the elk-horse Torrent) — it’s not just a mount, it’s a partner in exploration, making the vast landscapes feel alive and letting you dive into combat on horseback like a true fantasy hero.

And if you want to take the experience even further, the Seamless Co-op mod is a game-changer. Exploring the Lands Between with a friend from start to finish — without the usual summoning limitations — turns Elden Ring into one of the best co-op adventures out there.

From Frustration to Fascination – My Journey Through the Lands Between

I bought Elden Ring hoping for a new kind of experience. It was my first Soulsborne game, and from the very first moment — that haunting opening, the music, the atmosphere — I knew this journey would be something grand and epic.

But honestly, it wasn’t easy. I deleted the game four times out of pure frustration — the difficulty, the lack of clear objectives, the enemies that felt impossible. It tested every ounce of my patience and made me question if I was even capable of finishing it.

On my fifth attempt, I told myself: “I’ve paid for this game, and I have to complete it — at least once.” So I pushed through. I kept going even when I hated it. But as time went on, something inside me changed. The same game that once made me angry started to feel magical. I began to see the beauty in the struggle — the breathtaking landscapes, the haunting music, the deep lore, and the stories of fallen heroes scattered across the Lands Between.

What once felt like punishment turned into pure admiration. The moment I started understanding the world, the gameplay, and the meaning behind it all, I realized — Elden Ring isn’t just difficult for the sake of being difficult. It’s teaching you patience, curiosity, and respect for the journey itself.

By the time I finished it, I didn’t just complete a game — I completed an experience that stayed with me.

Elden Ring is not just a game; it’s an epic, a masterpiece, something that stands on its own. And I genuinely believe every gamer needs to experience it at least once in their lifetime.

Because only after playing Elden Ring do you truly understand what it means when a game challenges not just your skill, but your soul.

Posted: October 16
Elden Ring is good. Very very good.

Worthy of Game of the Year? Absolutely. Even if you have some dated gripes of "oh, DS1 is better! DS1 is the best game ever made!" (yeah, d*ck ride it more) you cannot deny that Elden Ring brought the world building, brought the visuals, and brought the game mechanics. I can't appreciate the Mona Lisa. Who is she? Some girl? But Nokstella? THAT is art. Seriously though, the graphics in this game are superb. I would rather wake up and see the erdtree outside than go out into our world nowadays.

It seems like this game contains all of the good features of the Dark Souls trilogy, plus its own identity. The jumping alone. You would think a small movement like a little hop wouldn't make much difference, but boy it does. As a Dark Souls veteran, I can say that it took me longer than I'd like to admit to adjust to the new combat system. And even now, if I play Elden Ring for a playthrough, then get into a Souls game, there is a large adjustment period.

Some negative points. As a DS2 apologist, I loved upgrading shields increasing the defenses. Why was that not a repeated feature? I also miss improving armor. You die in one or two hits no matter what you do in Elden Ring, pretty much. Especially once you get to the late game. Then there's new game plus. I understand it's a much larger game, but one of my favorite features in Dark Souls was the massive differences between NG and NG+. New enemies, new items, pretty much entirely new areas. I wish they could have done something like that. What did we get instead? The bosses are "harder." Yeah, okay. If you complete a full playthrough, you'll have no trouble until AT LEAST haligtree.

Some closing thoughts. Elden Ring: great game. Flaws? Sure. Is it a 10/10? I mean, it's damn near close, but I wouldn't say it's perfect. Of course that is subjective. But me, I'd give it a 9.9.

Oh, and George R.R. Martin helped (-0.1 for his weird hat).

Posted: October 15
Bosses have too long combos, too fast tracking, and too many unnaturally delayed attacks. At least for melee builds.

I believe boss fights are the single most important aspect in the FromSoft formula, so it brings the rest of the game down when the crescendo of an area is more fun than the climax. It pushes players to rely on defensive and "cheap" tactics like cheese, summons, or overlevelling. I like that the latter two are still viable options, but they shouldn't be the only ones outside of spending 10 hours memorizing tedious and unintuitive attack patterns for one boss. If I want fast paced combat (ie not ds1) then I much preferred learning the movesets of bosses from games like DS3 and Sekiro, where aggression is the key to winning and there is less visual trickery to "gotcha!" the player. Admittedly DS3 has many of these same issues, just not cranked up to 11 like in ER.

It comes off like FromSoft making each successive game faster paced and with less margin for error in order to keep up with returning players' ever-increasing skills, and adding "easy mode" options for more accessibility to new players. But as someone who likes to beat challenging yet balanced bosses while solo and at a reasonable level, neither option is a fun way to play.

The rest of the game is fine, although after playing every Soulsborne game besides King's Field, I think I prefer when the maps are more linear and curated than a vast open world. The points of interest themselves like dungeons and forts are good at least. It's also great that you can go grab more variety of weapons right at the start of the game. But ironically I get tired of exploring the open maps more quickly than I do exploring the linear ones. It's the difference between scouring a carefully designed and fleshed-out highlight reel versus aimlessly wandering the wilderness looking for something to do. The whole mounted combat thing and crafting using foraged reagents are really cool but not quite enough to justify the map design and size I think.

The atmosphere and lore are also inferior to DeS/DS/Sekiro/Bloodborne. It's not a dealbreaker for me, but I found it disappointing and less inspired. It's just more of the same recycled ideas from the Dark Souls trilogy. Sekiro and Bloodborne impressed me with the range FromSoft has with lore, settings, and levels of technology, so I know they can do better.

In terms of visuals the game is beautiful. Probably the best visuals out of all their games so far. Nothing more to say, other than mentioning that it sometimes causes frame loss. Thankfully this lag seems to (usually) vanish when entering boss arenas where it would be the most disruptive. But it's all hardware dependent so take that with a grain of salt.

I will probably come back to this game later because I'm a FromSoft addict, but after putting it down I have since struggled to find the desire to return when all their other games exist.

The Last Guardian of the Golden Dynasty
I am the veiled monarch, forever forbidden from revealing my true face—last of the kings bound to the Golden Order.
I am Morgott, the cursed child of golden blood. Laughable, is it not?
Born of Queen Marika the Eternal and the first Elden Lord, Godfrey, I was meant to be flawless—like my elder brother, Godwyn the Golden.
But fate was far crueler. Twisted horns, a monstrous hide—these heralded my birth.
An omen child. An accursed being, unfit for the Golden Order.
They say, in the age of the Crucible, bodies like mine were marks of divine blessing.
But what use is that now, in this age of the Golden Order?
I am but a blemish—born to be reviled and forgotten.
By royal blood, my horns were spared the blade. Yet even so, my mother, Queen Marika, cast me and my equally cursed twin brother, Mohg, into exile.
We were imprisoned deep beneath the royal capital, in the sunless, squalid Subterranean Shunning Grounds.
As if such burial could erase the shame we cast upon the golden lineage.
Endless dark, fetid waters, and eternal solitude—such is the rightful place for omens like us.
Where there is light, there must be shadow.
Though I was deemed impure and exiled, I understood.
The sacred order is paramount. I accepted the judgment of the Order—and of my mother—without protest.
It was my penance. My path.
To protect the Golden Order, I would endure any darkness, bear any pain, and even forsake my very being.
Yet just as I resigned myself to die beneath the sewers, a great explosion shook the royal capital above.
The crack of crossbow bolts and the cries of soldiers rang through the stone.
War had come. But with my father, Elden Lord Godfrey, standing guard, who dared strike at the throne?
The war dragged on. Then—something stirred within me.
A fragment of the Elden Ring fused with my flesh, bestowing the golden grace I had long been denied.
I trembled. This could only mean a terrible upheaval had befallen the Order.
Chaos flooded my ears, and I could sit idle no longer.
I broke my chains and climbed toward the surface.
To hide my omen form, I veiled myself in cloth.
When I emerged… I could not believe my eyes.
The Golden Order had collapsed.
Godwyn the Golden was slain under the law of immortality.
The Elden Ring—shattered.
My parents… gone.
Order had crumbled.
My kin—General Radahn, and the twin prodigies, Malenia and Miquella—they dared form a monarchs’ alliance to assail the capital.
Had they ever truly revered the Golden Order?
Did they ever bear the slightest burden of duty to this land?
Traitors. All of them.
Their rebellion was blasphemy.
Worse still, the new guardian of the capital—my descendant, Godrick the Golden—fled in disgrace.
Crawling on all fours, dressed as a woman, he abandoned his post, leaving loyal soldiers to die.
Great Erdtree… was this your will?
Look at the scions you chose.
They tear at their own homeland.
They insult their blood.
They trample upon the sacred order.
Unforgivable.
If a new dynasty were to rise from this ruin, then someone must stand in defense of the old.
Even if that someone is an outcast omen, cloaked in shadow.
I took the name Margit, the Fell Omen, and stood against the traitors at the gates.
Time and again, I repelled them. I severed their ambitions.
But the allied forces were vast. The royal capital was besieged day and night.
To end this pointless war, my brother Mohg and I enacted the Bloodflame Ritual.
While I battled at the front, Mohg scattered cursed blood among the rebels, driving them mad.
They turned upon one another in senseless slaughter.
Then, insurgents infiltrated the roots of the Haligtree and abducted Miquella.
Malenia, broken by the loss of her brother, withdrew from the alliance and returned to the tree.
The monarchs’ pact fractured under the weight of the ritual.
And so it came to pass…
That I, the cursed child—rejected by all—became the last monarch of the Golden Order.
I never sought glory. I merely wished to preserve all that was once beloved under the Erdtree.
Though we won the first battle, the traitors rallied anew.
A second alliance rose, descending upon the capital once more.
I again became Margit, the Fell Omen, and led the defense.
I carved rivers of blood through the enemy ranks.
Countless warriors loyal to the Golden Tree perished. Their corpses rose like mountains.
At last, I struck down Starscourge Radahn within the city walls.
Only then did the attackers retreat.
Yet the Shattering War knew no end.
My kin, returned to their homelands, waged war for the Great Runes, perpetuating their sacrilege.
Mohg, too, vanished after the Bloodflame Ritual.
And I… I remained alone, the last shadow cast by the Order.
Watching over a silent capital.
Year after year, I beheld this once-glorious land descend into strife.
The last king?
Who remembers my loyalty?
Who speaks of my sacrifice?
How many times did I long to push open the door behind the throne—
To see what became of the Elden Ring?
But the roots of the Erdtree barred the way, tangled in thorns that rejected all.
None could glimpse the truth.
O Elden Ring… is this my punishment?
Even as your final guardian, will you never accept my cursed flesh?
Then I shall wait.
Until one deemed worthy appears.
And one day, the Tarnished returned to the Lands Between.
They challenged the demigods, seeking the Great Runes—
To become Elden Lord.
Is this your new chosen one, O Elden Ring?
If the grace in their eyes is of your making, then I shall face them without fear.
I dispatched my loyal knights, who became Night’s Cavalry to hunt them in the dark.
I sent forth two avatars of myself, under the name Margit, to stand vigil before Stormveil and the capital gates.
If they could not overcome these trials, they were naught but fools, puppets of ambition.
At last, one came before me.
“What is thy business with these thrones?
Godrick the Golden, the twin prodigies—Miquella and Malenia,
General Radahn, Praetor Rykard, Lunar Princess Ranni…
Willful traitors all. The lot are of a piece.
Pillagers emboldened by the flame of ambition.
Etch upon thy pitiful epitaph the name of I, Morgott…
Last of all kings.”
I felt it—his strength.
The Great Runes answered to him.
My kin had fallen to his hand.
He was different.
And so, I was defeated—before the Erdtree, before the Elden Ring.
The gold left me. The omen’s strength faded.
I became but a brittle, broken mortal.
Mother…
Golden One…
Tell me—
This cursed life of mine…
What was it all for?
Did you ever once…
acknowledge me?
“Tarnished… thou art blinded by folly.
The Erdtree denieth all.
We are forsaken.
None may become Elden Lord…
Not even I.”

Elden Ring – Pain, Beauty, and Pure Freedom

Elden Ring is not just a game — it’s an experience. It’s that rare mix of frustration, awe, and pure discovery that few games ever manage to deliver.

You start as a nobody, lost in a brutal world that doesn’t care about you — and that’s exactly what makes it so powerful. Every victory feels earned. Every boss you finally beat feels like your story, not just a scripted event.

The world is breathtaking — hauntingly beautiful, full of secrets and mystery. You’ll ride across vast golden fields, dive into dark catacombs, stumble upon dragons, and accidentally fall off cliffs more times than you’ll admit. Exploration feels rewarding in a way most open worlds don’t. Nothing is handed to you — you find it.

The combat? It’s FromSoftware at its best. Brutal, precise, and incredibly satisfying once you learn its rhythm. You die, you learn, you adapt — and when it finally clicks, you feel unstoppable.

Sure, it’s hard. Sometimes unfairly so. But that’s the beauty of it: Elden Ring respects your patience and curiosity. It never holds your hand, yet it always rewards you for pushing forward.

If you love challenge, atmosphere, and the thrill of discovery, this is a masterpiece.

Rating: 10/10 — A world that breaks you, then makes you feel like a god.

I came to Elden Ring as a newcomer, excited by the hype and the promise of an open-world Souls game. But after many hours, I found myself more drained than inspired. The exploration that was supposed to feel boundless was full of copy-paste dungeons, identical bosses, and endless catacombs that make discovery feel like a chore instead of a thrill. What should’ve been a sense of wonder slowly became a checklist, as the same caves and enemy layouts reappeared again and again. The game’s scale, while visually stunning, is bloated to the point where the magic wears off long before the journey ends.

What frustrated me most, though, was how little the game seemed to care about explaining itself. The story and quests feel nonsensical, hidden behind cryptic dialogue and obscure item descriptions that make even basic understanding impossible without constant trips to the Internet. I wanted to play the game, not read wikis just to know what was happening. Still, for all its flaws, I’m thankful Elden Ring introduced me to the Souls series. Dark Souls and Dark Souls II both gripped me completely- smaller, richer, and far more rewarding experiences that remind me what great world-building and design can feel like.

Man, I used to not give a damn about this game. Fr, I thought I’d never enjoy some pain-fest like this. Like bruh, why would I play a game that looks straight-up torture? Watching people get smoked by bosses they can’t even touch... ain’t no way that’s fun, right? You gotta be some kinda masochist for that.

Then one of my friends started playing it, and I watched him go up against one of those crazy-ass bosses. Lowkey got me hyped watching that, not gonna lie. He kept saying, “Bro, you gotta try this game, trust me.” I still didn’t care that much tho…

One day I was bored af, and I remembered another friend of mine actually owned the game. So I borrowed it through Steam Family Share and gave it a shot. At first I wasn’t even serious. I was like, “aight, lemme just see what’s so fun about this pain simulator, pretty sure I ain’t gonna last long in this one.”

And bruh… the moment I started playin’? I fell deep. Straight addicted. That feeling when you die like 50 times and finally clap the boss? Dangggg, that hit different. This game wild af, bro. Never thought I’d be into this kinda pain, but now look at me now, finally bought the game and the DLC, lol.

Crazy how a game about dying over and over made me actually enjoy failing. Elden Ring lowkey built my character more than my architecture degree ever did lol.

Shout-out to my g who kept hyping me up, you were right, this game’s straight-up cinema. And to my other g who let me borrow it, I owe you big time. Y’all really got me hooked on this, fr.

Now I get it... Elden Ring doesn’t just test your skills, it humbles your soul.

10/10... would die again xD.

English version (french below) :

Elden Ring didn’t make all that noise for nothing. It’s not perfect, but it’s an excellent game.
There are tons of weapon types and builds, and there are far more tools than in the Dark Souls games to help newcomers enjoy the game. That said, it doesn’t make it easy, finishing the game is still challenging, no matter what build you use. This is especially true for the DLC, which is excellent but particularly tough.

Be aware : this is not Dark Souls. Sure, the gameplay is similar, but these are very different games with very different design philosophies. Don’t expect Dark Souls 4, or you might be disappointed.

For completionists, 100% isn’t really difficult, but as with most From Software games, some quests are missable. There’s also a legendary weapon you can miss in the city of Leyndell, so be careful.

Other than that, go ahead and enjoy the game! :-)


French version :

Elden Ring n'a pas fait autant de bruit pour rien. Il n'est pas parfait mais c'est un excellent jeu.
Il y a énormément de types d'armes et de builds différents, il y a beaucoup plus d'outils que dans les Dark Souls pour faciliter la vie des novices et pouvoir profiter du jeu. Attention, ça ne deviendra pas simple pour autant, finir le jeu n'est pas facile, peu importe ce que vous utiliserez comme build. C'est encore plus le cas pour le DLC, qui est excellent, mais particulièrement dur.

Attention, ce n'est pas Dark Souls. Certes le gameplay est similaire, mais ce ne sont pas du tout les mêmes jeux ou même le même type de design, donc ne vous attendez pas à un Dark Souls 4 ou vous risqueriez d'être déçus.

Pour les complétistes, le 100% n'est vraiment pas compliqué, mais attention, comme à l'habitude des From Software, certaines quêtes sont manquables (il y a aussi une arme légendaire que vous pouvez louper dans la grande cité dorée, donc faites attention)

Sinon, allez-y et profitez du jeu :-)

Least favorite boss? Gravity.

Posted: October 12
I've beaten the game, but I mostly did not find it enjoyable.

Combat is repetitive in the extreme. Every encounter, from trash mobs to bosses, is played in the same way:

Roll
Roll
Poke
Roll
Roll
Poke
Repeat

or

Block
Counter
Block
Counter
Crit
Repeat

...and so on. (Fill in whatever combat loop your character is built around.)

This might be endurable if it was a tenth as long as it is, but this is a prime example of quantity over quality. Doing this for 200+ hours is not fun.

Speaking of quantity over quality, this is one of the worst executions of an open world I've seen. There is simply no point in the open world parts of the game. The content you encounter is weak and repetitive. The mini dungeons you find are a dime a dozen. Boring, cookie cutter caves, mostly. Everything is designed to maximize a pointless grind that is utterly hostile to your time.

The legacy dungeons are the only part of the game I enjoyed, but they are so diluted by the open world content as to not be worth it, overall. If they cut out the open world and just had the legacy dungeons, the game would be an endurable length, and drastically higher quality.

On a positive note, some of the visual designs of the bosses is incredible. Truly remarkable.

Ultimately, the game is a tedious slog, overstaying its welcome, and overburdened with extreme repetitiveness and an abundance of, frankly, bad content.

On the bright side, people will give me their steam points with jester awards, so go nuts.

After 370 hours in Elden Ring, I can confidently say this is one of the most rewarding, frustrating, awe-inspiring, and occasionally infuriating experiences I’ve ever had in gaming.

FromSoftware took the essence of Dark Souls and dropped it into an open world that actually feels alive — not because of bustling towns or endless NPC chatter, but because every hill, ruin, and eerie tree seems to have a story behind it. The world design is immaculate. You’ll constantly find yourself saying, “Wait, what’s that over there?” and then three hours later, you’re deep in a cave fighting something that shouldn’t exist.

Combat is as tight and deliberate as ever. Every weapon feels distinct, every boss a puzzle. The difficulty curve can be brutal, but fair — most of the time. Some late-game areas (looking at you, Haligtree) cross the line between challenging and exhausting. Still, the sense of achievement when you finally overcome those fights is unmatched.

Visually, the game is stunning. The art direction carries everything, even if the technical side occasionally stumbles (stutters, pop-ins, etc.). The soundtrack deserves its own praise — haunting, epic, and perfectly timed.

Criticisms? Sure. The open world, for all its brilliance, loses a bit of momentum in the second half. Enemy variety dips, and a few recycled bosses show up too often. The NPC questlines remain as obscure as ever — sometimes charmingly mysterious, sometimes just badly explained. And inventory management is still peak FromSoftware chaos.

But despite those flaws, Elden Ring is a masterpiece. It rewards curiosity, patience, and persistence like few other games. Every hour I spent — from the first miserable death in Limgrave to the final triumphant swing against the Elden Beast — felt meaningful.

If you’re willing to be humbled, amazed, and occasionally broken, this game will give you an experience you’ll never forget.

10/10 – Would die to the Tree Sentinel again. 🌳⚔️

I've absorbed so much discourse on this game and the fromsoft catalogue writ large that I can hardly even put together a review that doesn't sound repeated, but I see my currently 946 hours on record and understand that I can't exactly carry on without putting down something more concrete than a number, eh?

To see some of the most marvellous, breathtaking, golden sights of the Lands Between, is why I played this game, and to see legendary names felled by the tarnished's hands kept me in.

My one issue, which I will always maintain as the weaker element of the fromsoftware game library, is the storytelling, which is once again lacking. However, this time, the worldbuilding and "story" is so enchanting that I can forgive the weak storytelling a bit more than usual. This team is full of masters when it comes to world design (on both small and grand scales), engaging combat, and adding a brilliant amount of flavour in its fantastical weaponry and fantastical enemies. THIS is fantasy, dark fantasy maybe, but brimming with magic and grit in healthy spades each, and tempered by a wonderfully intriguing world. Playing too much of fromsoftware's games makes you desensitised to things like gore and death in games, but in the right amount, it's perfectly impactful.

Its praises have been sung from the rafters already, but really, this is one worth experiencing, provided you can stomach some of the hardest gameplay you'll find in the action adventure genre (but if you're accustomed already to soulslikes, then you'll slip into it nicely).

Solidly meh, recommended With Friends TM because everything is Tolerable With Friends. It's quite literally open world dark souls, except that in the 15 years since Demon Souls we still move like it's DS1 but the enemies have gotten the 15 years of game developer improvements in crazy movements, animations, and cool ♥♥♥♥ instead. The end result is typical of From Software - a mixture of annoyingly unfair if doable fights, to some decently fun and plenty doable fights, but leaning even more heavily on the former.

The only thing that scales with you is damage and health, so even at a very high soul level you'll still be stunned like crazy against the weakest mobs and still feel like paper against bosses. It's less mechanically diverse and interesting than DS1, even if there are some flashier moves and graphics (lol barely)- poise is effectively nonexistent as you need to specifically build for it to even remotely resist some attacks.

Oh, and you can't upgrade armor (which is FAR more useless than any other Souls-like From Software game) - which again, only contributes to the feeling of missing mechanical depth.

But I guess you can play together with a co-op mod, which is quite literally the saving grace of the game.

If you don't care for that or have no friends, just play Dark Souls 1 instead for a more fair and interesting game with more coherent lore than whatever the nonsense ER is as a whole.

6/10, nice set pieces, some decent encounters, mechanically disappointing and uninventive. if you've played any Dark Souls game, you've played Elden Ring.

☢️‹‹ Gameplay ›› [10/10]
The gameplay is a masterpiece in every sense of the word. It has a large number of weapons, poisons, potions, armors, character customization, dialogues, characters, side stages, magic, bosses, etc., each of which is different and has its own characteristics. You have complete freedom to choose your own path, your own character and your own power. A Souls-like RPG that is the most complete work of Master Miyazaki. In a word, a memorable masterpiece.

☢️‹‹ Graphics ›› [10/10]
The graphics of this game are a masterpiece. Everything from the design of the environment and the characters, the clothes and weapons, everything is designed in the most beautiful way possible and has a lot of detail. The sound and light of the different environments are wonderful and sometimes blinding.

☢️‹‹ Sound & Music ›› [10/10]
Unique music that makes your hair stand on end. Played in the best possible way. In a way that many will never forget. During the boss fights, it shows the atmosphere in the most epic way possible. A masterpiece.

☢️‹‹ Story & Narrative ›› [10/10]
It has a complex and heavy but memorable story. One of the best storytelling you can hear in your life is this story of the lands between. Provided you understand and relate. A masterpiece.

☢️‹‹ World & Atmosphere ›› [10/10]
It has one of the most beautiful and memorable atmospheres, environments, and maps. Every corner of this world is full of mystery and detail. It is one of the few games that is not boring to explore at all. A masterpiece..

☢️‹‹ AI & Challenge ›› [10/10]
This game is a heavy and seemingly difficult challenge that requires a lot of patience, learning, and effort. This may be difficult at first, but after a while it becomes the most enjoyable thing possible. I would also like to say about the NPCs in this game, they are the most accurate and intelligent. You will rarely see any mistakes from them. Of course, many of them can make you cry.

☢️‹‹ Performance & Technical ›› [10/10]
Everything works as well as possible and I haven't seen any bugs in this game. Everything is smooth and without problems. Of course, provided you have a relatively good system or better.

☢️If you haven't played this game, you're definitely missing out. Because this game is more than just a game and will definitely be one of the best games of your life.

🎮 Elden Ring Review (English)

Pros:

Huge world filled with secrets. Every time you explore, there’s always something exciting waiting.

Absolute freedom — you can die anywhere you want (and trust me, you will ).

Beautiful map and breathtaking environments.

Boss designs look like pure art.

Epic soundtrack that fits the world perfectly (though not as memorable as previous Souls titles).

Deep and flexible combat system — both melee and magic are fun, and PVP is more varied than ever. I actually love the PVP in this game.

Cons:

The difficulty doesn’t forgive newcomers. Without the internet or guides, even veterans from other games might get confused. It’s a double-edged sword — sure, it filters players, but it also drives some away before they even taste the real flavor of the game. It’s like biting bread but never reaching the delicious filling — not because they gave up, but because they didn’t know what was waiting inside.

UI and menu system still feel stiff, especially when switching items mid-fight.

The story is cryptic and not newbie-friendly (but if you dive into the lore, you’ll fall in love).

The servers are absolute garbage. I can play every other online game smoothly, but this one? Either someone lags at me, or I lag at them. Fix your damn servers.

Please nerf OP stuff in PVP more often, seriously.

Elden Ring really left me torn. The hype is absolutely justified, this is a great game. Variety of Weapons, Drips and playstyles, visual aestethics and the combat itself are top notch. It cleary is the best souls in some regards. Yet there are more aspect that leave me unsatisfied compared to other souls entries. I am not a fan of the open world. It looks mesmerizing, FromSoft really nailed the aestethic over photorealism principle (again), but i dont find the open world itself that interesting. I explored because i didnt want to miss anything, but it didnt feel intriguing and was rarely rewarded. It ended up being a chore, just like the horrible and repetitive dungeons. This lead to me quitting my first palythrough. BUT i am very happy that i ended up finishing the game later on. The boss encounters in the last third were definitely worth it and the most enjoyable part of the game by far. For my taste, they just took a little bit too long, throwing 3 phases at you and finding its climax in the final encounter Radagon/Elden Beast (that simply felt unnecessary). In the end I had to come to the conclusion that i enjoy the linear entries of the soulsgenre more, as i enjoyed the more linear sections of Elden Ring.

For what this game offers, it's great. But, if you are looking for a character or story driven game, this is not it. This game is pure exploration, adventure and combat where, even when story does happen, it sounds mostly like the npcs speaking don't want you talking or even being around them.

Pros:
- Incredible exploration. You have the freedom from the moment you step into the over world to do just about anything you please. From wide opens plains to dusty dark crypts and more. You are sure to find an area you particularly like.

- A wide variety of weapons to change how you would like to approach combat. Though actually acquiring said weapons may not be obvious. Along with a wide variety of different weapons you can customize most weapons to make them more unique. From its damage typing to if it inflicts some sort of status ailment.

- Pleasing visuals and environments. You could spend hours just wandering around not doing anything in particular. In fact, the pleasing visuals that encourage you to explore around reward you with weapons, armor and items to make you stronger.

- Variable difficulty. You might hear that this game is difficult and wonder why it shows up in the pros, I can assure you it is only as difficult as you make it. I explored and used summons liberally and found the game challenging at certain points but, aside from a single boss (no spoilers), had no trouble at all progressing through the game.

(and these could be deal breakers)
- The controls and the camera are your enemy. The conscious choice to combine dash and dodge on the same button was, in my opinion, about the dumbest thing they could have done. Because of this choice the dodge button is the only button that activates on release instead of press. This may not sound like a big deal but you will likely spend the first couple of hours getting used to it. (and dying a lot) The camera will get you killed from time to time. Its wild swings as it redirects around corners will run you off ledges or into enemies.

- The almost complete lack of story or character development. There is the absolute minimum of story present in this game. You are told shortly after the game begins, though a totally optional message on the ground, that you apparently want to be Elden Lord, whatever that is. There is no plot, no character development and no real explanations for why you are doing what you are doing. The "story" in this game seems to be happening off screen somewhere. I got the sense there was intrigue afoot, just not anywhere near my character.

- The combat gameplay. It's... adequate. My biggest gripe, and why it ends up on the cons list, is the games decision to "lock" you into actions very far in advance of when they may actually happen. To give an example: A far too often occurrence is trying to do an action, like attack, and having it get interrupted and you hit a button you want to do next but it doesn't go off. Your character will stand there or maybe it gets knocked down and when you finally get control back, perhaps 3 seconds later, your character executes the action the game locked in 3 seconds ago when you got hit. This action is very likely terrible in the new situation you are in, like jumping or dodging when nothing is happening or launching an attack into nothing.

- A concerning lack of direction. The pro about its incredible exploration opportunities finds itself on the con list as well. There is very little direction on what you be doing at any given point in the game. The only real direction you are given is a pointer on what is "required" to beat the game. If you, like me, as a new player follow these directions as what you "should" be doing you might likely find the boss fights to be VERY difficult.

-Armor. There is a wide variety of armor but it is mostly cosmetic. Hardly a con to be sure but worth mentioning nonetheless. I wore a dress for most of the game with a talisman that increased my damage taken and noticed no real difference when I popped on the heaviest armor available. I still died to the same things just maybe one hit later and that was from basically no armor to the most possible.


Don't get me wrong. From the rather extensive cons list you might think I dislike this game but I do recommend it. Once you get past the controls fighting against you, and avoiding what the game is "suggesting" you do, the game is a quite enjoyable time sink that feels rewarding when you finally beat that boss you have a dozen or more tries on.

I think the base game is a solid 8/10. It's quite good but far from perfect. With a few adjustments to the controls and a flushing out of an actual story, this could easily be a 10/10.

Great game ONLY IF A SOULS LIKE GAME is what you're looking for. I am not a fan of games that have such a high learning curve and this game can be extremely punishing if you're not used to a SOULS LIKE game. I have a LOVE & HATE relationship with this game. I think it's a decent offering of a SOULS like game if that's your jam. I WOULDN'T CONSIDER THIS THE BEST GAME IN THE WORLD. I have played video games since the ATARI 2600, INTELLIVISION, and COLECO VISION eras. I share this review with great passion and truth, and genuine honesty.

I CAN NAME 10 BETTER ONES THAN THIS ONE. As I said UNLESS you LIKE SOULS LIKE GAMES this will probably frustrate you to great heights. When I play games I want a nice enjoyable experience, not risk a coronary and be constantly stressed out. If that's your thing then go for it.

THIS HONESTLY ISN'T MY KIND OF GAME, although I respect it for being a hugely significant offering to the SOULS LIKE family of games but I am going to uninstall this as I hardly play this and being a fantasy gamer running many fantasy TTRPGS such as Dungeons & Dragons 1e,4e,5E and Pathfinder 1st and 2nd Edition, and ELDEN RING checks that DARK FANTASY genre.

But it's not my thing and I can't recommend this UNLESS you are into those types of demanding precision type games. I don't expect games to be completely easy but this learning curve and often difficult mechanically difficult combat elements.

Due to limited HD space and friends the fact that I don't play it hardly as often and I need my space for games I actually play often. So I literally bought this game TWICE, The PRE-ORDER price for XBOX one (which to this date I have hardly ever played. To me it's boring me and not entertaining me like other games do. I bought it a second time at full price to play with my friends. We don't play it often enough to justify keeping it in my library.

For those who enjoy these types of games go ahead and enjoy it. This isn't really a game for me. As much as I appreciate it for what it tries to be a very IMPRESSIVE SOULS LIKE GAME. If you don't like souls games chances are like me you won't like it as much.

The other thing I ABSOLUTELY HATE IS GAMES THAT FORCE YOU TO PLAY PVP with the stupid INVASIONS. A lot of the higher tier players tend to grief players and I play games to have fun, not to be stressed out. So hard pass. It absolutely sucks when you're trying to progress and every five mins you have this way OP player trying to pick on the VERY WEAK, it's just BS and toxicity in a community that has no place.

Sorry I will pass on that feature alone. So if players want that nonsense then they should have the choice, NOT FORCE PLAYERS TO PLAY THAT WAY. Secondly they also should have INVADING PLAYERS ONLY be able to INVADE if the player levels of who they're invading to be relatively within a few levels. It's so UNBALANCED it is SO FRUSTRATING not in my GAME!

If the DEVS are smart they should and be patching a feature or an option to turn invasions and have the invading players only be matched with players being invaded if their levels are within 3 of each other.

No one sadly seems to do anything in the Game Industry that actually makes sense. YOU GIVE PLAYERS WHAT THEY WANT, period! Especially if they're paying 80$ for your game.

Anyway, know that if you buy this game you will get an amazing SOULS LIKE experience. But if you're not used to or like these kinds of games than you will spend most of the game painstakingly repeating many different areas and losing valuable souls to upgrade your character's stats.

So sorry to see you go ELDEN RING, but the games I play are WAY BETTER THAN YOU.. I PRAISE you for being one of the very good SOULS LIKE GAMES, but you ARE NOT THE BEST GAME.

Elden Ring & Shadow of the Erdtree Review: A Masterpiece That Redefines Soulslikes
Elden Ring didn’t just win Game of the Year in 2022 for nothing—it’s a triumph that blends FromSoftware’s brutal legacy with a breathtaking open world, and in 2025, it’s still the gold standard for action RPGs. Paired with the Shadow of the Erdtree DLC, it’s a sprawling, haunting journey that’s as punishing as it is rewarding. Your note hits it perfectly: it’s a damn good game, a touch easier than past Souls titles, but no less epic.
The base game’s Lands Between is a visual and mechanical marvel—galloping across Limgrave on Torrent, uncovering secrets in Caelid’s scarlet rot swamps, or storming Leyndell feels like living in a mythic painting. The combat’s tight, with build variety (katanas, sorceries, colossal hammers) letting you carve your own path through bosses like Malenia or Radahn. It’s more approachable than Dark Souls or Bloodborne—checkpoints are generous, and open-world exploration lets you grind levels to ease the pain—but it still demands skill and patience. The freedom to roam, experiment, and stumble into nightmares (looking at you, fingercreepers) makes every hour unforgettable.
Shadow of the Erdtree cranks it up, dropping you into the Land of Shadow with a denser, creepier map packed with lore about Miquella and Messmer. The DLC’s bosses, like Bayle the Dread or the final gauntlet, are brutal yet fair, though some (Midra, anyone?) spike the difficulty past the base game’s peaks. New weapons, like the euphoric Bloodfiend’s Arm or deflecting shields, add fresh playstyles, and Scadutree Fragments balance the grind for casuals without gutting the challenge. X posts still buzz with clips of players outsmarting Messmer or debating the ending’s implications, proving the community’s hooked.
Flaws? The PC port still stutters on some rigs, and late-game balance in the DLC can feel overtuned for non-veterans. But these are nitpicks in a package this colossal. Solo or with co-op buddies, Elden Ring and its DLC deliver a world worth losing yourself in. It’s not just good—it’s the kind of game you’ll still be raving about years later.
Rating: 9.5/10

Do you want a somber, revelatory experience exploring a strange, fantastic world while getting a close look at its most inscrutable and often horrific facets? Do you want a setting that seems to blend science fiction, fantasy, and folklore? Do you want to pick up a huge sword, or a shield, or a freaky ritualistic dagger, or a slab of an ancient civilization mounted on a stick turned into a bludgeon and stride into battle with it?

Do you want to unravel the mystery of a lost culture, and see firsthand how its beliefs and values appear to have led it to ruin? Do you want to see the dignity of life as it persists in the most dire and improbable of circumstances? Do you want to make contact with aliens? Do you want to fight that persistent stuff or those aliens with a gigantic hammer, or like a ceremonial crozier that you stole off a dead priest or a scholar or something? But like the priest or whatever that was dead actually got up and beat you up for stealing it? And then you fought a big dude who was like this world's Atilla at some point in history, but now he's like an angry skeleton that can't let the fight go?

It's that kinda game. And just wonderful, harrowing scenery and a wonderful flouncy horse-like guy often will be around as well. Recommended to anyone. The game can be as easy or difficult as you like; depending on how you interpret how you're supposed to meet the challenges in front of you, as well as your working knowledge of the game. There's a highly dedicated community for this game that both loves helping and dunking on newcomers, so there's also comraderie of all stripes to be sought here as well. How do you spell comraderie?

It's a 100% recommended at full price. It's easily in my top 3 games of all time as a full package even if I have some small complaints. I'm shocked I haven't gotten around to playing it until now, but sometimes life is full of more pressing concerns and picking up a 100 hour+ game seems like a big commitment.

Positives:

The world is amazing, every backdrops is a piece of art, exploring is a joy and I probably spent 80% of my first playthrough simply exploring.

The remembrance bosses (look at these as main story, or lore significant bosses) all feel unique and interesting.

The lore and story is S-tier, even the smallest interaction you have with NPCs is meaningful for the overarching narrative in true From Software fashion.

The attention to detail that From Software are known for shines through better than ever before.

Negatives:

The catacombs dungeon layout for the smaller Legacy Dungeons is over used to the point where I could only sigh when I stepped into one for the 10th time.

Some smaller bosses appear a significant amount of times, making them feel less meaningful as the game goes on, Erdtree Avatars, Wyverns and Night's Cavalry are some good examples of this. But they are also optional encounters so you can chose to just run past most of them and not interact with them. Also, because of the large size of the game, it makes sense that some things would be used multiple times.


This may just be me having played From Software games and souls-likes endlessly for nearly 15 years at this point, but I found a lot of the game to be extremely easy compared to my first playthrough of previous From Software titles, I never felt like I would have to put in the time to fully learn a fight even while going blind with no summons. The only real roadblock I ran into was the Twin Valiant Gargoyles because of how difficult the poison breath was to see in the water. This continued into the DLC too with the Twin Gargoyles 12 attempts being by far the highest I encountered out of any boss while clearing out the entire game. I do however think that someone who is new to this sort of game will find a serious challenge with a lot of the major bosses, so I will chalk this down to an experience thing and nothing else.


It's an excellent game, and I had to dig deep to find any negatives, but I had to as I don't care to write a review unless I feel it's a fair one, and a fair review needs to contain both some positives and negatives.

This is the first time in years where I've felt that I can confidently recommend a game at full price, usually I don't really feel that way, but I had a great time from start to finish and From Software never disappoints.

Buy it, play it, enjoy it.

I struggled to form an opinion about Elden Ring for a long time, being in the camp of those who fervently hate it. It has paradoxically slow combat, intrusive asset reuse, broken PvP (not technically, in this regard, on the contrary, but in the invasion system itself), problems with PvE balance... and... I could go on and on.

Now, I can say the game is different, and for a long time, I was suffering from the duckling syndrome. No, the problems haven't gone away, they're still quite glaring, but... the game is just different.

Elden Ring is no longer Souls Sike, but something vaguely similar, simply an RPG with an emphasis on familiar combat. You wander the world, explore it, admire the beauty, and overcome challenges without much trouble, and if you encounter them, you move to another road.

And here the DLC stands out. I mean, I like DLC, I like challenging fights where you have to memorize a moveset over several hundred tries (I beat the pre-patch Radan without a meta build. 200+ tries. It's completely broken, and if you did the same, respect to you :) ). But... it's DLC ABSURDLY difficult. Who was it designed for, if the average Elden Ring player has no interest in struggling that much?... Maybe I'm missing something.

Souls games have been needing a breath of fresh air for a long time, and this is probably it, since people love it so much. I hope that in the future games they will fix all the bugs and what was broken here, but for now... we have what we have.

I was one of those people that had heard how impossible Fromsoftware games were, but had always wanted to play them. Elden Ring was the one I wanted to play the most, but having spent 30 minutes just trying to survive the opening moments of Bloodborne, I was doubtful that I would ever play Elden Ring.
I resigned myself to a life of envy, watching videos of near impossible boss fights being narrowly won by players of much higher gaming skill.
Alas, I wanted to know for myself. I asked friends and family, if anyone had a copy of the game for PS4 or PS5 or Gameboy Color, I didn't care. I wanted to try it, but didn't wanna waste big money on a game that I can't play properly. One evening though, I saw Elden Ring Nightreign on offer, so on a whim, I bought it. I never opened it and then decided to return it after hearing that it's nothing on the original. I was disappointed, until I realised that the amount of money I had spent was more than Elden Ring cost for Steam. I bought it!

Now, I'm 42 hours in and I've beaten a few bosses and I've become a menace to the lower classes. There isn't a mid level enemy who can walk the main roads that hasn't had their skull caved in by my mighty axe! I love this game so much and I implore anyone who is lacking confidence in their ability to play this game and succeed, to just try it! There is so much to do and so much to see. The first two days, I was killed many a time by a stronger opponent, because I wasn't ready to face them. After killing a few mid level opponents and learning some skills, I was able to kill all those stronger opponents and now I'm getting there, more and more every hour that I play.

The main thing is this: If you can't beat them now, you will eventually. You can run/ride away and pick on someone less difficult, learn from those fights to excel in an opposite direction. I just beat a boss first time, before writing this and then I got killed by someone who had no business kicking my ass!

Frustratingly difficult at times, but even more addictive and incredibly fun. If Bloodborne is a 10/10 difficulty, this is 8/10 at the worst. Also, invest a lot of runes into Mind & Intelligence, then thank me later!
