Overall:
Great art—as in true art, not that "forcing you to upgrade your computer" type of visual trash.
Great amount of content. This game is like 3 Dark Souls 3s in size, if you count all the side quests and dungeon locations.
Good storytelling. The epic background story is solid, but the endgame feels kinda rushed.
Great gameplay mechanics and combat design. By adding more freedom to customizing Ashes of War and letting you jump in combat, this game has more variety than the Dark Souls series.
Great level design. If you want that Dark Souls 1 or 2 vibe, this game got tons of dungeons—some of ’em’ll make DS2’s Gutter look like a piece of cake. Highly recommend.
Reasonable hardware requirements and minimal bugs. In my many playthroughs, I never ran into any seriously game-breaking bugs that stalled my progress. Just some wonky hitboxes here and there, nothing major—no bad lag or random FPS drops.
My only critique:
It might not be ideal for people trying to get into Souls-like games, ’cause there are some broken OP crutches out there, lol.

Elden Ring is an absolute masterpiece — a breathtaking blend of open-world exploration, challenging combat, and deep lore that only FromSoftware could deliver. It’s a game that rewards curiosity, patience, and courage, turning every step into an adventure.

- Pros:

- Massive, beautifully designed open world. The Lands Between is full of secrets, hidden bosses, and unforgettable locations.
- Deep and satisfying combat system. Every weapon, spell, and build feels unique, giving you endless ways to play.
- Sense of discovery. There’s no hand-holding — exploration feels organic and truly rewarding.
- Incredible boss design. Each encounter feels epic, from massive dragons to mysterious demigods.
- Gorgeous art direction and atmospheric soundtrack.
- Strong performance improvements since launch and great optimization today.

– Cons:

- The difficulty can be brutal for newcomers — it’s not a game for everyone.
- Storytelling is cryptic and indirect, which may confuse players unfamiliar with FromSoftware’s style.
- Some areas can feel slightly repetitive or overwhelming due to the world’s sheer size.

Final Thoughts:
Elden Ring is not just a game — it’s an experience. It captures the spirit of adventure, mystery, and triumph like few others ever have. Whether you’re a veteran of Souls games or a newcomer ready to take on a challenge, Elden Ring will leave you in awe.

10/10 — a modern classic and one of the greatest games ever made.

Lemme get something out of the way, I am not critisizing the games difficulty. Thats fine, this review is on more pressing matters on why I dislike Elden Ring

Elden Ring is just boring to me, as someone from DMC 5, Monster Hunter, and Warframe, it just is boring lack luster.

I did indeed "Git gud" and it still is umipressive with the weapons. The Straight Swords despite taking up 95% of the weapons roster are so copy and paste and boring. The Katana are somehow boring (Moonviel is a Diet Yamato, I will just play DMC 5 for that).

Most ranged weapons are incapable of doing anything of note. Smithscript weapons have insane harsh damage falloff for no reason, same with bows, crossbows, and most other weapons of a ranged nature. Bringing the question of why they are in the game to begin with if they arent viable options on their own

And magic relies on Mana but instead of an actually interesting way to regain Mana/FP it is just "chug a mana potion"... cool... Warframe lets me regen mana on its own, DMC 5 rewards me for being aggressive by giving Devil Trigger for a higher combo. Elden has no such excuse for a boring, out dated, flawed system.

Melee is boring too, despite having so many options. It felt more like a shallow puddle than any real depth. The Straight Swords are a waste of diskspace as 90% of them do the same thing. Many Weapon types are redundant. And the affinity system is useless and redundant too, as there is only like 1 or 2 good affinities per weapon, you cant get goofy with a "Heavy Great Knife".

All in all, the game is boring and unintuitive with its controls. "Getting Good" was the worse mistake I could've made when playing the game as it isnt fun, or diverse in its playstyles

Masterpiece. Everything from the lore, OST, art direction, gameplay and even graphics is just flawless. The DLC is also honestly a masterpiece. The difficulty can be really frustrating, as is to be expected from a FromSoft game, but the spirit mechanics plus the possibility to do side-dungeons until you are more prepared sort of enable an "easier mode".
It is a great souls game because it can be played by any kind of player, whether casual or experienced, and they are assured to enjoy the experience.
The only personal thing I did not like was that the open-world can feel quite repetitive, because it really offers nothing more than dungeons and some NPC-areas with smaller side-quests. This is to be expected in a FS game, but this game was my first experience with FromSoft games and I got really annoyed by this.
9.2/10

While I understand it's a very good game and really polished, I just cannot recommend it. Overhyped as hell, this game fails to deliver a nice atmosphere, stuff all the previous SoulsBorne games have achieved with great success. To be fair, I only skipped DS2. I dislike riding this horse/bull whatever it is, I dislike the open world formula in this kind of games, I dislike having ambience music everywhere I go and I also dislike how much emphasis this games puts into multiplayer, as well as "hints" like summoning spirits or these white ghosts of NPCs you've defeated before. Believe it or not, there is some sense of accomplishment in running around, alone in an uncharted place while pressing LB to avoid an unexpected foe attack and the only sound you hear is the wind and your footsteps; stuff which games like DS1, BB and even DS3 managed to achieve quite well. Oh and don't get me started on the tutorials. I really enjoyed figuring out the mechanics on my own and I HATE how much this game treats you like an idiot. Also despite some specific moments, I feel this game is way too easy. Honestly: I don't know what the hell Miyazaki was thinking; perhaps they wanted to appeal to a more general public, but in that way I feel this game blew it up.

It's a very polished souls-like. The Legacy dungeons and castles are very fun to explore through. The addition of poise breaking to combat makes aggressive play very enjoyable. Spells as a main source of damage has never been as fun as melee however here its a lot more flashy and less stiff. The stiffness was the worst part so a full caster play-through ain't really a headache anymore. Is nice. Most spells that aren't magic missle-esque or melee tend to be mediocre though.

The open world is good but its also very easy to over-level. Just fill your inventory with random trinkets from every merchant to circumvent that. It's size is also very time consuming. My feelings on that fluctuate too often. It's odd.

Very good game but stronger enemies can get very annoying with how slow you are compared to them.

Posted: October 4
No spoilers.

I don't know why I held off so long on Elden Ring. I loved Witcher 3 and Skyrim, but when I watched the initial trailers and initial gameplay I somehow thought this game wouldn't be something I like. But this is hands down the single best game ever. Out of all genres. It is the best game ever. Why? Well Ima tell ya. Everything I wish Witcher 3 would've borrowed from Skyrim and wish Skyrim would borrow from Witcher 3... is in Elden Ring. Elden Ring is an evolution and right now it's as close to perfect as a game can get.

ZERO Bugs. I'm 831 hours into it at the time of this review. Zero bugs experienced. I don't need to say anything else about that... do I Todd Howard?

Deep detailed lore. If the story is important to you, just like Skyrim and Witcher 3, you won't be disappointed. I mean, George R.R. Martin (Game of Thrones.. sound familiar) helped write the lore.

Fully customizeable character. It's what I loved about Skyrim. And you have that here. You don't have elves and orcs and lizard people to choose from, but you have a character creation and have a lot of detailed options to build your character with. If you don't like your starting build, you can eventually respec your character. And respec'ing is easy. Beat a fairly early boss and obtain a certain item. Boom. YOu can respec. The item is not common in the game world, but you will eventually look in your inventory and see you have like 10 or 15 of them and be like... Ooh. Let me respec and play with some other builds. Unless you're OCD about respec'ing, you will have way more of the items needed than you will you use.

Combat system. When I say combat system, for me that is the combination of weapons, armor, special skills, and buffs. They are abundant and numerous, but also numerous and abundant.

Weapons: Hundreds of different weapons across dozens of weapon types: Katanas, Greatswords, Flails, spears, halberds, twinblades, daggers, hammers, axes, broadswords, maces, and more. But in each of these categories, different weapons have different move sets. Many of them have their own unique ash of war. When I say Ash of War... just sub in your mind the label "special skill". Many weapons come with organic special skills (aka ash of war). For many weapons, you can switch out an ash of war for a different one. Or if you're plain joe or jane, remove the ash of war and just go old school and crush um with confidence.

Armor - Personally this is a weakness of the game for me... relatively speaking. The armor is still a solid attribute overall though. There are dozens if not over a hundred armor sets. Mostly the armor is straight up there to provide protection against different types of damages. That is straight forward. However, there are some armor pieces that provide buffs/debuffs. Some pieces just provide a buff. But others provide a buff, but a debuff also to balance the buff it gave. It can make choosing your armor set a real strategic decision.

Special skills - I referred to ashes of war above. Ashes of war are items you can obtain in your inventory and then add to your weapon to use the ash of war's skill. Only one ash of war can be put on a weapon at a time. Some weapons come with a permanent ash of war which cannot be removed. But most you can switch them out at any site of grace (point on the map where you can rest, level up, sort your inventory, etc). Sites of grace are all over the map and you can fast travel to them. Once you get just one site of grace, you can fast travel to it and switch out ash of war at will. But by the time you're 10 minutes into the game you'll have already activated 3 or 4 sites of grace. There are probably a hundred or more ashes of war and each comes with a unique skill. Some are similar to others, but still unique.

Buffs- So many miscellaneous items you can either find or craft to give you just a little bit more of an edge over your enemy. Before a boss fight, understand what you want to do and how you want to do it. Arm yourself with the right weapon and pop your buffs before going in. This will take practice and repetition to get down. But once you learn how to use these items, you'll be well on your way to becoming Elden Lord.

Voice Acting- Shout out to the voice actors and whoever cast them for the superb job they did. This ruins a game for me. I loved Skyrim, but I couldn't skip through the dialogue in that game fast enough. It seemed like Bethesda had all of three actors to do all the voice work for Skyrim and Fallout 4. All that money they make and can't get decent voice actors. But i guess Todd Howard needed the money for new rims on his Lexus. You won't be disappointed in the voice acting in Elden Ring. It makes you really feel like you're living in the story.

Finally... big open world map. Lot's of different geographic features. Jump puzzles. Dungeons. And a level of frustration that will make you feel angrily alive. Have some spare controllers or keyboards on stand by. The first time you lose a few thousand runes (game currency and leveling up currency), you will want to throw your controller out the window or smash your keyboard. It's going to happen. Just know that going in. Did I mention big open world map.... and that's before you even download the DLC Shadow of the Erd Tree.

Stop playing whatever you're playing and get Elden Ring. I promise you the game you're playing right now is lame by comparison.

I still think it's debatable whether or not this one of FromSoft's best games, one thing I can give it credit for is it's ambitious in taking the Souls formula into an open world, and it succeeds for the most part. The world is fun to explore, there are more linear sections reminiscent of it's predecessors, fun build options, and enough lore to keep one invested in if that's what they enjoy.

Some faults I had with the game might be the lack of memorable bosses compared to the rest of FromSoft's output and, for some people, the novelty of the open world might wear off and they'll notice it starts to become a bit more of a chore as they go on.

Still, well worth the experience.

Elden Ring isn’t just a game — it’s an experience.
A massive world that doesn’t hold your hand, doesn’t tell you where to go, yet every time you take a step forward, it surprises you with something new.
That feeling of “What am I even doing here?” mixed with “HOLY— this place is insane” is exactly what makes it special.

When I started the DLC, I thought:
“Alright, a few bosses, some new areas, cool.”
Nope.
Shadow of the Erdtree showed up just to prove I had absolutely no idea what real difficulty was.
Boss designs, the environments, the pacing — brutal, beautiful, and insanely satisfying.

What I loved most is how every boss feels like a story.
Not the type that screams lore in your face… but the kind you discover yourself.
And when you finally beat them?
That feeling of victory?
Nothing else in gaming even comes close.

Overall, Elden Ring + SotE gave me everything:
excitement, frustration, awe, rage, joy, disbelief — and finally that big, stupid grin of satisfaction only FromSoftware games can deliver.

If you haven’t played it yet:
You’re missing out on one of the best gaming journeys ever made.

And if you have?
Then you already know exactly what I’m talking about.

10/10 — a masterpiece that I’ll remember for a very long time.

Great game but i have to give it thumbs down for now because of performance issues Fromsoft has not adressed.

When the game launch, i was running the game on a GTX 950 at about flat 40 fps with eventual drops to 28 or so.
As the updates went by, the performance was constantly getting worse and worse, to the point i was no longer able to play.

Now i uptated to an Rtx, and the framerate is great (56-60) but there are MASSIVE and constant freezes, they go from 3 to 15sec, or even to the point of having to restart.

At first i thought i was bottlenecking my cpu (i5), but searching arround, it seems to be a frequent problem now for a lot of people, people with crazy setups even (R9's and i9's paired with rtx X090's).

And after trying a ton of possible fixes, it doesn't seem to work for me at all (cpu affinity and prio, overlays, shutting microsoft services, etc.)

I tried other, heavier games, to check out performance. RE4R for example: Flawless, even with exceeding vram, 70+fps, no stutter, avg of 90fps. So, it's only really this game.

If it's worth anything, i tried checking with Rivatuner what was going on, CPU and GPU stay both at about 50-60% usage, up until it freezes, which is when CPU usage either drops to 0 or jumps to 100%. also searching online, this game apparentaly loves to sit on one core instead of spreading usage on other cores/threads, and core 0 of all the possible ones...

that's about it, hope they fix it, i'm at the point that i would refund if i wasn't way past the refund window.

First 'soulsborne' game I've ever played (Played Lies Of P but it didn't really click to me, will definitely try it at some point since I'm hooked on to these types of games now, currently playing Dark Souls III) and honestly it's an incredible game. From the story telling, the lore, the world-building, the environment and the overall atmosphere while you're playing it, everything the game offers to you feels like a breath of fresh air from the usual AAA games. Sure there are some problems like some areas feel like empty space and have no real use, some VERY annoying enemies (dogs, those rock goblins in dungeons/tombs, BIRDS, etc) but even then there wasn't a single point throughout my first play-through where I thought I was bored. There honestly wasn't a single time where I wasn't constantly trying to do something (other than searching on the eldenring wiki on how to traverse Leyndell). When you first start it sort of feels like a dream, no tutorial (I skipped the tutorial cave on my first playthrough and I still havent gone to check it out so I don't know how much it helps other than the basics of combat and graces probably) no compass, no quest log, no set path, nothing is given to you and there isn't really any help.

The build diversity is amazing (I thought it was the best build diversity a game had to offer ever but Dark Souls II fans said otherwise), and basically any build can get you from the beginning to the end of the game (no matter how terrible or cheese the build is). The NPC's all have their own dialogue, lore, and questlines, going about how to actually complete them is probably harder than beating the game (whoever found how to complete Ranni's quest is crazy) but that's what makes the game fun. The game is at a set difficulty so it doesn't really matter how you play, if you get better at the game, the game doesn't get any harder, it becomes a lot easier, and since the game is open-world, there isn't a set linear path that you have to take in order to enjoy it, you choose how easy or hard you want your playthrough to be (mimic tear).

While I played on PC (KBM) my specs aren't too great which was why I was sort of worried when I first played as I thought my PC wasn't going to be able to handle it, however with some changes to the graphics settings I was able to play at a stable 60 fps at most times unless I was being bombarded by vfx (fyi my specs are: GTX 1650, Intel Core i7-3770 and 16 gigs of ram)

If you are going to buy and play Elden Ring, cherish your first playthrough because it'll really be an experience you won't have again, stepping into limgrave for the first time and seeing the world around you was just so incredible, fighting the tree sentinel and realising you weren't good enough to beat it yet. facing margit for the first time, seeing noksetlla, understanding parrying and actually using movement and not just panic rolling and so on (also if any new players actually read this, PLEASE read item descriptions and actually LISTEN to npc dialogue, as their the main source of information that'll actually help you with completing questlines, hidden areas, items to access areas and secret weapons you wouldn't know about unless you read/listened (or search it up if you're completely lost). Truly an amazing game and I recommend it to anybody that won't give up 10 minutes into the game because they don't know what their doing.

I’d honestly rate the game a 8.5/10

Great game but on a technical front, this game is a disaster. The performance issues are a joke in this game. Lots of pop in, terribly low performance for such a non-technically demanding game, lackluster support for multiple forms of monitors, and so much stuttering. FromSoftware really needs to hire another studio to do all technical programming and engineering for their games, because they just will not fix their issues until they are forced to while hardcore fans will excuse these issues away.

I still recommend this game, but be warned: It will perform like you are several tiers lower on your GPU and CPU. I ran it on both of my PC's (i7-6700k/RTX 3070 TI, i9-13900k/RTX 4070), and even on my PS4 Pro/PS5 copy of this game, and these issues were so persistent on all of these platforms. It would be awesome if FromSoftware could fix these issues, but I strongly doubt they will.

Peak gaming experience.

Before playing Elden Ring, I expected FromSoft games to be nothing more than ragebait centered around punishing difficulty. But now, I’m in love with FromSoft’s Soulsborne games.

It’s a masterpiece, pure art and an unforgettable experience. The bosses feel like more than just encounters; they’re experiences in their own right.

You might feel discouraged by the series’ infamous reputation for difficulty, but it’s really just a steep learning curve at the start.
The game gives you countless tools to overcome its challenges and actively encourages you to use everything at your disposal. And thanks to its open world, if one challenge feels overwhelming, you can simply take a break, explore elsewhere, and return later stronger and better prepared.

Posted: September 28
Elden Ring is more than just another entry in FromSoftware’s legacy of punishing action RPGs—it is their most ambitious and refined creation to date. Built upon the foundations of Dark Souls, Bloodborne, and Sekiro, this game merges intricate combat, cryptic storytelling, and a hauntingly beautiful world into one seamless experience.

The most striking feature is the open world. Unlike many games that fill vast landscapes with repetitive tasks, Elden Ring rewards curiosity at every turn. Each ruin, cave, and towering castle feels handcrafted, hiding secrets, enemies, or lore that deepens the mystery of the Lands Between. Exploration is not only encouraged but essential, creating a sense of genuine adventure and discovery.

Combat remains brutally challenging yet deeply satisfying. Whether facing grotesque beasts or godlike bosses, every encounter tests both patience and skill. The variety of weapons, spells, and builds ensures that players can tailor their approach, from swift dexterity-based duels to overwhelming magical barrages. The addition of mounted combat and spirit summons broadens strategy without diluting the core intensity.

Visually, the game is breathtaking—ruined cathedrals, mist-shrouded swamps, golden fields, and towering mountains all carry a sense of melancholy grandeur. Coupled with a haunting soundtrack, the atmosphere constantly shifts between awe and dread.

The narrative, co-written with George R. R. Martin, is cryptic and fragmented, true to FromSoftware’s style. Rather than spoon-feeding the story, Elden Ring challenges players to piece together its mythology through item descriptions, environmental storytelling, and cryptic dialogue. For some, this will feel enigmatic and rewarding; for others, it may feel impenetrable.

If the game has flaws, they lie mostly in performance hiccups and occasional uneven difficulty spikes. Yet these shortcomings are minor when weighed against the scale of what Elden Ring achieves.

In essence, Elden Ring is not merely a game—it is an odyssey. It demands patience, resilience, and curiosity, but it rewards them with a sense of wonder and accomplishment rarely found in modern gaming. For veterans of FromSoftware, it is the culmination of everything the studio has learned. For newcomers, it may well be the most unforgettable introduction to their dark, punishing, yet beautiful worlds.

Score: 10/10 — A modern masterpiece.

Posted: September 28
Elden Ring is easily one of the most breathtaking and brutal games I’ve ever played. FromSoftware took everything that makes the Soulsborne series legendary and cranked it up to eleven in an open world that’s as massive as it is gorgeous. Every location — from rolling fields and golden trees to dark catacombs and ruined castles — is a work of art. You can stop at almost any point in the game, look around, and it feels like you’re staring at a painting. It’s absolutely beautiful, even when that beauty hides something that’s about to kill you.

Gameplay-wise, it’s pure Soulsborne. The combat is punishing, every enemy feels dangerous, and the bosses are on another level entirely. You will die a lot, but that’s part of the experience. Each fight forces you to learn, adapt, and improve, and the rush you get when you finally bring down a boss you’ve been stuck on is unmatched. It’s not just hard for the sake of being hard — it’s challenging in a way that makes every victory feel earned.

The lore is ridiculously deep. Miyazaki’s storytelling mixed with George R. R. Martin’s worldbuilding creates something both haunting and mysterious. The Lands Between feels alive with history, tragedy, and secrets waiting to be uncovered. Item descriptions, cryptic NPC dialogue, and environmental storytelling all come together to build a world that feels endless in depth and mystery.

That said, I’ve got to call it like it is: the Fire Giant is the worst boss. Period. He’s massive, awkward, and honestly feels more like you’re wrestling with the camera than actually fighting him. Still, even with that frustrating encounter, Elden Ring’s boss roster is packed with unforgettable battles that test your skill and patience in the best ways possible.

At the end of the day, Elden Ring is a masterpiece. It’s hard, it’s beautiful, it’s rewarding, and it sticks with you long after you’ve stopped playing. If you’re into Soulsborne games, this is the ultimate experience. If you’re new, prepare for pain — but trust me, the struggle is worth it.

I think this is the greatest game ever made at the time of this review. I have repeatedly told all of my friends that Skyrim is now my #2 favorite game of all time. This is the game that dethroned it.

There isn't a single aspect of this game that is lacking, in my opinion. It has some of the best gameplay you could ever ask for. It has hundreds of weapons to choose from. There's something to fill almost every niche you could think of. Multiple play styles are all perfectly viable and it includes the ability to respec your character after the second major boss, so you could potentially make use of literally any of them in a single run. But you can also have multiple characters. You can even play it with your friends without downloading a mod. (But if you want to have a better experience, the Seamless Multiplayer mod is safe and carefully designed so you won't get banned from the core game.) The unmodded multiplayer is the only minor problem I could list about the game.

Aesthetically, it is incredibly impressive. I can't think of a single game that has blown me away this much from just a visual perspective. The landscapes, the atmosphere, the music, the character and enemy designs, every tiny polygon of this game has been lovingly crafted. It doesn't have the absolute most high resolution textures or the most stunning raytracing, but you won't notice any of that because you'll be too busy drooling at the art direction.

The environmental storytelling is second to none. Where many games are content to just throw items at you for no reason that makes sense, Elden Ring continues the tradition that From Software started with Demon's Souls. Every single item was thoughtfully and carefully placed to make sense for it to be there, and each has a description that fleshes out the story of the world. Do yourself a favor, read all of them. This game is absolutely beautifully written and if you dedicate yourself to understanding the lore, it will touch your (dark) soul.

You may have heard that this game is hard. It is. It will be one of the hardest games you have ever played. And if you make it to the end of Shadow of the Erdtree and take down the final boss, you will have to overcome countless seemingly insurmountable obstacles. But the feeling of standing at the foot of the Gate of Divinity having overcome all of it... Well, there's nothing else like it. You owe it to yourself to give this game a try.

Elden Ring is honestly one of the best games I’ve ever played. The open world feels massive, dark, and beautiful, and everywhere you go there’s something new—whether it’s a hidden boss, a creepy dungeon, or some rare loot. Exploring never feels boring, because the game keeps surprising you.

The combat is tough at first, but once you get used to dodging, blocking, and timing your hits, it becomes super satisfying. What makes it even better is the vast build variety—you can go full strength with colossal weapons, be a fast dex fighter, focus on magic and sorcery, or even mix everything together. Plus, riding your horse, Torrent, makes the world easier to explore and adds new ways to fight.

The story is mysterious and doesn’t hand you answers directly. Some people might find that confusing, but for me it made the game feel deeper and more interesting, like a puzzle you slowly piece together.

Overall, Elden Ring is challenging, rewarding, and unforgettable. Every victory against a boss feels like a personal achievement, and that’s what makes it so addictive. Truly a 10/10 Game

Posted: September 27
Two full playthroughs and 80+ hours later, I can confidently say that Elden Ring isn’t just a game — it’s an experience that defines what modern action RPGs should aspire to be. I’ve played a lot of games across genres, but few have captured my attention and respect like this one. Every inch of the Lands Between is dripping with atmosphere, mystery, and purpose.

What really makes Elden Ring special is the sheer freedom it gives you. It never holds your hand, never tells you where to go, and never cheapens the satisfaction of discovery. Whether you’re wandering into an unexpected boss fight, piecing together lore from subtle world-building, or experimenting with builds and weapons to match your playstyle, it’s constantly rewarding your curiosity and persistence.

The combat is brutal but fair — every death is a lesson, every victory a real accomplishment. Boss fights feel epic and cinematic without losing the mechanical precision that makes them so satisfying. And the build diversity is genuinely impressive: from colossal strength builds that crush enemies to nimble dexterity setups and intricate magic hybrids, every run can feel completely different.

But what really surprised me is how much replayability Elden Ring has. Even on my second playthrough, I was still discovering new areas, side quests, weapons, and NPC interactions I’d missed the first time. The game’s open-world design rewards exploration on a level that few titles can match, and it never feels like it’s wasting your time.

Verdict: Elden Ring is a once-in-a-generation experience — challenging, beautiful, and endlessly rewarding. Two playthroughs in, I’m already planning a third. If you value exploration, challenge, and true creative freedom in how you approach a game, this is an absolute must-play.

From the very moment I set foot in the Lands Between, Elden Ring established itself as something truly special. Developed by FromSoftware, the creators of Dark Souls, Bloodborne, and Sekiro, this game feels like the culmination of everything the studio has been building toward for over a decade. It successfully blends the intricate combat and atmospheric world-building the studio is known for with a vast, seamless open world that encourages exploration at every turn.

The first thing that struck me was the sheer scale and beauty of the world. Elden Ring doesn’t just offer a large map—it offers a living, breathing landscape filled with secrets, dangers, and rewards. Every cliffside, ruined castle, and swamp begs to be explored, and there’s always something waiting for you: a hidden boss, a new weapon, or lore that deepens the mystery of the Lands Between. Unlike many open-world games that overwhelm players with icons and markers, Elden Ring rewards curiosity and intuition, making exploration feel organic and meaningful.

Combat is challenging but incredibly rewarding, staying true to FromSoftware’s reputation. The variety of weapons, spells, and builds ensures that every player can approach the game in their own way. Whether you prefer to be a nimble rogue, a heavily armored knight, or a sorcerer wielding devastating magic, Elden Ring gives you the tools to create your own playstyle. Boss fights are where the game truly shines—each encounter is memorable, with enemies that are as terrifying as they are beautifully designed. Beating a tough boss in Elden Ring isn’t just progress—it’s an accomplishment that feels earned.

What also sets Elden Ring apart is its storytelling. Instead of spoon-feeding the narrative, the game allows players to piece together the lore through item descriptions, environmental clues, and cryptic NPC dialogue. Combined with the haunting soundtrack and breathtaking visuals, the atmosphere is unmatched. George R.R. Martin’s contributions to the world’s backstory add depth without overshadowing FromSoftware’s signature storytelling style.

Performance-wise, Elden Ring is impressive. The level of polish in animations, combat flow, and world design is remarkable given the game’s size. Even after dozens of hours, I still stumble upon new areas, secret dungeons, and optional bosses, proving just how dense and carefully crafted the world truly is.

In the end, Elden Ring isn’t just a game—it’s an experience. It respects the player’s intelligence, challenges their skill, and rewards their perseverance. For fans of action RPGs, it sets a new gold standard in the genre. For newcomers, it offers a gateway into the brilliance of FromSoftware’s design philosophy, though patience and persistence are required.

Final Verdict: 10/10.
Elden Ring is a once-in-a-generation title that redefines what an open-world RPG can be. If you’re willing to embrace the challenge, you’ll find an unforgettable adventure waiting in the Lands Between.

When I think back on my journey with FromSoftware games, it feels almost surreal how it all started and how it eventually led me to Elden Ring. My very first step into the world of soulslike games was not through the most traditional door. It was 2023, I still had my PlayStation 4, and instead of starting with Dark Souls or Bloodborne like most people recommend, I chose Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice. Looking back, it was a strange, almost reckless choice for a beginner. Sekiro is unforgiving, precise, and merciless. For six long months I struggled, died countless times, cursed my own hands, and questioned if I would ever finish it. But eventually, after endless hours of practice and persistence, I overcame the final boss and realized something life-changing: I wasn’t just playing a game, I was experiencing something deeper, something that demanded every bit of me. And I fell in love.

From that moment, it was impossible to stop. I dived headfirst into the entire Dark Souls trilogy, one after another, discovering the haunting beauty, the cryptic lore, and the brutal yet rewarding gameplay loop that defines these worlds. Each death taught me patience, every victory felt like a triumph carved in stone. It was a cycle of despair and euphoria that I couldn’t resist. And then came Bloodborne, the crown jewel for me personally. Fast, aggressive, atmospheric in a way that no other game has matched—Bloodborne became my absolute favorite, the one that still lingers in the back of my mind long after I put down the controller.

I thought I had seen the peak of what FromSoftware could offer, until I decided to try Elden Ring. At first, I picked it up again on my old PS4, still buzzing from my past journeys. The game’s world was vast and breathtaking, yet I couldn’t ignore the painful reality: its performance was terrible on that console. Stuttering framerates, muddy textures, and constant slowdowns turned what should have been a magical experience into a frustrating one. I gave up after thirteen hours, not because the game lacked brilliance, but because I couldn’t bear to see it run that poorly. For a time, Elden Ring remained a distant dream, something I respected but couldn’t fully embrace.

That changed this year. After months of saving, I finally built my own gaming PC, capable of handling almost everything at over 90 to 100 FPS. The very first game I knew I had to buy again was Elden Ring. And the moment I stepped back into the Lands Between, it was like rediscovering the magic of FromSoftware all over again. The sheer scale of the open world, the haunting beauty of its landscapes, the freedom to carve my own path—it all felt overwhelming in the best way possible. Every detail amazed me, from the subtle environmental storytelling to the sheer brutality of its enemies. Combat felt smoother than ever, bosses were terrifying and awe-inspiring, and exploration never ceased to surprise me. Elden Ring isn’t just a game—it’s an odyssey, an entire world begging to be uncovered layer by layer.

And yet, despite all that, I still feel like I’ve only scratched the surface. Even after completing my first playthrough and diving into New Game+, I know there are still secrets hidden away, builds I’ve yet to experiment with, and corners of the world I haven’t truly explored. That’s why, for now, I haven’t bought the DLC. Not because I don’t want to, but because I believe I haven’t yet absorbed everything Elden Ring already has to offer me. One day I’ll get there, and when I do, I know I’ll savor every second of that new chapter.

Until then, Elden Ring remains not just another game in my library, but a monumental experience—one that continues to remind me why I fell in love with soulslikes in the first place. And when the time comes to step into its DLC, I know I’ll cherish every moment as if it were the very first time.

Elden Ring: A Stunning, Gruesome Masterpiece That's Hard to Put Down

The moment I chose the Samurai class — because samurai are quite irrefutably cool — I knew that Elden Ring was going to be an experience unlike any other. And it quite literally didn't let me down on any front. This game is a masterpiece in terms of scale and in terms of texture, having sequences that left me wide-eyed and never wishing it to ever stop.

The open-world exploration is where Elden Ring truly shines. It's not just big — it's meaningful. Every new area feels labor-of-love crafted, and there's always this sense of wonder as you uncover hidden paths, deadly bosses, and enigmatic NPCs. Ruined Farum Azula impressed me personally — a stunningly haunting zone that felt like fighting on the ruins of a dying god's fantasy.

Talking about fights, Maliketh, the Black Blade was my hands-down favorite boss. The fight was epic, stylish, and so satisfying to finally conquer with my strength player build. I didn't venture into co-op or PvP, but to clear everything by myself made the victories all the more special.

Ranni the Witch's questline was a particular highlight. It added a sense of mysterious, emotional depth to the world and had me actually feeling like I was controlling the fate of the Lands Between.

Yes, it's a difficult game. But the difficulty felt rewarding and fair — an authentic test of learning and perseverance rather than cheap tricks. And the reality is, the difficulty is part of the pleasure. It makes each victory feel earned.

Would I recommend Elden Ring to a person who's never played FromSoftware games? Yes. It's easier to get into than it looks, and after you're in, you're really in.

If you're even remotely curious, take a shot. Just don't be surprised when you're lost in one of the most iconic gaming experiences out there.

10/10

Posted: September 26
Must play game. In many ways it's a huge leap forward from Souls with a few minor steps back. Most of the cons come from trying to adapt Souls to be a fully open world game.

Pros:
Lore is very deep despite not building off of Souls lore.
More items to play with than ever and tons of build variety.
"Ashes of War" weapon arts are awesome.
Combat mechanics are even more polished and refined than Souls.
Real jumping opens up tons of new possibilities.
Less random difficulty spikes than I remember from Souls games.
Plenty of memorable areas and bosses.

Cons:
Lots of reused boss fights.
Lots of empty space making exploring every bit of the game tedious.
When exploring you often come across dungeons like catacombs and caves that have unique layouts and quirks, but that use the same "tileset" across the whole game.
While most summons felt balanced enough, the Mimic Tear felt too strong against some bosses I tried it on.

As someone with more than 1,000 hours on PS5 and 150 on PC, this is one of the greatest games of all time. While in my opinion the actual base combat and art style aren't as good as Sekiro's, Bloodborne's and DS3's, it makes up for it with an incredible variety of builds. This game certainly isn't the best souls game in any distinct category, but if each souls game has a 10/10 quality, Elden Ring takes that 10/10 quality from each game to bring them all together. It is certainly a jack of all trades, master of none in the souls space. The only glaring issue I find with the game is the move sets of the bosses. Miyazaki himself stated that they went too far in trying to craft a difficult game, instead causing unbalanced enemy stats, overlong combo strings, all while having input delay from a run button tied to the dodge button. Thankfully these issues have been acknowledged for their next games; and we should be even more thankful for even having this game at all. So, thank you, Miyazaki and your team at FromSoftware. Flaws and all.

There are video games, and then there is Elden Ring. To even call it a “game” feels wrong — it is an experience, a pilgrimage, a trial by fire that takes everything you think you know about open worlds and smashes it into glittering shards, reforging it into something at once terrifying and transcendent.

From the very first moment you step into Limgrave, you feel it: the world breathes with menace and wonder. The earth is scarred, the skies drip with melancholy, and every horizon whispers a promise — not of safety, but of secrets, challenges, and revelations. That golden tree, impossibly vast, dominating the skyline — it is not just a landmark, it is a gravitational pull on your soul, dragging you deeper and deeper into a story written not with words, but with ruins, ghosts, and blood.

Every fight is a heartbeat. Every enemy, from the lowliest soldier to the towering demi-gods, is a teacher, hammering humility into you until your failures become stepping stones toward mastery. You die — and die again — but when you finally fell that monster that once seemed insurmountable, when your blade strikes true and the beast collapses, the triumph feels earned, not gifted. You are not coddled. You are forged.

The sheer scale of Elden Ring is unfathomable. Just when you think you’ve seen it all — when the map feels vast enough to swallow you — you stumble into a hidden cave, an elevator that descends into an underground cosmos, a forgotten city glittering with starlight. It is endless discovery. It is curiosity rewarded a thousand times over. No checklist, no hollow icons. Just wonder, dread, and the courage to keep stepping forward.

And the lore — my god, the lore. Written in whispers, in item descriptions, in the broken architecture of a fallen world. George R. R. Martin may have helped sketch the bones, but it’s FromSoftware’s genius that fleshes it into a mythos so rich, so tragic, that piecing it together feels like unearthing a forgotten epic poem. It is a story you live more than you read.

Elden Ring does not just ask you to play it — it demands you give yourself to it. It is brutal, it is beautiful, it is despair and ecstasy bound in steel and flame. It is not perfect because perfection is sterile. Elden Ring bleeds, it rages, it scars, and that is why it feels alive.

This isn’t just Game of the Year. This is Game of the Generation. Maybe even the greatest game ever made.

When I think of Elden Ring, I don’t just think of a game. I think of standing before Margit, hands shaking, heart racing. I think of cresting a hill and seeing Caelid’s rot-stained nightmare for the first time. I think of losing myself under the eternal stars of Nokron. I think of being small, insignificant, fragile — and rising anyway.

Elden Ring is more than entertainment. It is a trial of spirit. It is art. It is legend.

Not my favorite Fromsoft game, but also not my least favorite. I really love the tight level design of every other game they've done in this formula, so the open world thing kind of falls short for me. While I did appreciate the notion that I can go exploring instead of getting stuck on something, I had a moment early on where I realized that a valid solution to many problems was to hop on a horse and just run around the problem.

I really loved all of the so-called Legacy Dungeons, I loved the boss design, I really appreciated how the Stakes of Marika worked because it made summoning a ton of fun and made me spend a significant amount of time helping other players out, and to be clear, I think there's a lot to appreciate about this game. It's a damn good game.

I just got a little tired near the end when a lot of the open world stuff just started to feel samey to me. I had been clearing out everything I could find for a while, but the crypts and castles started to blend together in a really bad way after a while. After a while I just started running around nearly everything with my horse because exploring crypt number 75 with the same general design and same general enemies didn't sound enticing.

I did enjoy the DLC a good bit, but I did feel like many of the areas were kind of devoid of anything to do. They certainly looked pretty, but that was about it.

Overall, I think this game was great and I had a ton of fun playing it, but I also sort of hope that they don't keep with the open world formula for their next games. I miss the well-designed smaller linear areas of all their other games. There wasn't enough of that in between running around a gigantic zone on a horse, for me at least.

Fromsoft Gold. Fashion Souls, pick your Armament and build around it. Only downside is how repetitive armament upgrading is since you'll likely want to make many characters and builds.

The upgrade material and rune acquisition becomes quite repetitive with multiple playthroughs, not just because it's easy to optimize these things using the internet, but also because the alternative methods are sufficiently in-effectual. Too many enemies were made to give little to no runes presumably with the assumption of exploring large swaths of the open world, but with the chance to lose runes and upgrade weapons a less prepared player might later choose not to use leaves little room for acquiring alternative materials and runes. it works fine for figuring things out and enjoying the first playthrough, but when you make your 5th character and figure you'll just go do some random mausoleum or dungeon for runes and it can't even level you up after multiple, you quickly notice there's only a few actually decent ways to progress, and it's a shame some of the lesser enemies and bosses weren't given a bit of a runes and upgrade materials rewards boost so you could kinda do some different stuff outside obtaining very specific items that make your current build different.

Spells that are asinine to land hits with should probably have their damage buffed a bit to give an incentive to using a larger variety, AI spell dodging is often ubiquitous enough to make some spells feel un-usable.

Posted: September 23
This game saved my life.

I am 27.

My ex-wife and I have a daughter together, and adopted our son together. They are now both 4 years old.

When we were going through our separation, I found myself lost and miserable. I was self destructive. I got so mad one day from everything spiraling out of my control that I punched some concrete in a moment of overwhelming emotion. That caused me to break my 5th metacarpal in my right hand... my working hand... my games hand.. the hand that I held and carried my children to bed with.. The hand I desperately needed to make sure I could continue to provide.

After learning of the severity of my self-inflicted damage, I was borderline suicidal. Keep in mind that just a few months before this, I was the happiest man with no history of depression or anxiety. I have never had fits of rage, or been one to break down and cry, but I was in a low spot that just really buried me from being able to see the light on the other side.

Having nothing better to do, I searched for a game I could play, ONE HANDED while I recovered. I somehow stumbled upon this game and read some of the reviews. I decided that it had to be worth a shot... I must admit, I didn't beat the game, or play nearly as long as some of you. In fact, I may have only played this game a day or two.. With that being said, after doing so, I had a new found joy and hope for life. I was able to put behind me the pain and suffering that had been cast over me. I was able to experience other peoples joy and happiness. I was able to see the fruits of my "labor". I relaxed for 5 ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ minutes to this game long enough to realize that I was going to be okay.

After coming to that realization, I turned the game off, and I went back to work. It hurt my hand like hell but I was motivated. I stopped feeling so damn sorry for myself, and I became the father I needed to be in that moment, not the weak boy I was behaving as.

Today, I am close friend with my kids mother. We don't fight, or argue, or say hurtful things to each other. We are parents, and friends.

I now have 3 kids. My third child is, wait for it, ALSO 4 YEARS OLD. The woman I am with was going through a very similar situation at the time of my own separation, and we just stumbled in to each others life unexpectedly. We have been in a relationship for a year now, and are very happy together.

Moral of the story, you never know what life holds in store for you, and if I would have given up when all the odds were stacked against me, I wouldn't be where I am today. This silly little game helped me realize that.

Thank you

A Journey Beyond Compare – Elden Ring Review

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

Hark, fellow adventurer, and lend thine ear to my tale of wondrous discovery, for I have traversed the vast and perilous lands of Elden Ring—a realm where danger and glory dance in equal measure, and where the very heavens themselves seem to watch over thy every move.

Elden Ring is not merely a game; nay, it is a legend in the making, a saga that shall be whispered of for generations. A world, vast and teeming with secrets, awaits those brave enough to set foot in the Lands Between. In this mighty creation from FromSoftware, the hand of the master Hidetaka Miyazaki has woven a tapestry of unparalleled beauty and unrelenting challenge.

The Realm of the Lands Between: A World Like No Other

The Lands Between is a kingdom of staggering beauty and danger, stretching far and wide in every direction. From the golden fields of Limgrave to the ancient, haunted halls of the Forbidden Lands, every inch of this world is rich with story and substance. The landscapes are painted with care, where every ruin tells the tale of fallen kings, lost gods, and the dark forces that plague the land. There is no place quite like it—a place where the very soil seems to carry the weight of the ages, and where adventure beckons from every horizon.

The freedom to explore this vast realm is a gift unto itself. With no fixed path, the player is free to journey as they will, uncovering secrets and battling foes in whatever order they see fit. Each discovery, whether it be a hidden cave, a forgotten village, or a towering fortress, fills the heart with the thrill of the unknown. And oh, how that sense of wonder never fades, for the more you explore, the more you realize just how much is left to uncover.

Combat: A Symphony of Steel and Sorcery

If the world of Elden Ring is the stage, then the combat is its grand performance. Gone are the days of mindless button-mashing. Here, every swing of the sword, every spell cast, and every dodge performed feels like a crucial moment in a battle for survival. Combat is fluid, deliberate, and deeply satisfying, whether one wields a mighty greatsword, a dexterous rapier, or a staff to summon the power of the stars.

The enemies—my gods, the enemies! From the lowly bandits to the towering giants and the terrifying demigods, each foe presents a unique challenge that demands skill, patience, and timing. Boss fights are nothing short of legendary, each encounter a grand spectacle where victory is earned only through perseverance and wit. The satisfaction of overcoming these mighty beasts, their roars and shrieks echoing in the air, is beyond measure.

But do not fear, for though the enemies are fierce, you are not alone in this journey. The spirits of the fallen, the spectral allies, and the wise NPCs offer guidance when needed, though trust must be earned. Elden Ring rewards both caution and boldness in equal measure, and the choices you make—whether to confront an enemy head-on or find another way around—are yours to decide.

The Elden Ring: A Quest Like No Other

At the heart of this adventure lies the mysterious and powerful Elden Ring, an artifact of immense power that has shattered the world and set the stage for your quest to become Elden Lord. The story is one of great ambition and mystery, told in fragments that you, the player, must piece together through exploration, conversation, and the remnants of ancient lore scattered throughout the world.

And yet, the story is not thrust upon you. It is a tale that unfolds naturally, through the choices you make and the paths you choose to follow. Will you embrace the light, or will you succumb to the darkness? The world of Elden Ring allows for many endings, and each one feels like a true reflection of your journey.

An Experience Unmatched

The world-building in Elden Ring is a marvel in itself. Every character, every locale, every item, and every detail in the game feels meticulously crafted. The lore is deep and rich, unfolding like the pages of an ancient tome, with tales of gods, kings, and heroes long lost to time. The dialogue, the visual storytelling, the very design of the world—all serve to immerse you in a world that feels as alive as it is mysterious.

The soundtrack, composed by the great Yuka Kitamura, is a masterpiece in its own right. The music swells with grandeur in moments of triumph and chills with foreboding in moments of danger. It is the perfect accompaniment to the journey, an unseen presence that breathes life into every moment.

A Call to Arms: The Challenge Awaits

Be warned, brave soul—Elden Ring is not for the faint of heart. The challenges that await you are as fierce as they are numerous. Death will come swiftly and often, and the road to victory is fraught with peril. But this is no punishment; it is the very heart of the adventure. For it is through trial and tribulation that true strength is forged.

Those who dare venture into the Lands Between must be prepared for a challenge that will test their skill, their resolve, and their patience. Yet, it is precisely this difficulty that makes the victories so sweet and the journey so rewarding. Elden Ring does not hold your hand—it beckons you into the unknown, with the promise that the rewards, both in glory and in story, will be worth every trial.

Final Thoughts: A Masterpiece for the Ages

In conclusion, Elden Ring is a monumental achievement in gaming, one that will stand the test of time as one of the greatest works of its kind. It is a world of beauty, danger, mystery, and wonder, where every corner hides a story waiting to be discovered. It is a game that invites you to lose yourself in its depths, to face impossible odds, and to rise from the ashes stronger than ever before.

If thou art a lover of adventure, if thou art a seeker of glory, if thou art willing to risk all for the sake of greatness—then Elden Ring awaits thee. Answer the call, and become the Elden Lord.

5/5 stars – An experience beyond the bounds of mortal comprehension.

Product received for free

tl;dr: excellent mechanics, nice visuals, heaps of content; somewhat empty "open world", slightly inconsistent difficulty.

Game Mechanics and Content
They finally figured out that boss runbacks are not fun, and now there are respawn points next to every boss arena.
Fast travel is available immediately and doesn't require any items or spells.
Multiplayer is finally done right: there are no PvP invasions in solo play, unless you manually trigger them; and participating in co-op is as easy as ever with summon signs being automatically sent to every area with a single click.
The amount of weapons, spells, and other items is overwhelming, you can make infinite build combinations.
Weapon upgrades are simpler, and special attacks and infusions are free - mix and match all day long!
Spirit summons provide co-op-like experience in solo play.
Day/night cycle is beautiful, and has an extra effect that some optional bosses only appear at night.
Side quests are interesting and quite difficult to mess up by accident (though still possible in some cases).
Map is very helpful for exploration, and supports custom markers; but for some reason doesn't show some side characters, not sure if it's intentional or needs to be fixed.


The fact that they had to give you a horse says everything: it's a band-aid for the waste of time that is travelling from one cave/catacomb/castle to another. And sure, the horse comes with extra features such as mounted combat and double-jump, which is nice and cool, but it doesn't solve the underlying problem of content being too sparse. Maybe these large open spaces could be used for PvP or for getting out of range of enemies you don't want to interact with at a given time, not sure.
And just to make the space seem less empty, they put a group of enemies here and there, and fill everything with crafting materials.
There's also not a lot of background sound in woods and fields in my opinion. If you want scenery, Witcher3 does it better.


On one hand, it's far easier than previous titles. Due to its "open-worldness", you're not locked into a specific route and can traverse areas in (almost) any order. So if some boss is giving you trouble - just go the other way and return later with better gear.
On the other hand, late game turns into the same rollfest as DS3.

I'm incredibly happy that my first playthrough happened after all the nerfs, but I think a couple of late-game bosses need to be dumbed down by another 20%. You can always make the game harder for yourself if you want, but it's difficult to make it easier.


The game uses EAC, which isn't advertised on store page (at the time of writing), and while not ideal, is understandable for multiplayer part of the game. However, mad props to FromSoftware for keeping an offline mode and also a non-EAC binary that can be used with mods outside of official environment.

Leaving this here to let anyone that loved the souls/bloodborne games know this game is not AT ALL similar to your traditional souls games. The mechanics, playstyle, and overall "souls" feeling is drastically different in this game.

Where the Souls franchise let you make almost any build viable, in Elden Ring you're forced to play "meta" or suffer low damage. I was intended to run a tank/bruiser build but HAD to swap it out for a dex/faith build instead due to the fact I couldn't come close to beating a boss, even with perfectly executed evades. I'd have to waste 10 minutes just dodging and dealing small amounts of damage.

Some other mechanics like not being able to upgrade your armour is also not present in this game. It can be confusing at times to figure out what to do especially when it comes to your build. The world is VERY vast and expansive so you can very easily miss essential items that could otherwise compliment your build.

Having to look up a guide, or use a map just to find key items without spending hours running through and naturally discovering items shouldn't be the case. In the other Souls games item discovery wasn't this easily missable and didn't require you to open a map to find one item.

In short, the story is interesting, the mechanics are infuriating at times but not unbearable. If you really want to experience what Dark Souls could have been as an open world slogfest then this is the game for you. Much less fighting and much more exploring/platforming, which in my opinion is the weakest part of this game.

The community is still the same tho with leaving very bs messages around the map.

A good 6/10 for me, but yours will probably rate higher.

Posted: September 22
Elden Ring certainly knows how to make a first impression. The game is gorgeous—FromSoftware has basically turned “postcard screenshots” into a gameplay feature. The vistas alone could sell copies, and honestly, wandering around just to see what’s over the next hill feels like half the point. The lore, in its usual FromSoft “good luck figuring this out” style, is surprisingly compelling this time, especially the cosmic hints about stars, aliens, and whatever eldritch thing the Erdtree really is. Magic users also got the royal treatment: slinging spells is flashy, satisfying, and borderline overpowered if you commit. As for melee combat, well—it’s fine. Serviceable. You press the button, things die eventually. Nothing revolutionary, but it holds the house together while everything else steals the spotlight.

Of course, once the shine wears off, Elden Ring reveals its less flattering side: it’s boring. Not in the “slow burn, deep atmosphere” way, but in the “what am I actually doing here for the next 200 hours” way. Exploration may sprawl endlessly, but don’t expect any real surprises—there are no clever puzzles, no hidden systems, no magical item that makes you rethink how you play. Just another sword slightly bigger than the last. The dungeons blur together into the same recycled layouts, and the bosses, while initially intimidating, all boil down to the same strategy: chip away, lower the health bar, roll credits. After a while, it feels less like adventuring and more like wandering a medieval mall—walk, kill, loot, repeat. Roleplaying? Nonexistent. Pickpocketing, dialogue choices, clever problem-solving? Forget it. It’s combat or bust, and the monotony sets in fast.

One thing worth celebrating is that Elden Ring is, by FromSoft standards, actually playable by regular humans. Compared to Dark Souls, the difficulty curve is flatter, the checkpoints are more forgiving, and the whole experience feels streamlined rather than sadistic. Some purists might grumble, but honestly, it’s refreshing to enjoy the world without needing to dedicate your life to memorizing boss patterns like it’s a part-time job.

It’s also worth noting that, for all its reputation, Elden Ring isn’t much of an RPG. Sure, it borrows the terminology—stats, builds, gear—but in practice it’s a barren action-adventure game wearing a thin RPG mask. There’s no real roleplaying, no branching choices, and very little in the way of meaningful systems beyond “swing harder or cast flashier.” If you’re looking for the depth of a true RPG, something like Baldur’s Gate 3 or even Solasta delivers far more value than Elden Ring ever will.

In the end, I like Elden Ring, and I’d recommend it—especially if you’ve always wanted the “real Soulsborne experience” without the constant threat of a controller embedded in your wall. It’s enormous, sometimes to the point of being intimidating, and yes, it can absolutely get boring once the novelty fades. But for the most part, if you’re into big-budget, AAA mainstream games, this is a no-brainer. It delivers the spectacle, the atmosphere, and just enough challenge to make you feel accomplished without forcing you to suffer. Not perfect, not revolutionary, but definitely worth the ride.

10/10, fantastic game.

The difficulty is much more artificial than actualy hard games (the combat can be very hard if you ignore like 30% of the combact mechanics, like Summons and consumable items, for example, or if you put a hard level cap on yourself), and the ending is atrocious (especially because the very last boss of the main game is one of the worst in the game - not the hardest, no, but worst, as in boring, since like half of the battle is you chasing the thing).

The OST is excelent (Godskin Apostle is fantastic), the ambiance and the visuals of the game are great. They also work very well with verticality in the exploration, and the freedom to do whatever you want in (mostly) any order you want is also very cool. Reminds me of Fallout New Vegas a bit, since the maps don't entirely scale with you, so you can end up in much harder places than you can handle at the time.

The boss fights are generally great, with some glaring exceptions (the final boss, the Dragons and the Fire Giant). In the DLC most of them are also great (PCR being a much better final boss than Elden Beast), and with the exception of Furnace Golems (the only "puzzle" field bosses, but that end up being tedious by your third or fourth encounter) and most Dragons (with the exception of Bayle - Bayle being an actual good fight took me by surprise) all of them are very well implemented.

Most characters you meet are great, though I do think Melina was pretty much wasted. Her sacrifice should feel impactful and personal, but you interact so little with her (even if you go out of your way to get all her dialogue at Sites of Grace) that it ends up being whatever. Ranni and Fia are very well done, and most side quests in the game are great.

The story great and with some exceptions (like one of the biggest reveals in the game being written as a DEV MESSAGE ON THE FLOOR) I quite enjoyed how they tell the story (either through the scenary or through item descriptions - might sound weird by I do enjoy reading up on what each item represet in the world). The ending, however, is atrocious. I got the basic "Elden Lord" ending and honestly, the fact that this is what follows the slog of fighting Elden Beast felt like a personal slight. Everything that people complaing about the ending of ME 3 (pre-patch at that) is actually what the ending of Elden Ring was. Just, wow. The last 5 minutes of the game is pretty much heavily phoned in. It feels like they wrapped the game up on launch day.

All in all, I do heavily recommend it. This was my first FromSoft game and while the ending soured the experience heavily for me, I do plan on tackling the rest of the Soulsborne games, including Sekiro.

I'll do a couple of NG+ runs to get the other endings.

Posted: September 22
TLDR: Get this game for the lore and world-building if it's on sale, avoid because of jank combat system.

I really wish steam had an in-between option for recommendations because that is exactly how I feel about Elden Ring.

Of all the souls-like games I've played, Elden Ring has given me the most enjoyment. Adapting the dark souls formula into an open-world where exploration, story-building, and combat are all up to the player is such a fantastic idea and the amount of effort that the devs put into that concept shows! I really felt like I was experiencing the world through my Tarnished's eyes, and learning to choose for myself the direction that I felt the story needed to go. The characters have as much depth or shallowness as you give them, and despite my worries, I had no problem figuring out where to go to progress the story.

Unfortunately, there's one major flaw that keeps me from recommending this game: the combat system.

1) The system shows its age. It clearly hasn't been seriously re-worked since dark souls 3. If it has, it wasn't enough. How do I know? Well, play any other Fromsoft game and you'll see that standard Souls jank. Stuff like enemies hitting through solid objects, enemies phasing in and out of things, and physics glitches that I can only imagine are left to irritate the player. These issues have persisted ever since the original Demon's Souls, and for some reason Fromsoft has never fixed any of them.

2) Input queing. For those unaware, input quieing is a way for games to prevent you from spamming items and attacks. When a button is pressed, it enters the que, and after a certain period of time has passed, the game allows that action to occur. Unfortunately, Fromsoft's implementation here is just... broken. There are times where I will press a button to do anything, say attack, roll, or heal, and my character just... stands there. I've checked my controller multiple times and tested it with other games, but no. Elden Ring just sometimes eats my inputs, which can be all the difference between beating a boss or starting the whole thing over. The opposite is also true in that action canceling is also inconsistent. Often times I'll press a button to attack, then roll to dodge, only for my character to come out of the roll swinging. While that sounds cool, if I needed to dodge repeatedly this usually means I'm now stuck in a stun-lock combo.

3) Enemy AI. I'm not a big fan of the whole "wait for an eternity while the boss holds their sword over their head" shtick, but I can concede that it can be overcome by learning to time things properly. What I can't excuse, however are:
- Overabundance of seeking projectiles, including normal arrows. Wood and stone do not make heat seeking missiles.
- Enemies winding up for an attack in one direction and then instantly pivoting 180 degrees to smack you on the head. I hate to be that guy, but physics still does exist in-game, and I double anyone can spin while one-handing a heavy club.
- Aforementioned stun lock combos that provide no opportunity to counter or dodge out.
- Attacks that hit mid-roll, completely trivializing its role as a survival method. Also, inconsistent hitboxes for enemies and weapons.
- Input reading. Look, it's fine if the enemy AI needs to know what I'm doing in order to attack. However, the AI should NOT be able to instantly react to one of my button presses, especially if they react before the qued action even begins. That's like if a cowboy was able to shoot a man in a duel the moment their opponent even started thinking about drawing their revolver.

I understand and accept that this is a game in a fantasy setting. But if you set some rules for the player, it seems reasonable that other entities in the world should somewhat follow those rules as well. This is not that game.

Overall, I think this is a game everyone should play for the experience, but not at full price. I honestly wish a little more time had been spent on reworking the combat, even if that meant a little less lore.

Get this game, but only on sale.

After spending 127 hours in the Lands Between and achieving the platinum trophy, I can confidently say that Elden Ring is one of the best gaming experiences I’ve ever had.

FromSoftware managed to combine the challenging combat they are known for with the freedom of a vast open world. Exploration feels rewarding at every step – whether you stumble upon a hidden dungeon, a powerful weapon, or a completely unexpected boss fight. The sense of discovery is unmatched.

The combat is brutal but fair. Every death teaches you something, and the satisfaction of overcoming a tough enemy is unlike anything else. The variety of builds and playstyles also makes replayability huge, even after finishing the game.

Visually, the game is stunning, with breathtaking landscapes and hauntingly beautiful areas that make you want to stop and just look around. The soundtrack also perfectly captures the epic and dark atmosphere of the world.

Elden Ring is not a perfect game (some bosses can feel unbalanced, and performance could be better at times), but its strengths easily outweigh these small issues. It’s a masterpiece that rewards patience, curiosity, and determination.

If you enjoy challenging action RPGs and want to lose yourself in a world full of secrets, this is an absolute must-play.

10/10 – An unforgettable journey.
