Posted: September 20
(Long review, focuses on bad first then the good.)
I only got a PC recently after years of wanting one, and years of watching other people play games like Elden Ring. Im currently dithering around with the fire giant (i just dont have the patience right now to keep throwing myself at him).
But the second I got the game I went ham on it and got tired/confused after about 30 minutes; because knowing the lore, major bosses and world through youtube vids that have been edited and shortened don't prepare you.

I returned to it after a disappointed break, started a new playthrough and began learning and understanding stuff that is really easy to miss and get wrong. I think that inability to know what the hell is happening and where to go is what makes the game hard at first and puts people off.
I appreciate that people can feel coddled if there's a book long tutorial for every step, and they may want to take initiative to figure stuff out on their own, but having the CHOICE to be guided by more than just a ray of light and the vaguest possible directions from an NPC would be nice.

This leads me onto something else- despite the games major mechanics being easy to figure out within a patient hour or so of playing, there's still some lingering design choices that make me wonder what the living ♥♥♥♥ were they thinking.
Through the whole game, every single quest and NPC has been the most gatekeeping and vague individual on the planet. I don't really ask they spit their location as coordinates at you and hold your hand during their quests but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.. 50% of the time they don't give you any warning, they just disappear with NO mention of where to find them next (and then you go about your life and manage to lock yourself out of progressing the quest by doing someone mundane). 30% of the time they just won't progress dialogue until you do SOMETHING? that they never tell you what. And then 15% of the time they give you a very vague indication of where they're going, which usually just about suffices, and the last 5% is the golden few that actually tell you where they'll be.
Its frustrating to be locked out of quests in an instant, especially when it appears some of them interlink, so one step forwards in someone's quest could throw a wrench in someone else's. And i hate having to go google where an NPC will be next, and then find out they're halfway across the map under a random rock that you're expected to find organically.
A quest guide available with adequate spoiler prevention would be amazing, just so that if you reload an area and an NPC disappears, you can see where they are next, or see what item you need to progress, or simply the general area the next step takes place in.
Though luckily, vital 'quests' or storylines are handed to you like with Melina, the others you can just about forgive for being horrifically confusing as they're not reallyyyyyy essential, but its sad to miss out on loot, armour and the lives of interesting NPCs.

One thing I find that still confuses me somewhat is the approach on combat. The hitboxes (aside from silly glitches) are impressively good and I have no issue with, the attacks feel smooth but not too lightweight like in older fantasy games.
My first issue is how many times you click, your character is going to do that exact number NO MATTER WHAT. That sounds normal and pretty grass is green, but when fighting, if you missclick once or misjudge an attack opening, even BETWEEN those three attacks, you cannot break it to roll/block/heal.
I'm not asking to be able to roll mid swing, that's illogical, but if you click three times on accident, after the second hit, you cannot roll or move. I'd love for there to be a window, can be tiny, between the attacks you've clicked out, so if you realise you don't have time, you can spam roll and break that sequence.
I'm aware people probably just view it as another obstacle that makes the game nice and difficult, but it doesnt feel rewarding if you have the reflexes to dodge or block a very quick attack, but instead die because there's no way a tarnished could roll just after an attack. It's like once you click that apparently cements your attack into the universe's divine fate, doesn't matter if you swung twice and there's a whole pause in the animation before the third swing, you're gonna do that third swing and get wiped.

Similar to this is how LONG it takes to drink tears and NOTICE YOU DON'T HAVE MANA/TEARS LEFT. Holy ♥♥♥♥. The fighting is so realistic, but a tarnished couldnt down a potion a TINY BIT FASTER against a DEMIGOD?? No urgency at all. I don't want an instant heal but good lord is whatever they've got going on rancidly SLOW.
Huh i've been stabbed, now i'm low. HMMM.. i could chug it down as fast as possible and then hurl myself at mohg... or even better :D i could stand up stock straight, stop moving aside from the slowest walk possible and take a nice refreshing swig from the flask! Yeah lets do that then get mauled.
Then if you're out of heals- hm! What's the tarnished going to do? Swing back the flask, taste nothing and toss is aside to go right back to fighting the omen king? NOPE! They shake it over their mouth... hmm nothing coming out >n<. They shake it out infront of them.. hmmm still nothing >o<? Peer inside briefly.. yeah nothing. Oh then a huge sword cleaves you in two.
I'm a mage build and I'm out of mana? So no spell came out my staff, hurry and drink cerulean tears for a year, get it over with before- oh no wait nvm lol :3. Lets stare at my ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ stick with the cognitive strength of a slug, turn it over a few times, give it a little shake and- oh yeah, now we can safely say i'm out of mana :O.

This became very bitter. All that aside i love the game so much, played it to the point when i closed my eyes i saw sites of grace and wind swept trees and then i made myself take a few days break from playing.
The customisation in your character and ability to change that whenever you want at the roundtable is golden, the armour sets and weapon types available are to die for, the map's design is outstanding and the environment is stunning.
Progressing feels iffy at first, rune collection is slow, especially on your first playthrough, but it evens out quickly and soon you almost forget to focus so much on runes and begin to just play your heart out and all the runes and levelling is just background.
Getting the hang of fighting is just so energising and you can almost never be bored. I did complain about the combat mechanics earlier, but despite all that, i still live with it and loved fighting almost every boss (except the draconic tree sentinel).
The characters stories are just so tragic. I'm a sucker for any media that touches on the concept of family, and more 'medieval' settings always carry strong conflicts surrounding duty, loyalty and obligation that interfere with family. And you get LOTS. Rya and Tanith, Ranni and her mother and brothers, Marika and all her children, Malenia and Miquella. Most of this is seen in paintings and brief voicelines, or if your lucky a cutscene, but it feels real and emotional all the same.
I also wish we got more direct lore about Marika, we have gathered quite a lot about her and her confusing family tree, but being able to see that in action, as cutscenes or more paintings, ugh I'd love it.
I'm going to get the DLC, but there's no DLC i'd want more than a DLC or spin off of elden ring that perhaps was more story or NPC focussed, set when the golden order was in its prime. I just think the complicated story is such a strength, and even though the mystery around it makes it very cool, expanding on it would be incredible.

My love for the game is less specific and picky than what i complained about, because i love the vast majority of it- its just those little aspects that don't ruin the game, but are worth mentioning in a review and piss me off from time to time.

Elden Ring was my first Fromsoft game. Getting to 100% took complete focus and dedication to improvement in a world that was new and unfamiliar to me. This was a tremendous journey in every sense, and it felt like a single player tabletop RPG campaign from a truly inspired and imaginative GM come to life. The boss fights are spectacular, grandiose affairs with multiple phases and fair mechanics that encourage tempo, observation and patience in equal measure. Exploring the grand over-world allowed for the discovery of innumerable secrets. This game makes lasting memories for those who put in the time to appreciate it. Crafting even felt cozy and relaxing amidst the storm of traversal and epic combat.

I can offer my heartiest recommendation after 200+ hours in the Lands Between, yet moreover, I was inspired by the pure fun & challenges here to go back and start fresh in Dark Souls. Why did I wait so long?
Fromsoft has made a loyal fan of me indeed.

The open world is massive, mysterious, and rewarding. Every detour off the beaten path leads to something meaningful—hidden bosses, rare loot, underground cities, or even entire new regions. The map design is brilliant, and with Torrent’s double jump, you’ll be scaling cliffs and rooftops like a parkour god.
Combat is wildly flexible. Whether you’re a bleed build berserker, a spell-slinging sorcerer, a parry master, or a spirit-summoning tactician, the game lets you play your way. Ashes of War, Spirit Summons, weapon upgrades—every system feels like it’s saying, “You don’t have to be strong, but you better be smart.” And every boss fight feels like a mythic showdown, where victory is earned, not given.
Visually, the game is breathtaking. The glowing Erdtree, the rotting red wastelands of Caelid, the starry depths of Nokron—every location is a concept art come to life. Even without dialogue, the environmental storytelling is so rich you feel the history bleeding from every ruin.

Posted: September 18
Elden Ring (+DLC)
[no spoilers] [soulslike enjoyer — 1k+ hours]


Combat

On the player’s side, things are looking good. There’s a huge variety of weapons, spells, incantations, and summons to choose from. Weapons have Ashes of War, weapon arts, and can be enhanced with additional status effects. The only notable issue is with the hitboxes of small weapons, which can often miss at point-blank range (e.g., Star Fists).

For less experienced players, there are many mechanics that can make fights easier or even trivial. The “horse” system allows certain fights to be done mounted, but this often boils down to circling the boss and striking from behind, which trivializes the encounter.

When it comes to bosses, there are two categories: Remembrance bosses and everything else. For the first group things are excellent. They are varied, with deep movesets, and fights are fast and dynamic (especially the DLC bosses). New mechanics like input reading (fits the genre well) and chain damage (getting hit by the first strike guarantees follow-up hits, often resulting in death) work well, in my opinion. Delayed attacks are also present to counter players who rely on reflexes - fair enough.

On the downside, many encounters are highly choreographed, with bosses frequently backing away to launch ranged attacks, forcing unavoidable dodge phases. There are also many AoE attacks and status-based effects, which can introduce “RNG moments” - something I personally dislike in a soulslike. Overall, there’s too much reliance on cheap tricks to inflate difficulty, making some fights unsatisfying.

The second boss category tends to feature repetitive encounters, e.g., in dungeons. These bosses have small movesets, are quite easy, and appear far too often. Even if the first encounter was decent, subsequent ones are usually disappointing.


Pacing

The base game has a steady pacing, generally relaxed and non-pressuring. The DLC, however, is much more dynamic, with a clear sense of rising intensity as you progress through its areas.

Shifting from interconnected but confined maps to a fully open world changes the tension curve of traditional soulslikes. In Dark Souls, moving into a new area meant a sustained exposure to danger; here, the horse and open terrain mean that most threats can be bypassed. This grants the player unprecedented freedom, but sometimes it dilutes the sense of dread and narrative weight that the genre is known for.


Mechanics

Classic character progression done properly, with clear soft and hard caps. Weapon upgrades use smithing stones, and most types can be unlocked at merchants, giving plenty of room to experiment. Ashes of War and status effects can be applied to weapons, changing scaling as well. Crafting is available and can be quite useful in certain fights.

The economy is based mainly on runes as a universal currency and rare materials for upgrades. Initially, runes are valuable and require careful balancing between character development and equipment upgrades. However, in the middle and late stages of the game, an “inflation” effect occurs - there are so many runes that the risk of losing them ceases to be painful, and purchasing decisions become automatic.

The basic loop - exploration → combat → progression → unlocking new areas - is engaging in the first run thanks to novelty and discovery. However, in NG+ or after long play sessions, exploration loses its freshness, and the loop compresses into targeted boss runs or rune farming. This is natural for an RPG, but in Elden Ring the shift happens faster than in more tightly designed soulslikes.


World Design & Exploration

An open world is new for FromSoftware. Credit where it’s due - map fragments must be discovered before use, and even once found, unexplored areas remain hidden until visited. This adds immersion and encourages active exploration.

Both the base game and DLC feature massive worlds with plenty to see. Having a horse changes the feel - exploration is closer to Skyrim, without the constant tension you’d get exploring an unknown path in Dark Souls. Enemies have short leash ranges and can’t keep up with the horse, which reduces danger (I’d personally prefer a light sense of threat).

Level design is excellent, with great enemy placement, verticality, and logical world structure. Rewards for exploration, however, are underwhelming, making the world a “one-and-done” experience. The biggest letdown is the dungeons: they’re plentiful but boring, with cheap difficulty tricks, weak bosses, and poor rewards.

The risk reward system is uneven. In many places, taking greater risks (descending into hard-to-reach locations, fighting a strong opponent while exploring) does not bring rewards directly proportional to the effort put in. There are moments when the risk is satisfactorily rewarded with unique equipment or lore, but just as often it is simply upgrade material or a single-use item. As a result, in the later stages of the game, the player naturally begins to ignore some of the challenges and focuses on “sure” sources of rewards, which slightly weakens the desire for full exploration.

As in many soulslike games, the late-game and DLC areas are often rushed past because enemies are over-scaled and not worth fighting.


Story & NPCs

The story is strange. I enjoy dark fantasy, but here it didn’t fully pull me in. FromSoftware feels slightly weaker in this regard compared to past works (too many elements crammed together without perfect coherence). Still, the lore is dense, most characters are deeply rooted in the setting, and their placement makes narrative sense. There are many side quests - complex, well thought-out, and offering worthwhile rewards.


Difficulty

The game introduces the basics well, but in the middle of the world, there is a sharp difficulty threshold where you need to master mechanics such as iframe windows, reading dragged animations, and controlling stamina in longer battles. The problem is that at times, new mechanics are not “taught” by the level design, but suddenly enforced by a boss or enemy, which can give the impression of an artificial jump in difficulty. The DLC introduces a few such moments, but there they are more telegraphic and fit into the natural development of the player's skills. The base game offers a balanced challenge - experienced players will find it satisfying, and newcomers have enough tools to make it manageable. The DLC is aimed at advanced players, with demanding but enjoyable fights (more so than in the base game). The downside is that some difficulty design relies on cheap tricks that lower the satisfaction of victory.


Replayability

The classic RPG structure offers lots of replay value, with many builds and weapons to try. While future runs will likely skip exploration and the DLC, you can still get a dozen or so unique playthroughs out of it.


Performance

Stable framerate with no major issues or bugs (DLC had FPS drops at launch, but they were patched). Smooth throughout the playthrough.

Overall: 8.4/10

It’s been 3 years since the game released and a year after the only DLC ever released for this game and as someone who played his first souls game I can speak for a lot of individuals who might still be interested but also not sure about this game.

Everything I’m about to share is my personal experience.

I bought the game on release all because of my friend insisting in playing together (co op mod and summons) and because he loves souls games.

I spent an hour, I got off, never touched it again until late 2023.
I hopped on all by myself, walked around Limegrave for an hour or so exploring trying to the fight the first little boss riding in a horse.. I got annoyed by how strong and easy was for him to kill me, I gave up.

I went back in to play a few days later after watching a few videos, ever since that day. I began to explore, learn from my recent deaths and trying to achieve the title of “Elden Lord”.

I just finally understood the whole Lore and story behind it.

Fast Forward to today, I have began to collect all achievements and almost done with my first NG+ and began a new game in my PlayStation because my wife saw me playing the game and wanted to try.

That’s how deep the game got in me and now after defeating Consort Radahn, I felt something I haven’t felt in a long time, something I used to feel after completing a game growing up with my uncle.

I recommend this game to everyone, even if you never play a souls game, Elden Ring is a great game to teach you how to play and give you the tools to be better.

Yes, you will die a lot, and sometimes won’t even know or understand how did you die, but at the end of your journey, you will become the Elden Lord.

Posted: September 16
Absolutely disappointing…

I've never played a Souls game in my life. From that you might draw what my perspective is immediately.

Here are my main gripes with the game:

1: The progression:

When you first start out it's obvious this game is harder than you think (as a new player) but the degrees to which it makes your life difficult are a little unorthodox.

Mechanics like stamina drain for dodging and blocking make your life hard for no reason, never giving you room to ever regain ground during a fight, while your enemies (who have infinite stamina) constantly barrage you with 3 hit attacks that are sure to hit. It would take HOURS just to get the hang of one enemy, so imagine getting used to HUNDREDS of different types of enemies, it's absolutely ridiculous.

If the game would’ve shown you the way up to the first boss, letting you semi explore/semi show you the way through a preliminary open world so you can get the hang of the rest of the game, that would've been more helpful. Instead, I get absolutely r@ped by the first boss (an obvious indicator I need to level up/ practice a little more) so I explore for hours only to find nothing but a couple of minibosses I'm not even able to fight…

So I pulled out a last resort.

I looked up a guide online telling me I need better weapons and items (obviously), but in order to get those, I need to kill a Miniboss that takes hours to get the hang of as well. Not to mention armor and other tools that I don't know where to look, and don't feel like looking for because ALL I WANT TO DO IS KILL THE BOSS.


2: Open world

Open world interactions are no better, with all of them just summing up to 2 things:

Random npc you can press E to talk to, but is absolutely useless. (except for merchants who sell ♥♥♥♥♥♥ items for gucci level prices)
Random Enemy that locks on to you even when you’re not looking from a mile away.

There's nothing creative about open world interactions other than those 2, which really discourages me from exploring further. There's nothing interesting about the world itself either, it's just a bunch of castles, forests, beaches and swamps.

No interesting interactions, no random events. Just a bunch of OP enemies waiting to kill you.

3: Builds: I've never been a build game type of guy, so I'm a little biased when I say I didn't like this game's build system whatsoever. I kinda get how it works? I get you need to use runes to allocate stat points. But why is there like 4 classes that use melee? Are they different somehow? The same? Well the game doesnt f%cking tell you so whatever I guess. In fact the game doesn't tell you sh!t. It tells you you need gold seeds for more flasks, but never tells you the requirements. It kicks your ass in bossfights, but never tells you you need stronger equipment. It tells you you need to allocate your stat points but doesn't tell you where to do it depending on build. Only to find out later on that build doesn't even matter and you can just reset later in the game…


Conclusion:

I get that this game is a hardcore game, but it does it (In MY opinion) in the worst ways possible…

Expecting your playerbase to explore in a world that hates them, filled ONLY with overpowered creatures and the like that want to kill them is a recipe for disaster in an open world game.

Instead of the world being your oyster, it is just a giant mouth that wants to eat you alive.

I tried so hard to like this game, but everytime I try it almost feels degrading how I get my ass kicked just for exploring, only to run away like a little ♥♥♥♥♥ and never want go to that area again.

Or when I find a cool miniboss, only to never wanting to meet it again after getting killed like 50 times (not an exaggeration btw)

Every time I play this game my mood just declines like a rock falling to the ground. Constantly degrading you telling you "you're not good enough” or “stop your foolish ambition” (direct quote from the first boss lol)

So sorry if I don't want to play a game that ruins my mood, wastes my time, and feels like I'm going nowhere. Yall may be into that self degrading sh!t but I’m not.

I've played and completed a lot of games, RDR2, The COD Campaigns, Wolfenstein, Chrono Trigger, Super punch out, super mario bros wii, Shadow of war/ shadow of Mordor, Assassins creed and others. and a lot of different titles.

Posted: September 15
Promised Consort Radahn: The Cosmic Red Flag

This isn’t a boss fight—it’s a hostile workplace.
Radahn shows up like gravity itself took steroids and decided to ruin your weekend.
Phase One is basically “welcome to space dodgeball,” except the ball is a miniature moon and you are the ball.

Then Phase Two begins and the arena turns into a physics TED Talk nobody asked for.
He teleports like a drunk comet, snap-quickswaps between ballet twirls and black-hole slams, and somehow rides a horse the size of a garden gnome while weighing roughly three freight trains.
Hitboxes? Pure fan fiction.
Camera? Laughs in cosmic horror.

You can’t “learn the patterns,” because there are none—just a randomised PowerPoint of pain.
The soundtrack could be angels singing or a car alarm for all you’ll hear over your own screaming.

The only reason I survived was hiding behind a Fingerprint Shield like a panicked armadillo and poking him with the Bloody Helice until shame did what skill could not.
That’s not a victory; that’s a cheese-flavoured war crime.

FromSoftware calls this “challenging.”
I call it Elden Ring’s HR violation.
11/10—would get suplexed by a gravitational meatball again, but only out of spite.

Been a fan of Souls since Demon’s Souls back in 2009 and Elden Ring is, to the surprise of next to no one given its widespread popularity, is the best game that From Soft has delivered upon its release. It may not be the best in every single aspect of their prior games, but it’s the best package as a whole and is an incredible journey to experience.

Pros:
- Build Variety
- World Design
- Legacy Dungeon Design
- Boss Design
- Exploration
- Shadow of the Erdtree Expansion
- Soundtrack

Mixed:
- PvP System
- Replayability

Cons:
- Pacing can drag near the end
- Too many side dungeons
- Some character quests can be too hidden

Elden Ring does so many things well. Exploration, it’s huge amount of good weapon variety, excellent art direction, tons of interesting areas and side content (arguably too many side areas), boss design, and interesting lore. The list can go on but these are the things that stood out to me. Something that particularly stands out, similar to Dark Souls 2 and Demon’s Souls, you’re able to ‘pick’ your own difficulty essentially due to so many options being available to lower it: travel to different areas to level or upgrade your weapons, use NPC or player summons, use spirit ashes, choosing a worse weapon in your kit than the best one, choosing to buff, and maybe a bit more. I’m a hardcore Souls player but I still appreciate what is essentially accessibility options for those who just want to beat the game. In addition, the Shadow of the Erdtree expansion is top-notch as it’s essentially more Elden Ring with more brutal bosses and a smart way to handle everyone’s levels (Scadutree Blessings).

There are some minor nitpicks I have like no way to fight bosses after beating them again and how online invasions work but it’s not that big of a deal. Running through a NG+ run when you’re on horseback 70% of the time isn’t exactly fun just to fight a boss again so I wish they’d have implemented some sort of boss rush mode like Sekiro or bonfire ascetics from DS2. Getting ganked is fairly common online when invading so I kinda prefer the other souls games in how it works.

As for issues, there are some which ultimately boil down to the game can be too long. I tend to explore anywhere and everywhere and I clocked in over 100 hours on my first playthrough on console hugging walls and everything and now on PC, knowing where all dungeons and such were, it still took me about 80 hours or so to do those dungeons and as many character quests as I could. I think if the game had reduced the side content by 30% or so, it would be a tad better. Replayability can be an issue to me as well as due to its open-world nature, it can be tedious to have to recover flask upgrades, smithing stones, any gear you want for your build over a more linear game like all of their previous entries. As for the dlc criticism, one thing I disliked about it is From Software’s need to make bosses absolutely brutal. I like difficult bosses although there comes a point where after fighting so many, it just becomes tedious and that feeling did hit me during the expansion.

Overall, Elden Ring is the culmination of everything the Souls series had lead to up until its release. ER takes elements of all the past souls games: the level + world variety of Demon’s Souls, the clever and sometimes looping level + world design of Dark Souls 1, the vast build variety and adventurousness of Dark Souls 2, the unsettling boss designs and lore elements of Bloodborne, and the general fast-paced, fluid combat of Dark Souls 3, and the verticality of Sekiro. While it’s not perfect based on my criticisms, it’s close to it and it stands at the top above the rest of Souls genre overall. 10/10.

Posted: September 15
I rushed the game and DLC cuz I already 100%'d the game on XBOX w 300+hours

One of the most aethetically pleasing games to ever exist, a true visual delicacy, a humanity heritage.
The world is a wonder to explore, doesn't feel empty and it is a joy to visit every single corner

Gameplay-wise it's the same Dark Souls formula but now in open world, the enemy variety is good enough and it's fair with the player, it's easier than previous From Software games due to the spirit summons and the fact that, being an open-world game, if you get stuck at one part you can go to another without problem and come back later, you can summon random people or friends with the risk of being invaded and the cost of enemies being tougher (unless you summon a high lvl friend and cheese the game which makes you skip the joy of overcoming difficulties in this type of games and loses the entire point of it)

The music in this game is astonishing, really sets the setting, I even listen to it sometimes on music streaming platforms.

My only gripes with this game is comparing it to DARK SOULS, while this game is definitely more polished and reflects the experience FROM SOFTWARE got from previous entries, MOST bosses aren't as "memorable" as previous games, while visually they are beautiful, the fights themselves aren't as memorable as for example SISTER FRIEDE, ORNSTEIN N SMOUGH, GAEL, PINWHEEL jk, to name a few from previous "souls" entries. The best ones in ELDEN RING could be DRAGONLORD PLASCIDUSAX, RADAHN (but without the summons is kinda meh, the point of the fight is that is a FESTIVAL and YOU HAVE to summon the rest, which by default makes it easier), MOHG, RENALA and MALIKETH, maybe MALENIA but in my personal opinion the ones I mentioned are way better, also final boss spoiler: .

Also while I said the OST in this game is phenomenal and indeed it is, the songs for the bosses themselves are VERY forgettable, I finished the game like 5+ times already and none of them sticked to me, with the exception of MOHG, the GODSKIN APOSTLES and obviously the FINAL BOSS, maybe it's just a matter of taste but I think i'm not the only one thinking the same.

Gotta mention the PVP which is very entertaining even with its jankiness, unless you get invaded by a "no-lifer" tryhard using RIVERS OF BLOOD and VEILMOON because it's the most broken stuff. The character customization is very good and you can spend hours making funny characters, I remember there was a fightclub at the starting zone and you could see funny stuff like the ninja turtles or homer simpson, just having fun with the PVP.
By the way the crafting itself is very "useless" and you can play the entire gaming without touching it, I hope they can make it better in future games and make the player feel the need to really use it.

Overall a MUST PLAY title you must experience at least once. Except the DLC but that's a review for another time, and nightreign but that's just a cashgrab title. GG

Great game in many respects (lore, environment, story, etc), but has a huge problem with with aspects of the difficulty. Not about it being too hard, but too unfair. There are certain enemies can just manage to hit you once, and then stun lock you into death or just hit you multiple times while you are recovering to kill you. Or, control inputs not registering because you were in a stun lock animation, while other inputs inexplicably seem to queue up, leading to something like an attack you input while in an animation take priority over something else you input near the end of the animation.
Other frustrations are having a long epic battle with a dragon, only to have the dragon fly off a cliff and get stuck right before it was going to die, with no way to hit it anymore from that far away.
Meanwhile, on the other end of things, the randomness of attacks means that a hard boss can suddenly become a cakewalk because it decided to spam some useless attack 5 times in a row.
Also, while it is mostly stable, I am not a fan of the random crashes, or booting you out because their network went down.

Posted: September 13
This game is a masterpiece, honestly. I don’t even know why I’m still keeping myself from purchasing the DLC—it feels like the DLC is calling me, like Green Goblin’s mask at this point.

This was my first Souls-like game ever, and I nearly gave up in the first 15 to 25 minutes of playing because of the freaking Tree Sentinel. Bro absolutely destroyed me so badly that I thought I’d never be able to finish this game. It was crazy. But when I upgraded my gear, fought him, and finally won, it felt like pure ecstasy. In that moment, I got hooked on Souls-like games.

Elden Ring single-handedly made me fall in love with a genre I thought I’d never enjoy in my life. I’ve poured 175 hours into it, fought Malenia, Blade of Miquella countless times, and managed to get every possible ending. I’ve played the game multiple times from start to finish—which was brutal but also so rewarding—and I even earned every achievement. I felt so proud of myself. Now, I want to try Dark Souls, specifically Dark Souls 3, and I really hope it’s just like Elden Ring.

I also played Black Myth: Wukong recently, which was my second Souls-like game, and it was a masterpiece too. I highly recommend it if you loved Elden Ring as much as I did.

So yeah, it was like a drug—I just wanted more. More challenge, more bosses to defeat. It was the best. Definitely buy this game. You won’t regret it.

Posted: September 13
I tried. I really tried to enjoy this game. Buckle up. I have some thoughts. No story spoilers here.

Let's start with basic ratings that I can give a number.
Pros: | Graphics 8/10 | Visual Design 10/10 | World-building 10/10 | Music 10/10 |
Cons: | Audio Design 4/10 |

Elden Ring does not reward progression or skill (in the normal gaming way). Every game has obstacles that can be bested either with some progression or skill-based learning of the game mechanics. Yet, as I’ve progressed and defeated various non-boss enemies, explored, retrieved armor and weapons, and leveled significantly, no skill or progression has prepared me for the first boss (if you follow the guided path). There is no enemy like this one leading up to this obstacle, and the attacks are so designed to be unrelenting and difficult that no amount of traditional gaming skill could allow the player to beat this boss without knowledge of the attacks. In fact, the only thing that will get you past the obstacle is memorization. Not skill. Not levels. Not anything enjoyable (imo). Just memorization of the attacks, animations, exact timings, and eventually executing this idealistically after many attempts. This is the uniqueness of FromSoftware games; the development of a niche "skill" to pass an obstacle, never to be used again in any other instance. Allow suffering to consume you and master it to move forward. It can be a great life lesson... or a source of unneeded stress for those who've learned this lesson time and time again, only to repeat it and suffer more.

For that reason, despite the beauty of the world, the incredible monster designs, and world building, Elden Ring is not a masterpiece in my eyes, and I find no satisfaction in playing. I simply do not have the level of masochism required to mistake the end of suffering with true joy and mastery. Back when I beat Bloodborne, I was happy to confuse the two. However, I am no longer one who avoids reprieve in their limited free time dedicated to a game they bought to, in one way or another, escape suffering. If I wanted to spend my time memorizing to progress, I would do so outside of a video game that breathes more stress and anxiety into a sleepless, loud world, echoing the oppression of peace of mind and creativity that has invaded the entertainment industry.

And, look, this may be a masterpiece for others. I have no doubt that if you have the willingness to endure, and the persistence to recognize patterns, memorize them, and engage in patient execution, you will beat this game, and perhaps you will love it. But, for me, it is the illusion of skill and the gate-keeping of beauty behind intense suffering that, after beating the first boss 12 hours in, only brings me relief from my self-prescribed suffering in the form of a game titled Elden Ring.

Elden Ring brings so much to the table that the table almost breaks.

Elden Ring, the seventh scion of the noble Soulsborne house, has quite a legacy to live up to. As the popular siblings tend to do, it surpasses all these expectations with seeming ease and looks splendid doing it, too.

That is not a metaphor. The production values of this game are through the roof, as everyone stepping even one foot into the Lands Between will attest. The Great Outdoors greets its intrepid explorers with incredibly lush vegetation, a masterpiece of a sky box, majestic mountains, foreboding castles and a golden haze of light beatifying everything. Where earlier Soulsborne titles used to trap their players in incredibly intricate labyrinths, Elden Ring sets you loose in a gigantic open world and tells you to go nuts. Every single place you can see on the horizon you can set a food in, and in a few other places you would never suspect are there besides. Just take note of the golden knight over there. He will in all probability cut you down a few times, just to start things off on the right foot.

Whether you make your first experience in combat with him or the much softer targets beyond him, combat feels very familiar to a Dark Souls 3 veteran. A couple of things have been added: A jump button complete with jump attacks, a horse to get around on and engage in mounted combat, and probably the most consequential addition: Spirit summons, the ability to conjure up an NPC to fight with you in certain situations, e. g. boss fights.

When encountering this feature, the brows of an experienced Dark Souls player furrow. Won’t this mess with the hallowed Difficulty, make things way too easy?

To my mind, it does only a little. Elden Ring is an appropriately harsh game: If you play it like a traditional Souls game, it will send you to the death screen quite often. But it is the kind of dominatrix that really is in it mainly for your enjoyment: The kind who always makes sure that the straps have cushy insides, who always sets down a pillow under the pillory, who always listens an extra second for your safe word before proceeding. Whether it is a particularly difficult constellation of regular enemies or one of the dozens of stunning and exciting boss fights that have you stumped, somewhere out there is a spell, a weapon or a spirit companion that will help you jump that hurdle and still feel like a bad-ass for having done so. Or you can do it the old-fashioned way and just bang your head against the wall until the wall is reduced to rubble. You do you. Just one problem: If you do want that vintage Dark Souls experience, you have to resist the siren call of the spirit summons, not just once, but every time a boss has struck you down with only one percent of health remaining. That might be a tall order.

But all this quibbling about difficulty aside, combat is still an eternal font of enjoyment. Squaring off against a Cleanrot Knight is just as enjoyable in hour 200 as fighting a lowly foot soldier was in hour 1. The core gameplay loop of Elden Ring is second to none.

One area where there has been remarkable progress in the Soulsborne franchise is the story telling. That doesn’t mean that the plot told is any less cryptic than before, but it is told in considerably less impenetrable fashion. There are quest chains that one might even be able to solve without resorting to the wiki! Some of them are quite engaging too, even if most of them end up on the grimdark side of the spectrum. So the plot department hasn't exactly delivered a diamond of storytelling, but they have polished their part of the grand opus a lot more than they used to do.

Still, the story isn’t the draw of a Soulsborne game, and Elden Ring is no exception. Fighting for your life in spectacular yet grounded fashion in high stakes high difficulty duels with superbly animated foes in front of spectacularly beautiful vistas is. In these regards, Elden Ring delivers in spades. So much so in fact that it was almost too much for me. The Lands Between are vast, and if you truly mean to see and do everything, you are looking at a 200 hour time commitment, at least. That amount of time can be hard to come by, and sustaining sufficient amounts of enthusiasm for this long a time can be a challenge, no matter how enticing the game might be.

But whatever amount of time you choose to let Elden Ring embrace you, you will have an excellent experience full of wonder, existential dread, mortal danger, frustration, elation, and always a new thing to discover right around the corner. Every session of Elden Ring truly feels like an adventure.

TLDR: Elden Ring is the pinnacle of polish in the Soulslike genre: welcoming to all players, appropriately harsh to the fans. Almost too much of a game, maybe, but an excellent experience for whatever amount of time you choose to give it.

A Beautiful F***ing Nightmare

Elden Ring kicked my ass more times than I can count — and I loved every second of it. Every boss felt like a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ wall, but the thrill of finally smashing through them was pure f**ing ecstasy. Holy sht, nothing else in gaming compares.

The Lands Between sucked me in completely. Every corner had some twisted secret, every path had some bastard waiting to ruin my day, and I couldn’t stop exploring. Even when I was pissed, I kept pushing, because the game makes pain addictive.

And the ending? I went full Frenzied Flame. Watching the world burn in yellow chaos while my Tarnished became a walking apocalypse — chef’s kiss. After all the blood, sweat, and swearing, that felt like the perfect “f*** you” to everything in that cursed land.

This isn’t just a game, it’s an experience. Brutal, cruel, and absolutely unforgettable. 10/10 would set the world on fire again.

Do yourself a favor, play Elden Ring. It is the masterpiece that people are saying it is, more a experience than a game.

This is my first, and probably last time I am writing a review for a game, but there has been none that deserved it more so far.

Elden ring has artistic environments, cinematic fights and epic music. It has what so many games lack: an atmosphere. And Elden Ring gives you back what you put in. If you care to learn and engage in the systems that are available to you, you will find the world your playground, with every new challenge not something to whine and sweat about, beating your head bloody on that seemingly invincible wall, but instead growing with every challenge, endorphine flooding your system when you finally bring your foe down. And you will get better, you will get to a point where you anticipate enemy attacks even thou you never fought them before. The game rewards exploration. You want to push through the main game as fast as possible, not bothering with nonsensical things like lore, npcs, quests or the myriad of hidden things all over? You can do that. It will be more challenging, it will be shorter, alot of things won't make sense to you. But still it is fair and very doable. You are like me, and like to look in every corner of every map to see how creative level design can be? Then you are in heaven.

For short context: I play video games for about 30 years now. I hate the level / map "design" that near all of games use: That of a tube. Games have become tubes for rats to run in. But I want mazes to explore with a sense of wonder. The first positive exception in the modern gaming world was the original World of Warcraft. How did my undead holy priest love to gather 4 other clueless people to enter that dungeon and find out what that other route held for us in Blackrock Depths... Well it was very good times for me, but it didn't last (not even the first Addon, but also wikis were "invented"). And then in 2024, everyone with more sense than me was already playing the newly released Elden Ring DLC, I finally took the plunge and tried Dark Souls 1 Remastered, a random comment had me convinced I could participate in the series with mouse and keyboard as well. I thank that comment so very much (It is so very true. As a keyboard and mouse player I still can't fathom how controler players manage, not in any shooter, but not even in this game. Camera control is everything to look up + down, to soak in all the beauty... and find those hidden treasures.) Dark Souls 1 has the best level design I have experienced in a game so far (so looking forward to playing part 2+3...) and with Elden Ring FromSoftware does not give you the same amount at the same time, but actually alot more of it, split up in portions, to a point where the open map demands the horse to find every secret dungeon hidden below that cliff over there... It is not necessary to find everything, infact the game gives you so much content you can very easily "overlevel" early on and find a good portion of the game to be not very demanding. It took me way over 200h, I tried to look everywhere, to find everything, to gather everything, I still missed a big boss (I knew existed) and a dungeon. I went in completely blind (I STRONGLY advise you to do the same), with the exception of looking up core mechanics in wikis. (Like what stat actually does what, what poise means etc. Some people will need wikis for NPC quests) I took advantage of everything the game offered and at halfway I realized it was not very demanding and stopped using a certain mechanic with a certain something I found down there, under the horror and beauty of a false night sky...

I could go on praising this game, but I will stop here. There is no need to be afraid of the game's difficulty, it is indeed the best game design choice, the higher challenge offering higher satisfaction in the end. You can grow, you can learn, and like in real life, perseverance will be rewarded. But if you ignored everything, upgraded nothing, explored nothing, learned nothing, went in with your naked ass, armed in ignorance and thought this is a hack and slash arcade game where victory is decided by clicks / second? You will find the hard game you were looking for. You will not become Lord unless you adapt, Tarnished.


Do yourself a favor, play Elden Ring.

---

That being said, if you decide to take the journey, From Software games do not hold your hand, to a point it borders on crime, so here a few tips to get you going:

Character class does not matter. Vagabond might be the best for new players, simply because of the heaviest armor.
Do the Tutorial Cave. Read the few lines of texts the developers gave us to explain their advanced combat system. There are many ways to play the game. In general, read the few pop up texts you get (the only times the game is paused).

For keyboard: bind dodge to space, I also recommend left mb - light attack, right mb - heavy attack. How lock-on and camera works can be changed in the settings. Especially the camera settings changed alot for me. Fiddle with them.

Learn to play with lock-on and without. Some situations can be easier without lock-on (also better directional control of your character). Some big bosses demand lock-on so you keep them on your screen and can react to their animations. The best direction to roll is usually towards bosses rather than away. If you have no lock-on you can roll right towards and past them to get into their back.

You can evade many attacks by simply strafing. Some attacks are way easier to evade by jumping over them instead of dodging.

Concerning the dodge roll: The dodge roll is activated not by PRESSING the button but when RELEASING IT. I died so many times in the start because I kept pressing the dodge roll button for half a second to a second and wondering why I was only getting smashed in the face instead of dodging. So if you miss your dodges or you feel like your character doesn't even dodge, check yourself if you are not like me and just didn't release the button but kept it pressing.

Aggression is rewarded. Stupid aggression (aka buttonsmashing) is punished. Elden Ring is a game of patience (watch your stamina bar), timing and spacing. Faster weapons have a easier time finding windows of opportunity to get a punish free hit in on the enemy, but with lower damage and shorter range (spacing) than their bigger variants. Better armor / higher Poise allows to brute force certain enemies up to a point (It is always better to dodge the hit and not have to heal afterwards, but if your big stick is big enough you can stagger enemies with it).

Stay in the starting areas, the first thing you want to level is vigor to 40. Try out different weapons, to find what you like. There are TONS of options. Even if weapons are in the same weapon class they can have different movesets. They actively change the way you play the game (as do the skills you decide to put on them). If you miss a few points in strength or dexterity to wield them, then level them to the minimum requirement to use the weapon. (Two-handing any weapon gives you a 50% boost on your strength). Level your vigor to 40.
Eventually you have to find "your" weapon. Upgrade it. In the starting areas any un-upgraded weapon will do. ANY weapon will do to beat the game with it. Your starter weapon, good enough to slay a god. But then, again, when you found the one you like, upgrade it. Level Vigor to 40. You want 60 after you have reached 40, but at that point you also invest into your main stat(s), and endurance for heavier armor / more stamina.

Armor matters very much, with stacking damage reduction from multiple sources making a very real difference, please don't try to play FashionSouls/Glasscannons to the point you get frustrated. You never want to fat roll (=heavy equipment load).

Do explore, read and enjoy.

I'll keep this short.

To be fair, you have to have a very high IQ to understand and "get good" at Elden Ring. The story is extremely subtle, and without a solid grasp of developer sadism most of the game will go over a typical player's head. There's also the overall nihilistic outlook, which is deftly woven into the characterizations - the developer's personal philosophy draws heavily from Narodnaya Volya literature, for instance. The fans understand this stuff; they have the intellectual capacity and genuine skill to truly appreciate the depths of this game and story, to realize that they're not just masochists with a huge pile of time to burn, doing the same repetitive tasks over and over - they also say something deep about pride, arrogance, and shame.

As a consequence, people who dislike Elden Ring truly ARE idiots and bad at games generally - of course they wouldn't appreciate, for instance, the complexity of weapons passing through the floors, mountains, castle walls, which themselves are cryptic references to Kafka's . I'm smirking right now just imagining one of those addlepated simpletons scratching their heads in confusion as Miyazaki's genius unfolds itself on their monitors. What fools... how I pity them. 😂 And yes by the way, I DO have a "Try Finger But Hole" tattoo on the small of my back. And no, you cannot see it. It's for the ladies' eyes only - And even they have to demonstrate that they're within 5 IQ points of my own (preferably lower) and have completed a No-Hit Consort Radan run (with only a Lvl 1 Dagger) beforehand.

It truly is a game of a year.

The Lands Between is one of the most immersive and hauntingly beautiful worlds I've ever explored in a game. Every hilltop, ruin, and forest feels hand-crafted, hiding secrets, deadly enemies, or cryptic lore. Unlike many open-world games, Elden Ring doesn't hold your hand. There's no quest marker telling you exactly where to go; instead, curiosity and risk are constantly rewarded.

Combat is satisfying, and endlessly customizable. Whether you’re a sword-wielding warrior, a stealthy assassin, or a powerful mage, the game offers a vast array of builds and weapons to support a wide variety of playstyles. Boss battles are intense, often brutally difficult, but always fair — victory feels earned, and every defeat teaches a lesson.

The story is subtle and mysterious, told through item descriptions, environmental storytelling, and sparse NPC interactions. It’s not a traditional narrative, but for those willing to dig, there’s a rich and tragic mythology beneath the surface.

Technical performance has improved since launch, and while there are still occasional frame drops or glitches, they rarely detract from the overall experience.

Simply put, Elden Ring is a game that respects its players. It encourages exploration, rewards perseverance, and challenges you to improve — not just your character, but yourself. It’s a modern classic that will be remembered for years to come.

Rating: 10/10

Elden Ring is the best worst decision I’ve ever made. Imagine paying full price for a game that actively bullies you, laughs at your pain, and then has the audacity to look stunning while doing it.

Every enemy is either:

A tutorial dummy that folds in two hits, or

A walking god of destruction that erases my existence before I can say “roll.”

Boss fights? More like therapy sessions I didn’t sign up for. “Just dodge,” they say. Yeah, let me just perfectly time 17 consecutive dodge rolls while an angry demigod turns the ground into lava and throws a meteor at my face. Easy.

And the best part? I keep coming back. Like some kind of masochist, I willingly boot up this game just to get humbled over and over again. It’s not fun. It’s not fair. It’s not healthy. But it’s amazing.

10/10. Would recommend if you enjoy screaming at pixels and reconsidering every life choice that brought you here.

elden ring is not just a game its a test of willpower and patience. from the very beginning the world throws everything at you. towering monsters, cruel landscapes, bosses that feel impossible. you die, again and again, sometimes before you even take a step forward. but somehow you always pick yourself back up. there is a strange beauty in that cycle of defeat and return.

every loss feels heavy but every victory no matter how small feels like fire in your veins. the game does not hand you triumph you have to carve it out of stone with stubborn hands. every time you rise after being crushed, every time you swing back even when the odds laugh in your face, you understand what it means to endure.

elden ring is more than combat. it’s survival, it’s persistence, it’s a reminder that even when the world is cruel and unrelenting you can still find a way forward. you fight, you win, you live. and in that struggle, in those moments of rising after endless defeat, you discover why this game matters so much.

Game has absolutely painfully incompetent combat mechanics. It is not a matter of "this was meant to be challenging." It's the case of input buffering being wildly inconsistent with one of the worst camera systems I've ever had the displeasure of encountering. Sprint will frequently not work and need to be attempted again.

The world building is gorgeous and the graphics are stellar. The game has a compelling story-line and you are able to carve your own path at your own cadence. It has some outstanding "HOLY SH*T" moments and satisfying upgrade paths.

I love the idea of tackling the same boss repeatedly, learning their move set until I can perfect the dance of the fight and beat them with flawless execution. It feels like a rewarding accomplishment.

However, melee combat is tediously inconsistent, almost exclusively due to this disgusting input buffering (cannot speak for sorcery or ranged combat - perhaps those do not suffer the same fate). Sometimes your move will buffer to complete immediately after your current one, other times it won't, despite being cast at identical times. Another deeply flawed issue is you may attempt to dodge roll backwards, to the south, let's say. But, if you are camera locked onto an enemy, and they've quickly sidestepped or dodged behind you, your dodge will already be queued up in the original direction and send you somersaulting towards them to your death. But, the fun part is, sometimes it won't queue either! It's thrilling not knowing if my dodge will behave the same way moment to moment.

The camera control is also atrocious. I turned off all optional settings in an attempt to have full camera control and it feels like I'm fighting the camera to go where I intend. I have fallen off countless ledges because as you are running around a corner, precariously perched on a tower ledge, the camera will arbitrarily flip 180 degrees and send you running head first off to your death. It's a shame you NEED to have locked camera on to keep up with the speed of some bosses, since they don't allow your sens to be adjusted very high, but bosses will frequently go over your ahead making attacking or dodging nigh impossible.

You frequently CANNOT swap to an intended target. You can try every single angle with your right stick to change to a different target, yet certain ones will be excluded from selection, even though they are right in front of you, or directly between two other targets you can easily swap between.

Or, you may simply miss a completely stationary target, despite being in range with your weapon. I've slain 3 demigods but suddenly I'm incapable of hitting a target dummy!

I would not recommend subjecting yourself to this game, at least as a melee. Too inconsistent in the core mechanics of the game to be enjoyable.

Pros:
-Masterful Art Direction
-Simple and Engaging Combat
-Massive Build Variety encouraging replayability
-Rewarding Legacy Dungeons (Stormveil Castle, Raya Lucarian Academy, Leyndell)
-Fun open world to explore (not so much in the 2nd playthrough tho)
-Great boss fights (with the exception of the fire giant, elden beast)

Cons:

-identical catacombs/caves
-too many reused bosses (Ulcerated Tree Spirit-dragons etc)
-The camera is bad against large bosses
-The level design fell off in late game (Mountaintops of the Giants, Crumbling Farum Azula, Haligtree)
-FromSoft’s worst habit? Duo bosses. Haven’t seen them this bad since Dark Souls 2

Posted: September 7
🔥INTRO

You know the deal by now. Souls games don’t hold your hand.
I usually lean toward story-driven or cinematic games — the kind with clear direction, strong characters, and a sense of purpose. Shooters, fighters, action adventures, stuff that pushes forward.
But every time, I still get pulled back into FromSoft’s worlds. There’s something about the lore, the atmosphere, and that haunting world-building that always grabs me, even when the experience itself drives me crazy.
So here’s my full take on Elden Ring and Shadow of the Erdtree.

🌄 VISUALS & WORLD DESIGN

Let’s get one thing straight: the art direction is phenomenal.
The architecture, the armor, the bosses, all of it looks like it belongs to a civilization that truly existed. Every ruin feels like something incredible once happened there. There’s no denying it, visually this game is breathtaking.

But when you look past the presentation, the world doesn’t really react to anything.
It’s beautiful, but static. You travel through deserts, castles, and divine towers, but nothing about the player’s journey ever reshapes the world in a meaningful way. Cool things happen off-screen, and that kills immersion for me. The world looks alive, but it never feels alive. It’s just a stage that resets endlessly.

I do understand that for lore reasons it all makes sense. You are stuck in a world of death and rebirth, and even victory only resets the cycle. I’m not knocking that idea, it fits the theme perfectly. I just don’t personally enjoy loop-based worlds that never move forward. It’s not a flaw, it’s simply not the kind of storytelling that resonates with me.

If the world had reacted to your choices, or if the environment had told more of the story through action instead of text, it could have been unforgettable. Instead, it ends up as gorgeous noise stretched across too much land.

🎧 SOUND DESIGN

No complaints here.
FromSoft’s audio team delivered another masterpiece. The soundtrack, environmental soundscapes, and weapon impact all hit perfectly. The mix feels balanced right out of the box, and the voice acting is excellent. This is one area where Elden Ring completely nails atmosphere.

⚔️ GAMEPLAY

Elden Ring doesn’t care how you play, and that’s one of its best qualities. You can fight naked with a stick or min-max until you one-shot every boss. It’s your choice, and that freedom deserves respect.

For most players, the difficulty can be overcome through understanding the systems, doing research, and learning from others. That’s how the game is designed — a shared struggle where players leave messages, share discoveries, and help each other figure it out. I actually respect that. It asks for commitment, but if you embrace it, the game can be really fun. You get what you put in.

The DLC shows that side of FromSoft at its best. Messmer became one of my favorite bosses in any of their games. The fights leading up to the finale were creative, intense, and rewarding. But the final encounter with Promised Consort Radahn left me a bit disappointed. I was happy to beat it, but I didn’t enjoy the fight itself as much as I expected. That’s personal preference — he’s meant to be the hardest challenge, and I’m fine with that, but I also want a boss that elevates the experience of overcoming it.

Still, the DLC is stunning overall. It feels like a melting pot of Bloodborne and Elden Ring, full of incredible ideas, beautiful art, and memorable fights. Even with the uneven boss design, it’s some of FromSoft’s best work.

📖 STORY & LORE

Here’s where I check out completely. I understand what FromSoft is going for with its cryptic storytelling, player interpretation, and environmental clues, but it just doesn’t land for me.

On paper, I like the idea. The concept of the Ring shattering, gathering fragments, and becoming whole again is thematically strong. It’s about finding will in chaos, about building yourself back together through struggle. I actually love that message. The problem is how it’s delivered.

The world relies too much on telling instead of showing. Most of the story lives in item descriptions, vague NPC dialogue, and YouTube breakdowns. That’s not mystery to me, that’s just friction. It feels foggy, not profound, and it’s been the same loop of death and rebirth since Demon’s Souls. At this point, I’m over it.

It’s a style of storytelling that works for some people, but it’s not for me. I prefer something closer to Sekiro’s approach where the story unfolds naturally through clear action and dialogue. By the time I reached Shadow of the Erdtree, I couldn’t care less about the NPCs. They feel like empty vessels built to dump exposition instead of characters with emotional weight.

So yeah, I chose the Frenzied Flame ending, because it fits how I feel about this world — burn it all, break the cycle, and move on.

💭 PERSONAL TAKE

The combat is fun once you find your rhythm. Switching weapons, respeccing, and experimenting keeps it fresh, and I did enjoy beating a lot of the bosses in both the base game and the DLC. The experience can be thrilling when it all clicks, but sometimes the game straight up trolls you. I love it and hate it at the same time. It’s a game that demands patience and teaches you to get comfortable with dying, to not stress, to just keep going.

My real critique isn’t just about Elden Ring, it’s about the whole formula — Demon’s Souls, Dark Souls 1–3, even Bloodborne and Sekiro to a point. It all feels like one giant connected world that never really moves forward. I still love what FromSoft does when they switch things up, like with Sekiro, which had a tighter story and better pacing. But Elden Ring feels bloated. It lets you skip a lot if you want, which is good, but if you try to see everything, it starts to drag. Some areas shine, others feel empty, and halfway through it becomes a mix of highs and lows.

I’m not really an endgame-type player. I don’t need a game to go on forever. I just want an experience that stays engaging. Elden Ring delivers that in bursts, but it wears me down over time. When I finally beat it, I felt relief more than accomplishment. It’s a game I respect deeply, but I don’t love playing it the way others do.

🧠 CLOSING THOUGHTS

Elden Ring feels like the culmination of everything FromSoft has built since Demon’s Souls. It combines the best aspects of that style — intricate world design, haunting atmosphere, and rich lore — into one massive experience. Even if I think the scale and repetition diminish some of its impact, the world itself is still elegant and fun to explore.

To me, a slightly more focused game, expanding on Dark Souls 3 rather than spreading wider, could have delivered the same effect with more consistency. Still, this is FromSoft at the peak of their craft. The ambition is undeniable.

I don’t really care what other people think of it at this point. Elden Ring’s design is perfect for players who love piecing everything together, where almost nothing gets in the way of gameplay and challenge, and the world itself tells the story. I’m not criticizing that approach — it’s just the part that engages me the least.

The combat, character building, and exploration are where I have the most fun. I enjoy the simple side of the lore and the visual storytelling. What loses me is being left with too many unanswered questions. I don’t want to chase every theory or spend hours researching the meaning behind it all. For me, that starts to feel like homework. But the lore itself is strong and beautifully written, so if that’s your thing, more power to you.

Elden Ring is grand, ambitious, and visually unmatched, but also overextended, uneven, and emotionally distant. For Souls fans, it’s legendary. For me, I’m somewhere in the middle.

Malenia's gimmicks and WFD first part are unintuitive.

Veterans probably will have no issue, but for players new to the genre, its an easy turn-off.

Combat remains true to the Souls formula: precise, punishing, and immensely satisfying. Every swing, dodge, and spell cast carries weight, and each encounter demands patience and skill. Victory never feels cheap—it feels earned. What sets Elden Ring apart, however, is the sheer flexibility it grants players. Mounted combat adds a new layer of strategy to encounters in the open fields, magic has been expanded into entire schools of sorcery and incantations, and the build variety is staggering—ranging from colossal weapon bruisers to nimble assassins and powerful spellcasters. Most importantly, the open world itself gives you agency. If a boss feels overwhelming, you’re no longer forced to grind against a wall; instead, you can venture into new lands, uncover powerful gear, or level up, and then return stronger on your own terms.

Posted: September 6
Elden Ring is a fun, epic, open-world RPG. Made by Fromsoft and notoriously difficult, this is a huge game with knights and dragons and monsters.

The gameplay itself is fun - you explore a big world, slowly improving & leveling up as you piece together bits and pieces of the lore. The game is challenging, sometimes brutally so. Some fights you're simply not meant to win, at least right away. Thankfully, while the game never refrains from being challenging, it does offer different strategies & ways to progress so you're never too stuck.

The world is easily the highlight of the game, with lots to do, and heaps of abilities & items to find. Everywhere you go you'll see something interesting in the distance which will often be fun to explore, and so on. It's pretty and atmospheric.

I think this game is definitely flawed though. Because of its open world design, sometimes you'll bash you head against a challenge only to realize you did things in 'the wrong order' and suddenly you come across an area that's too easy. I've had this happen multiple times in my playthough which was a shame.
The combat is fairly simplistic. It's not that it's not challenging, but it doesn't have lots of depth.
Also, some of the puzzles or quests are really obtuse. I doubt that somebody could find all the secrets or complete all the quests in this game without a guide.

I'd recommend this game to people who are looking for a big meaty game with challenge & exploration.
That being said, steer clear if you don't like hard games where you have to die over and over, or get overwhelmed by directionless games.

From the moment Elden Ring was released, it set a new standard for open-world action role-playing games. Developed by FromSoftware and published by Bandai Namco, the game quickly became a global phenomenon. Its blend of rich lore, challenging combat, and breathtaking landscapes has made it one of the most critically acclaimed titles of all time. For players looking for an unforgettable experience, Elden Ring easily earns a five-star rating.

One of the most impressive features of Elden Ring is its vast open world. Unlike many games where exploration feels restricted, Elden Ring encourages players to venture in any direction, rewarding curiosity with hidden dungeons, powerful weapons, and unique encounters. The Lands Between is filled with secrets, and every corner of the map holds something worth discovering. This sense of freedom creates a level of immersion that few games can match.

The combat system is another reason why Elden Ring stands out. Building on the mechanics perfected in the Dark Souls series, the game delivers battles that are both strategic and thrilling. Players must master timing, stamina management, and weapon choice to overcome powerful foes. Every victory feels earned, and every defeat provides a lesson for improvement. Whether using melee, magic, or a hybrid build, the combat always feels fresh and engaging.

In addition to gameplay, Elden Ring offers an incredible atmosphere and story. The world was created in part by fantasy author George R.R. Martin, giving the lore a sense of depth and mystery. Players encounter cryptic NPCs, haunting ruins, and epic bosses that all contribute to the narrative. The story is not handed to the player directly but instead discovered through exploration, encouraging deeper thought and replayability. This makes the game not just fun to play, but also fascinating to unravel.

Overall, Elden Ring is more than just a video game—it is an unforgettable journey. Its open world, challenging combat, and rich lore combine to create a masterpiece that continues to captivate millions of players worldwide. For those who want a game that challenges the mind, rewards persistence, and provides endless adventure, Elden Ring is truly worthy of a perfect five-star rating.

Posted: September 6
This game is just too damn amazing that it took me a full year to fully process it after completing it with all achievements obtained; and finally leave a review on how amazing it is.

I used up all of my vacation leaves at work just to play this non-stop for an entire week.
Not many games would have gotten me hooked that way.

I'm more of a casual gamer who's not really into any "hard" games, let alone souls-like.
I feel turned off towards games with the whole "magical world/medieval/dragons" whatever else similar genre.
I tend to get overwhelmed very easily and because of this, I steer off of games that look visually overwhelming + can have a confusing pacing/too much freedom in whatever you can do and not a linear path.
I'm not a fan of open world games for the same reason stated above, but also because lots of them just get boring very easily and always I just resort to fast-traveling every step of the way.
Every single aspect about this game turns me off and isn't anything close to my usual cup of tea when it comes to a specific sort of game.

Yet despite all of that, I genuinely enjoyed it more than most of every other game in my entire life and is without a doubt top 5 for me personally.
I do feel like this is one of the only other few games that I feel is perfect for anybody who actually consider themselves to have gaming as a hobby regardless of their tastes and preference where I can imagine it being enjoyable for any kind of person.

Everything in this game is so perfect and never gets boring; it keeps you engaged and the community for it is amazing.
PvP feels like you're playing the most responsive, yet the most cinematic game yet.
Fighting off a horde feels so rewarding.
This game is able to take away the stigma I have in most other games where the bosses are always way too easy compared to a horde of minions/enemies.
Has tons of replayability despite me being one of those people who only really like to play a game once; thanks to it having different paths you could take, different endings, and different builds that are all just incredibly fun to try and tinker with.
The story is amazing and so captivating that even a small-brain, feeble mind like mine can easily follow along.
The OST? Just pure peak cinema.
Achievement hunting isn't a chore and was rewarding; it only meant for you to actually explore more.
No micro-transactions, no guilt-tripping, no absolute nonsense gobbledygook, just pure fun.
This game definitely upped my standards in a lot of games and it's hard to even match that.

This game is monumental to me because it's actually game that made me save up for a gaming PC years ago after mainly being a console player and even when I first played it with a 1650, it still worked like a charm.

There's nothing I can really say that hadn't already been said for how great of a game this is.
I couldn't recommend this any more for any sort of person with any kind of taste in any games.

After playing this game for close to 140 hours, I can say that this game is totally worth buying! Just like many other players this was my first souls-like game.

It can be overwhelming at first with so many options to customise the character, unfamiliar terms like 'builds', intelligence, stamina, armaments, sorcery, incantations etc. But you will eventually get used to them.

This is an open world game where you are heavily rewarded for exploration. The game story does not follow a linear path and you are free to even skip a tough enemy or boss and come back to it later.
The environment, soundtracks and visuals are incredible!
The game may feel unfair at times, and it is indeed a tough game to beat. However, there are many guides online which you can follow to make your game play easier.

There are multiple endings as well. Although, I had only one play through, I can certainly see myself coming back to it in the future.

A truly wonderful game with great characters to meet along the way and challenging bosses and areas to beat!

I have tried and tried and tried to enjoy this game. I've gotten good and came back to this multiple times and it's just still so utterly awful every single time. This game has none of the qualities that made the original Dark Souls good. Miyazaki has lost sight of his original vision and has instead decided to pursue a misguided notion of gravitas and spectacle rather than the mindful and careful purpose that was integral to the quality of the first game.
The open world is barren and worthless. 9/10 items you find are completely worthless crafting items. This would be tolerable if you could just safely ignore them and get on to the actual content, but there are integral items randomly sprinkled in. The enemy placement is random and mindless, groups will just be standing in open fields not even guarding a trash item, just filling in space. Every moment spent in the open world is a moment wasted. If you try to have an organic playthrough it will be miserable. I don't want to be following a wiki-checklist to play the game. I refuse.
The intended areas that were actually designed to be played through are alright, but they have the same problems as the open world. Most of the items are garbage and useless. Enemy placement is nonsensical and spammy.
The enemies themselves bosses included all rely on cheap tactics such as undodgeable zero windup attacks meant to chip damage you or stun lock you into getting killed and unnatural delayed attacks that were purposely designed to be slightly slower or faster than your brain would expect them to happen. Animations as a result always look comically floaty or sped up. Pressing roll 20 times and hitting the boss once is not fun or skillfull. It's just tedious.
This entire game is tedious. Walking off in some random direction is tedious, clearing a camp of trash mobs that don't even give you good amounts of runes is tedious. Doing dungeons and getting a single trash item from them is tedious. Trying to figure out NPC missions without a guide is TEDIOUS. THIS GAME IS TEDIOUS. ITS BORING.

Posted: September 5
Elden Ring honestly ruined other games for me—nothing else hits the same anymore.

What I love? Pretty much everything. The story, the bosses, the world-building, the weapons (so many weapons), the lore, the enemies that jump-scare the hell out of me, and even the dumb moments where I fall off a cliff and die. Exploring is always this mix of curiosity and regret: “hmm, what’s over here?” → immediate death. But I keep coming back because it’s just that fun.

This was my very first Souls game, and honestly, I was super hesitant to buy it. I thought I’d never finish it. But somehow, I now have 300+ hours in and it’s one of the best choices I’ve ever made. 11/10, wish I could play it blind again. From getting chased by the Tree Sentinel into the Church of Elleh (traumatized forever) to finally beating the Elden Beast, every single moment felt memorable. Every death, every challenge, every secret—it all just made the experience better.

Dislikes? None. This game is fun as hell.

And yeah, the second I finished the base game, I bought the DLC full price. Still getting clapped, still loving it.

Thanks, FromSoftware, for making me miserable in the most enjoyable way possible.

The game is great for what it is. I don't know why "RPG" is the fourth tag for this game though. It is not an RPG. Not even close. It IS a Soulslike, Open World, Dark Fantasy, so they got all those right. But it's a hack-and-slash (if you can call it an RPG, I can just as legitimately call it a hack-and-slash). I'm not a huge fan of "kill everything" games, so this one wasn't for me. I much prefer a lore-and-story RPG over this gorgeous monstrosity. If you like to kill everything you see on your screen, no matter how beautifully rendered, then this game is for you. If you love learning challenging enemy patterns and timing your attacks perfectly while avoiding theirs, then this game is for you. If you like more nuance and strategy, not to mention fully developed NPCs with something to offer other than gear and upgrades, then skip this one and try Avowed, Witcher 3, any of the last three Elder Scrolls, Kingdom Come: Deliverance, or some other actual RPG. If you really like RPGs, this one isn't.
