A majority of people play Dark Souls for the "Action" in Action-RPG, but I might be one of the few people who actually prefers the RPG aspects (magic in particular), and spending more than half an hour fighting a single boss isn't what I usually play for (though I might do so if I really like a boss). So for me, Elden Ring was probably the best of the Soulsborne games (though I've yet to play Sekiro).

My first run was as a STR/FAITH build, so mileage may vary, but the amount of options you get is fantastic, even if you take out spirit ashes and summons. The open-world is a bit of a drag as expected, but it's still better than most of the other ones (I don't get Breath of the Wild for instance, but this one clicked for me), and the options it gives you for progression and building is wonderful.

The bosses are, for the most part, fine, despite what everyone says. They are harder than previous games, but not much, a lot of complaints like "oh you can't heal safely even after dodging" or "the boss can extend his combo" have been here since Dark Souls 1 DLC. I know, because I reluctantly replayed the game again after ER. You just probably don't notice after playing the game 10 times over and mastering every boss.

The only outliers are the endgame boss rush and the "superboss". "Superboss" is the only boss I used spirit ashes for (which still took a few tries, even using double colossal weapons). For the endgame bosses, I did end up turning to double colossal jump attacks after a few tries of not feeling progress. If you're stubborn and unwilling to use strategies like this, then the last 5% of the game is going to suck, but I implore you to try. Use different weapons, skills, respec maybe. You can try using weaker spirit ashes instead of the OP ones if you feel they break the boss experience.

Ultimately, a higher skill ceiling just leaves more room for improvement over multiple playthroughs, compared to beating most DS3 bosses in 1-2nd try. I actually look forward to beating the endgame bosses without trading hits for stance breaks, maybe on my next run.

I've been playing this game for about a year and a half at the time of writing this. It is a game that I never wanted to play as I thought it would be WAY too difficult for me and I just wasn't a skilled enough gamer to find any enjoyment from this game. I was so WRONG!!

I needed some guidance initially as some of the game mechanics were very vague, but once I started to work it out, and received help setting up a full strength build, I started to love this game.

There is nothing quite like spending hours trying to defeat a boss, losing runes, and dying a maddening amount only to finally beat the boss. I've never felt such achievement in my life. Such a sense of accomplishment. I enjoy finding all the secret places and just wandering around.

There are a few minor annoyances about the game, but for me at least, they pale in comparison to the joy that this game has given me. It is beautiful in such a disturbing way. So much detail in every little thing. The story, the lore, just everything. I love finding new weapons, items and armour just so I can read the lore.

I have really enjoyed laughing with friends about the different bosses and enemies - "f*ark, that boss was the worst" or "oh yeah, I know the bit you are up too, good luck with that..." etc

Did I mention I love this game??

Posted: September 3
I hate this game. I hate this game with the intensity of a thousand suns collapsing into a black hole. Every boss fight feels like being mugged in a dark alley by a Victorian chimney sweep with knives taped to his shoes. I swear the developers sat down in a meeting and said, “How can we hurt people without technically committing a crime?” And the answer was Elden Ring.

First of all, the map is too big. I walk five feet in one direction and suddenly I’m in another dimension being screamed at by a dragon the size of my childhood trauma. And don’t get me started on the enemies. Why is there always ONE guy hidden around the corner waiting to stab me in the kidneys like I owe him rent money? It’s medieval jump scares, but instead of ghosts it’s just some dude with a rusty butter knife that somehow one-shots me.

And the bosses? Oh, the bosses. I don’t fight them, I audition for their comedy special. I roll, I swing, I die. Repeat 47 times until I ascend spiritually and realize the only thing I’ve defeated is my own will to live. “Get good,” people say. Buddy, I am good. I’ve become a part-time mathematician calculating invincibility frames like I’m trying to send rockets to Mars.

But here’s the problem: I love it. I love it so much I want to smash my controller into dust and then eat the dust so the game lives inside me forever. The world is gorgeous. The combat is addicting. Every victory feels like solving world peace with nothing but a rusty sword and unearned confidence. Elden Ring doesn’t just give you a challenge, it gives you an abusive relationship with a fantasy setting, and I keep crawling back like it’s my toxic ex who makes great pancakes.

Final score: 10/10. Game ruined my life. Can’t stop playing. Send help.

This game is entirely too long, overstays its welcome, repeats copy paste content over and over again and like most other souls games, the story is just nonsense held together by the wiki and youtubers. This game will make you ask so many questions, no answers will ever be given for 99% of them. I tried, god did I try to finish this game, but I cant. I've spent 112, hours and after doing the single thing I understood and was working towards for over 100hrs, my reward was being teleported (no cutscene, or anything to explain it, you just goto sleep in a prior cutscene, a character does stuff and then you wake up in a random place with no explanation or even a vague idea what your doing, you just awake in a new zone that is never mentioned and you have zero idea or reason to take a single step forward.

I still don't know what an elden lord is, or have any reason to care or want to be one.

the open world is at first impressive, but quickly becomes a chore, as you realize you'll have to navigate that massive castle or castle, or castle, or cave, or tree, and you do that without any reason what so ever, besides the vague "become an elden lord". WHY I'm just some dude that gets woken up, and told to go kill all these people who haven't done anything to me.

Playing this game is like being the main dude from memento, its just a bunch of loosely connected zones with the thinnest thread of story linking them all together and the whole time your forgetting what your doing or if you even ever knew what you were doing.

I wish I could go back in time and not buy this game. I kept pressing on hoping it would get better, hoping the time I'd invested already would be worth it, hoping the end of the game was near, It wasn't. Save yourself the time and go play Silksong or literally any other souls game. I am done with fromsoft games, this has completely killed my enjoyment for their games.

Of all the Souls games I have played and died in, Elden Ring is the only one I have managed to finished and I understand why it can be seen as one of the best games of all time.

While I cannot speak on the DLC, since I do not have it, I can say that the combat feels better than how I remember other Souls games. And despite the best efforts of my controller's drifts killing me constantly when I went for the Lord of Frenzy Ending, that did not detract from the experience.

With the variety of weapons, talismans, spirit summons (which you should not feel bad for using is you want, I personally love my dogs even if they die, constantly), armor, endings and a randomizer mod, there is a good amount of replay value. Heck, cinematic value only is high both in cutscenes and in the soundtrack while playing. And graphically it is a gorgeous sight, even the hell swamp since it provides variety both in terms of things that want you dead and how it feels to explore.

The voice acting is also excellent, including secondary characters that don't play any major parts and it is nice that they have different outcomes depending on your choices too. And despite being a large world it doesn't feel boringly empty. Which is helped by the access to fast travel so you're not running through the same place over and over and over again when you eventually have to backtrack to someplace else and get something you missed. Which is more than likely.

Although I did die to Maliketh. A lot. I'm not the type to rage and throw a controller, but I had to take a break from the game because I was smacking my face into a wall at mach five. Some will say skill issue. Some will say bad build. I will say, yeah pretty much my build was trash because I didn't fully commit and I did not learn the boss patterns. I react, and if I fail to roll or parry, I die and try again. Because I eventually came back and committed trial and error constantly. The payoff for beating him and the rest? Worth it.

If you want to get into Soul games, this is likely to be the easiest, at least that I've played and worth it both on and off sale.

Dark Souls I, 10/10, is the greatest game of all time. Dark Souls II was almost as good, 9/10. Dark Souls III, the boss design felt too dumb (let's just lazily stick tumors on everything and call them bosses; no thanks) to want to finish 6/10, never finished. Elden Ring: 4/10. 99% of the map needs to be removed. Everything after Stormveil Castle is boring and the enemies all suck (but are not hard). The weapon arts are underwhelming. The level design, the absolute best thing imo about Dark Souls I and II, leaves you either spending more time searching and getting frustrated or looking things up than actually playing the game. Within instances, level design is decent, but the instances don't cleverly chain together, instead you have to travel MASSIVE distances of worthless, sparse content just so they can force the "open world" concept (which is DOGSH*T in EVERY game I've ever played) wasting 90% of your time. Unlike Dark Souls I and II where you actually spend the time playing the game, Elden Ring feels more like doing research so you can write a paper than an actual game. In previous Dark Souls games, it was super fun hunting for hidden content due to the brilliance of their level design. Elden Ring is just the same level of fun combat but feel as if you get 1/10th of it, despite the game being massively larger. It's overall more content, but every piece of the map is nauseatingly unsatisfying and takes all the nerves you should spend stuck against enemies instead spent on finding what in the hell you are supposed to do. I don't want to spend half my day to find where I need to go unless I look it up, both options ruining the game, just to spend an hour crushing the bosses and monsters, which, if you are a veteran of the souls series, aren't tough, but are sometimes annoying due to poor design. Targeting of flying or small monsters makes them tougher than any boss in the game due to you swinging 180 degrees away from where you tell your guy to swing (eagles and dogs), and is entirely dependent on what weapon you are using whether or not its even possible to hit them out of the air, not whether you are good at the game. The way they implement PvP is better than the previous Dark Souls games, but isn't the main part of it, so it doesn't make up for poor world design. The world part about it all is that there is only one clear path you are supposed to take to deal with the content at an appropriate level, but it is purely guesswork or google searches for direction. Game would honestly be better if it contained 4 total bosses and they just ended it after Stormveil. Can't start with such a beautiful and then followup it a hundred hours after of garbage content. Dark Souls franchise rating: 9/10. Elden Ring rating: 4/10

Posted: September 2
Elden ring is everything that made Dark Souls good and brought to perfection. The open world is honestly the most beautiful and stunning world i have ever seen in my whole gaming career. It´s one of the rare games where i sometimes stopped traveling and just had to admire the view because it was so stunning or so interesting that i tried to wrap my head around what could be the history behind it.

Not only is it beautiful but it presses all my High/Dark Fantasy buttons in every possible way. There are Dragons, big bad ass looking castles, sorceries, demons, dungeons, and more. And all of it sprinkled with the well known and beloved fromsoft/Myazaki weirdness.

As the tarnished the player arrives in this world to follow divine guidance and become the new lord. Quite cheesy i thought when i started. A stereotype story about an outsider to become a hero that is going to save the kingdom from evil and despair and become the new king. But its not that easy in reality. Nearly every little thing/item that is in the world was placed by design and tells a story (literally in the item description) and lets you puzzle together what actually happened before your arrival and why you do what you are doing.

And thats my only complaint: Even though the mysterious worlds, that can only be understood if studied through items description, are a 100% fromsoft watermark and it was an absolute refreshing new attempt at story telling for a long time, I really wouldn´t have mind if we got at least a LITTLE bit more information in cutscenes or NPC dialogues. But most is only a puzzle piece, pregnant with meaning but still vague by its own.

When i saw the story trailer for elden ring i actually thought it would be the case this time. Sekiro for example went in a good direction in those terms.

The gamemechanics are complex and allow you to play in incredible amount of ways.
You love Melee? You can use swords, daggers, katanas, scimitars, spears, maces, whips(don´t know of many games with whips), martial arts, big inhuman hammers, shields, epic armor and even more weapons in dual wield, 2 hand or shield/weapon combo. And every weapon can be enhanted with a skill like a leap attack, a flurry attack, parade and even magical ones like transforming to a big magical sword and many more. There are even unique items that have their own unique weapon skill.

You like playing with magic? There is holy magic, fire magic, star magic, gravitation magic, death magic and more.

There also bows and a big bunch of craftibles like fire bombs or poison knives. You can specialize in any weapon or combine all of the above for your own unique playstyle, the possibilities are insane and can offer 1000+ hours of fun if you can not get enough of trying out different builds.

You can even try the builds in PVP if you know the game better than your own house but still want to continue playing. I am not a fan of souls pvp anymore though so i did not try it.

Definitely the most overrated and overhyped game in the history of gaming. Great visuals, but that's about the only real positive. The combat system is worse than most souls-likes I've played, it's janky and most encounters are extremely cheesable even if you don't intend to since the hitboxes and animations are subpar. The outdoor visuals may be great, but the open world is so big the exploration gets repetitive fast, made worse by the fact that all dungeons are virtually the same and there is little mob variety aside of recolors. The locations layouts are also completely uninspired. Characters look and feel like bricks, the gear does nothing and also looks bad.

I came to this game with great expectations due to the hype, but it's like eating stale bread that came in a very fancy wrapping, ultimately you have to look past the cover and stop chewing.

Well, I haven't played any other game for 10 hours straight in one sitting so yeah, I would say this is really good.

The game has an interesting phasing, it doesn't do the usual so you cannot just rush the main questline. Also the leveling system is very non-direct and I concluded that really what has made me get better is really to spend time on it from the really interesting world building. It creates this loop of "this area might be too hard for me right now" then you go explore, get lost, discover cool stuff, get new weapons, new skills (like, as a player, not like, your character) and then you go back and get better. Is like scooping parts from different areas instead of trying to eat the main dish in one hit. I really appreciate this type of game, even when the systems are really difficult. Additionally, it does have an advantage playing after a couple years because the game has been balanced and a loooot of informative media has been created for it.

Also, level up vigor to 40 at least before anything else. Like seriously, the weapons will do more damage for being upgraded, not from your stats.

Posted: September 1
To say Fromsoft and I have had a strained relationship would be an understatement. With my first exposure being Dark Souls 1, I saw what looked like a really fascinating phenomenon that I wanted in on. But it just... didn't stick. It seemingly couldn't. And not just Dark Souls 1, but also Dark Souls 3 courtesy of Humble Monthly and PS3 Demon's Souls courtesy of a local yard sale.

These games were pretty appealing and I understood why they worked so well, but I couldn't just make them for me. Abandoned them as "not for me", and figured Elden Ring would be the same and not worth trying over.

Initially, I was right. When a friend gifted me my copy, I made a genuine effort to enjoy it, see what all the hubbub was about. And while I liked it more than any of the prior three games I'd played, that familiar brick wall pancaked me yet again after about eight hours and no real progress. So I put it down and forgot about it.

Flash forward a few years, and I'm just getting off an adrenaline high from having finished Jedi Survivor. In the heat of the moment, I had an epiphany. Why was the Jedi series and also Code Vein working for me, but not Elden Ring? What can I do to tear down that brick wall and finally "get it"?

Initially, I tried copying my Jedi Survivor playstyle, and as positive results rolled in, as well as starting to play Nightreign with friends on the side, things started clicking. My build ideas were coming full circle. I was comfortably beating bosses. I was pouring degenerate stretches of long hours in because I was suddenly hooked like a crack fiend. And I can barely explain why.

The open world where you're virtually never stuck in one place? Buildcrafting finally feeling approachable instead of vague? The game's willingness to shrug its shoulders and let you do whatever the hell you want? Story and lore that feels followable and interesting without requiring video essays? Honestly, I don't know for certain. But Elden Ring didn't just click, I was made absolutely feral for the experience. Even when I was getting my ass kicked! ESPECIALLY when I was getting my ass kicked. Determination is a hell of a drug.

It's not flawless, but nothing ever is, and this is one of those very, EXTREMELY rare games where it gets really damn close to that perfection. And I can't wait to eventually play more, between Shadow of the Erdtree and onboarding new friends in Seamless Co-op. Any excuse to get back in and lose myself in this amazing game.

Absolute masterpiece of the game.

I also love the gameplay. The player should account for every attack they do or watch out for attacks from enemies. Which makes the game harder, but fair. Since player can learn patterns and perform attack/defence based on them.
Difficulty in this game is not strictly set. Player can level up their character or find better tools to make fights easier. Finding bosses' weaknesses and counter-playing based on that feels great.

Note: this is my first Souls game)

I’ve been playing the soulsborne series for a long time and Elden Ring has satisfied most of my expectations.

What I’ve always loved about the soulsborne series is its ability to make me fear the unknown in all of my first playthroughs. By that I mean every area/place we explore we’re bound to see something scary, challenging, interesting or outright strange. Elden Ring takes that element up a notch by throwing us into a giant open world to explore with little direction. Everytime I explored the world I was constantly on high alert and forced to choose my fights carefully. On the topic of exploration, it’s very easy to miss certain areas, dungeons, bosses, NPC and weapons due to how vast the world of Elden Ring can be. I ended up missing a lot of NPC sidequests and a few optional bosses.

The gameplay mechanics are mostly taken from Dark Souls 3, aside from the jumping mechanic which came from Sekiro. There’s a lot of new weapons, magic to choose from and many weapons from previous titles have also carried over too. The armour design in Elden Ring is probably my least favorite in the series. Some armour are too bulky, too slim or too specific to the point that mix matching aka fashion souls is kind of hard to pull off, so I just ended up wearing the same sets throughout the whole game. The soundtrack is alright. Some of the boss themes were good, most of them were kind of generic sounding epic boss music. For the most part it didn’t wow me as much, but the background music in certain parts of the game does a good job in setting the mood and making exploration or farming not as monotonous.

Now, onto the biggest problem and probably the most controversial topic on Elden Ring, the difficulty. Elden Ring is definitely a punishingly tough game. This is most likely due to how overly aggressive and deceptive the enemies and bosses have become. Attack animations have unnatural delays or vague movements that make it difficult to determine an opening. Chain attacks are abundant and extremely long to the point where our stamina is drained and we are forced in a 50/50 situation at all times. AOE magic is spread wider than ever to the point where we just have to take the damage. And everyone is either as fast or faster than us. Combined, these elements create a punishing combat system to overcome with little room for error or forgiveness. At times, it made me wonder whether learning some of these fights was even worth the effort.

Overall, I would recommend any long time soulsborne fan to try Elden Ring. Just keep in mind that the combat is not the same as the previous title. For newcomers curious about the series, I suggest starting with Dark Souls III first. Jumping straight into the Elden Ring without prior experience could be a frustrating introduction.

I want to preface this review by saying that, at the time of writing, this is the only FromSoftware game I've played, So I'm not sure how much what I say applies to every game from them. That being said, on with the review.

Never have I played a game with as much flexibility, nuance, and depth, as this one. No matter how you like your combat in games, there is a playstyle for you in this one.

This game is hard, but its design allows you to tune the difficulty to your liking even without a direct difficulty option. Don't want to spend hours on a boss? The game also looks incredible. Between the golden leaves of the Altus plateau and the colorful water of the lakes, this game proves that you can have a hyper realistic artstyle without making your game look the 256th generic grey shooter that came out a decade too late.

All in all, a masterpiece that I would recommend to anyone looking for a challenge.

4 Hours In, and Elden Ring is Already a Masterpiece

I’ve only spent about 4 hours in Elden Ring so far, and I’m absolutely hooked. From Software has once again proven why they’re the masters of their craft. Here’s a quick breakdown of my early impressions:

The World is Stunning
The open world is huge, beautifully crafted, and full of hidden details. From rolling green fields to looming castles and eerie ruins, every area feels distinct and alive. The lighting and atmosphere are top-notch, and the day-night cycle adds a dynamic layer to exploration. I’ve spent so much time just wandering, taking in the sights, and getting lost in the world.

Combat is Tight, Challenging, and Rewarding
Elden Ring’s combat feels as precise as ever. You’ll need to be patient and strategic—timing your dodges, blocking at the right moment, and knowing when to go in for the kill. The enemies are tough, and the boss fights (even early on) are nothing short of epic. It’s hard, but the satisfaction when you finally take down a tough enemy is so rewarding.

Freedom and Exploration
The freedom you get in how you approach the game is a game-changer. You’re not locked into any specific path. Want to fight a boss early? Go for it. Want to explore caves, dungeons, or the open world? The game lets you do that. There’s a real sense of discovery, and I’ve already found myself stumbling upon hidden areas and tough enemies just by exploring off the beaten path.

Character Customization
From the start, you can customize your build to fit your playstyle. Whether you prefer magic, ranged combat, or getting up close and personal with heavy weapons, there’s a lot of flexibility in how you approach battles. I love how you can mix and match weapons, spells, and abilities, making each encounter feel fresh.

A World Full of Mystery
The story isn’t handed to you directly, but that’s part of the charm. The lore is there if you’re willing to dig for it, and the mystery surrounding the world and its characters is gripping. It makes me want to learn more with each new discovery.

Final Verdict (So Far):
I’m only 4 hours in, but Elden Ring already feels like an incredible journey. If you love challenging gameplay, rewarding exploration, and deep lore, this is an easy recommendation. It’s got all the From Software magic, but with the added freedom and scope of an open world.

Can’t wait to see where this ride takes me next. Get ready to lose yourself in this one.

Elden Ring is overall a good game, unfortunately it has both really good high-points with really bad low-points. The first half of the game is really well executed in my opinion, bosses are unique, difficult but fair and the overall game has a reasonable difficulty curve. The game gives you decent access to a variety of different ways of doing damage, whether you want fast and weak, slow and strong, range or melee, magic of different kinds etc. so there's something for everyone.

Unfortunately the latter half of the game can be quite the chore. Areas feel significantly more empty, both in terms of bosses, mini-dungeons and larger dungeons, as well as in random discoveries. The first half of the game is littered with these things so you can always find something interesting to explore, interact with or engage in combat. After a certain point however these things are mostly missing and areas become large mostly empty zones you run through simply to reach the next required boss-fight.

Quality of bosses also takes a nosedive in the second half of the game. For one particular boss (but there are multiple) this largely comes down fighting the camera. Bosses that are very large are not enjoyable to fight in this game, as you won't be able to see any/most of the enemies "tells" for what they are about to do (because you are so close when you attack that the enemy can't fit on the screen). This means you are left with a couple options, either become psychic to see the future, repeat the fight until you happen to get lucky or play very safe: run in after an attack get a single small hit in, then run super far away just in case they attack. The first option is rather impractical, the second is arguably not that fun and the third is tedious, leaving you with no fun way to finish the fight. I have yet to fight a rather large boss in this game were I'm not spending just as much time fighting the camera as I'm spending fighting the boss.

Another issue with boss quality later in the game is that their core moveset is just less fun in my opinion. More bosses move quickly whilst still hitting really hard, near all of them have WAY too many faints and have a ton of large AOE attacks where it isn't always obvious where you get hit. AOE attacks in particular make a lot of bosses irritating for me, as they serve as a way for the boss to force you to flee far away, which is fine, except they do this really often. In addition a lot of AOE damage is just unintuitive to dodge. Whilst hitboxes for most physical attacks in this game are done extremely well, some hitboxes like fire don't make it obvious where you'd get hurt.

Overall this leads to the latter half of the game feeling like quite the chore as I'm playing through, that was true in my first, and every subsequent playthrough.

One pet peeve I also would like to add: Please FromSoftware, just please stop trying to make this a platformer, you're very bad at making platformers. The movement in this game doesn't work well for platforming at all and the sections you put together are very poorly done, stick to what you're good at.

So why am I still recommending the game? Because the game looks great, runs fine even on older systems (I did most of my first ~70 hours playing on a mid-range PC from 2015), has beautiful environments and an amazing soundtrack. The combat is addicting, the freedom in playstyle and builds provide excellent replay value and there are a lot of really well designed boss-fights. The second half of the game can drag on and the quality is noticeably lower, but my overall experience of the whole game was without a doubt great.

Of the few soulsborne games I've played this one is the best. It's open world is packed full of content to explore, you'll never be dissapointed when adventuring off-course, and although the soulslike aspects may enrage you, you'll feel immense accomplishment however you defeat the enemies and progress through the game, upgrading your weapons and discovering new armor. Every time you play through the game again in New-Game or a new save, you'll discover whole new areas and bosses you never experienced the first time. Having obtained all trophies on console, when I moved to pc, I acquired this game full-price a second time on steam and have put hundreds of hours on either PC or console, and am nearly completed with every Elden Ring trophy on steam. The graphics are beautiful and not so intensive, so even lower end computers can run this game while not being too detrimental to the performance and therefore the actual playability. Do be aware of the difficulty present in the game, if you're sensitive to that, but also be aware there are tons of features and mechanics in the game that can assist you in that difficulty. This game is an experience and another world. Of any game, this one, I think, I would choose to be able to play for the first time again because of the whimsy and magic that's exceedingly palpable when you roam the world and discover new artifacts and bosses. The story is 'vague', I'd say, but through bits and pieces you can construct a consensus of whats going on; ultimately, the lore is fascinating and the journey the game takes you on is one of the best in all of gaming.

Elden Ring is the ultimate fusion of Dark Souls, Sekiro, and Bloodborne, delivering an experience that feels both familiar and refreshingly new. It is truly a masterpiece, not only because of its vast open world, but also due to the sheer variety and quality of its improvements over FromSoftware’s previous titles.

While it’s true that Elden Ring is somewhat more approachable than the older Souls games, the sense of challenge is far from gone. The early and mid game encourage exploration, experimentation, and learning at your own pace, but the late game ramps up significantly demanding precision, mastery of mechanics, and a carefully thought out build.

What makes Elden Ring stand out is how it balances freedom with difficulty. Players can choose their own path, discover hidden bosses, and tailor their character in countless ways, making every playthrough feel unique. The world design, atmospheric storytelling, and combat depth combine to create an unforgettable adventure that rewards persistence and creativity.

If you beat the game, help some folks out beating Radahn or Radagon.

Posted: August 28
From a Newly Crowned Elden Lord

Okay, so... 210 hours. Two hundred and ten. That's what it took me to finally beat this absolute monster of a game, and honestly? I'm still processing it all.
Look, I'm not usually the type to write reviews, but after everything Elden Ring put me through, I had to say something. This game broke me. Like, actually broke me. I rage-quit so many times my girlfriend started keeping a tally on the fridge. There were nights I went to bed angry at a boss fight, and mornings I woke up thinking about different strategies.
But here's the thing – and this might sound weird – I loved every frustrating minute of it.
The first time I walked out into Limgrave and saw that golden tree stretching up into the sky, I actually stopped walking my character and just... stared. My roommate thought something was wrong with my controller. "Dude, why aren't you moving?" Because I was too busy picking my jaw up off the floor, that's why.
I've played a lot of games. A LOT. But nothing prepared me for the moment when I realized I could go literally anywhere I could see. That castle in the distance? You can get there. That creepy building on the cliff? Yeah, that too. And trust me, you probably don't want to, but you absolutely can.
The combat is... well, it's FromSoftware, so you know what you're getting into. My first character was this tanky knight build because I thought I'd play it safe. Forty hours in, I realized I was having way more fun with the weird spell builds, so I started over. Then I discovered katanas and... yeah, I started over again. The game just makes you want to experiment, even when it punishes you for it.
And the bosses – oh my god, the bosses. Margit nearly made me return the game. I'm not ashamed to admit I looked up a guide after my twentieth attempt. But when I finally beat him (with help from that golden knight summon, don't judge me), I literally jumped out of my chair and scared my cat.
Don't even get me started on Malenia. Just... don't.
What really got to me, though, were the quiet moments. Finding that underground city and realizing how massive this world actually is. Reading item descriptions that tell these heartbreaking little stories. Standing in some random field at sunset while the music swells and thinking, "How is this even possible?"
I know everyone says this, but the game really doesn't hold your hand. At all. I spent probably twenty hours in the starting area alone because I was too scared to venture out. But that's the beauty of it – when you finally do work up the courage to explore, every discovery feels earned.
The story is... well, it's a FromSoftware story, so good luck figuring it out without YouTube videos. But somehow that makes it better? Like you're an archaeologist piecing together this ancient tragedy. I've watched probably fifty lore videos and I'm still not sure what happened to half these people.
After 210 hours, multiple characters, and more deaths than I care to count, I finally made it. Stood there in front of the final boss with my hands literally shaking. And when that "You have become the Elden Lord" message popped up, I may have gotten a little emotional. Okay, I definitely got emotional.
Is it perfect? No. The PC performance was rough at launch (better now), and some of those late-game areas are just mean. But perfect games are boring. This game has soul, and scars, and moments of beauty that'll stick with you long after you put the controller down.
I'm already planning my next playthrough.
10/10 – Game of the decade. A true work of art. Miyazaki and FromSoftware didn’t just raise the bar — they built a new one entirely.
P.S. – That underground area with all the stars? Still gives me chills thinking about it.
P.P.S. – Use the Spirit Ashes. I don't care what the internet says. Life's too short to suffer more than necessary.
:)

A beautiful game.

Its graphics are amazing, the sceneries and different regions of the Lands-Between are breathtaking!

The difficulty is well thought (Although still a very hard game, It's a FromSoftware, after all)

The gameplay is very fun, the freedom of choice for your equipement and stats is wonderful!

The story is very good, needs brain power and will to explore aswell as reading a lot of things to understand the lore but it's so cool!

And this game allows you to make your own character, you can make your own lore about it, a whole story, it's up to your imagination!

The Exploration is so good aswell, if you see something in your field of view, anything, you can go to it, literally.

And the game is very long (in a good way!) If

Overall, one of the best RPGs ever made, if not the best, if you like RPGs, like Zelda BOTW and TOTK, and you don't mind difficulty or are looking for challenge, then you MUST play this game, would still recommend it to almost anyone!

And also, don't forget, the most important stat in this game, is STYLE! choose what you think looks cool, not what's "meta" or "best", the fun is amazing!

11/10 game for sure!

Thank you Miyazaki and the FromSoftware team for making such an amazing game!


It's sceneries are just, wow. Worth 10€ each time you enter a new area. Some of the most beautiful areas in the ENTIRE GAME are in the DLC.

It is BIG, very surprisingly BIG, you will probably spend more than 70 hours in the DLC, it's that big!

The bosses are extremely cool, the ambience is well made, the new weapons have incredible movesets, and almost every aspect of the base game is even better in the DLC.

The DLC adds a lot to the understanding of the story aswell, which is amazing!

It is probably one of the best (again!) DLC ever made for a video game, and it's entirely worth the 40€!

And also, it is difficult, VERY difficult, way more than the base game, so if you want to buy the DLC, keep in mind its difficulty is extremely high and that it's not for everyone, it way harder than the base game. Still insanely good though!

HOWEVER, the performance of the DLC are worse than those of the base game, and even with a pretty good setup, my FPS were dropping sometimes in some areas of the Lands of Shadows, so be aware of that.

Overall, an extremely good DLC, with amazing bosses, breathtaking sceneries, although extremely difficult and not the best optimized.

9/10 DLC!

Thanks to Miyazaki and the FromSoftware team for making such an amazing DLC to an already wonderful game!

Why did the hipster burn his tongue?
He was sipping coffee before it was cool.
That said, I really don't like Elden Ring. I'm glad its popular, able to reach such a vast audience, and can afford to off-load the usual "how do i play this game" stuff to Youtube, but I joined the franchise during Demon's Souls. Yeah that's right, before Bloodborne, Before Dark Souls 1, this old gamer was touching the demon inside the maiden. Back then, every single time you died, your health bar shortened permanently and the enemies grew in strength. This was called "black alignment" and if you never die you have "white alignment". Weapons, buffs, doors, cutscenes, and even the nexus were adjusted to match your alignment.

Now it's all slowburn cutscenes of some old lady wanting to sniff your fingertips or yet another eccentric NPC profiling you for being tarnished. Pvp combats are next to hopeless, meaningless co-op messages litter every inch of the game, and unless you feel like spending half your game time watching youtube, you're instead going to spend it looking for your soul stain.

Regardless, all the enjoyable elements are there and you could really lose yourself in the "Lands Between". But maybe pick up DS3 first and see how much harder it hits than this.

Elden Ring is a masterpiece, there is no doubt about it. The game is punishing, but when one overcomes this struggle, the feeling is amazing, and I am still disappointed because of my inability to replicate those memorable moments of truimph when I beat some of the most difficult bosses in the game.
If you are concerned that the game is too punishing for you, as I was at the beginning (since it was my first soulslike game) you should note that Elden Ring, unlike many other soulslike games like the Dark Souls trilogy, is open world, and allows the player to strengthen his or her build in case the player feels it is not strong enough, potentially creating builds which can melt bosses in seconds.
Almost every aspect of the game is perfect. The exploration is increadible, and allows the player to expirience an original fantasy world with more lore than than you can imagen and with an artistic style which changes for every area.
The game gives the player so much freedom concerning the playstyle and progression, and it allows each player to choose their path in the game.
There is more to this game which can be written in such a review. But "more" doesn't necessarily mean bigger, but with more meaning and depth. Elden Ring has such a huge open world, and evey bit of it is filled with meaning and depth, so much so that the game's open world actually seems much larger than it really is if compared in size to other open worlds.

Truly the Skyrim of Soulslikes: a mediocre game with mass appeal and a lot of "content". If you ever wished Dark Souls was more like Assassin's Creed then this is a game for you.

You won't ever get surprised by an invasion. Hey, but you can now teleport all around the map, visiting cave number #11, gathering bazillionth upgrading and crafting material, fighting repeated bosses and mobs. Previous FromSoft games were meticulously crafted and a joy to explore and replay. In contrast, Elden Ring feels bloated and diluted. You need to sift through a lot of mediocre open world to get to the good parts.

I tried really hard to like this game, but this was a disappointment.

Yeah, this game is just amazing. If you're thinking of buying this game, without a doubt, it's worth every penny. It's just peak, there's a reason why it won GOTY in 2022, and it shows. This game is the most fun and joy I've ever gotten from a video game in such a long time. Trying to learn and persevere through hard bosses is just really fun to me, and that's not even all. The map is just huge making it so much fun to explore the actual world with so many different environments. The entire story for those that actually care is just so well put together, you even feel a bit of tension knowing the lore behind some bosses and that thrill just adds so much to the experience. For a Soulsborne game, this is also definitely the most beginner friendly, hands down. You're able to just make builds that vary from so much like making a fun "OOGA BOOGA," strength builds to just brain dead overpowered magic builds that shreds. Don't get me on the DLC, the DLC is a phenominal expansion to the open world Soulsborne. Shadow of the Erdtree is considered to be as big as Limgrave? It just does not feel like it at all bro, there's generally so much to do in it as well as a bunch of more fun and broken builds because... we all love that. The difficulty of the bosses in Shadow of the Erdtree however... oh man, they're really difficult, you better hope you actually gathered all scadutree fragments because, oh my Radahn is hard. I have so much more to say but I think the surprises should be reaveled when you actually play it. Peak game, one of if not the greatest games I've ever played and it just surprises me that there are people out there that haven't given this master piece even a shot. Once again, and I'll keep saying it, you should play it if you haven't, if you ever get the chance. :)

🌕 “The flame has faded, the curse is lifted, and FromSoftware has ascended into godhood—this isn’t just peak, this is the new summit.” 🌕

Elden Ring isn’t a game.
It’s a revelation.
A towering, divine fusion of everything FromSoftware has ever crafted—Soulsborne, Sekiro, Bloodborne, all of it—poured into one unrelenting, genre-defining magnum opus that dares to ask:

“What if the world was open... and every inch was unforgettable?”

And the answer?
Elden Ring is gaming's Second Coming.

🗺️ The Lands Between: Open World Perfection

You want freedom?
This game kicks you out of a crypt, hands you a sword, and says “figure it out.”
No quest markers. No babying. Just you, your instincts, and a world begging to be explored.

From the golden, decaying grandeur of Leyndell to the cosmic horror nightmare of Nokstella, from blood-soaked Caelid to the chilling elegance of Raya Lucaria—every zone is its own religion.
You’ll climb mountains, plummet into subterranean galaxies, wander castles swallowed by rot—and none of it feels procedural or hollow.
It’s a world so alive it feels like it’s dreaming you.

⚔️ Combat Refined to Divinity

Every build is viable.
Every weapon, from the most cursed dagger to an anime-sized ultra greatsword, is oozing personality and flair.

Want to be a katana-wielding samurai, melting bosses with Bleed procs?
Go ahead.
Want to toss black holes and summon giant dragon heads to breathe rot?
Absolutely.
Want to cosplay as Guts and delete enemies with raw strength?
You can. You should. You must.

Boss fights are pure cinema.
Malenia? “I am Malenia, Blade of Miquella”—she will humble you.
Radahn? You literally ride into battle in an MMO-style war.
Mogh? Disgustingly drippy.
Every fight feels like the final boss of a lesser game—and that’s not even counting the optional monstrosities.

🧠 Lore by George R. R. Martin? No—It’s Mythmaking

Forget exposition dumps.
Elden Ring tells stories through landscapes, ruins, whispers, and despair.
The shattering of the Elden Ring wasn’t just an event. It was a cosmic fracture, and you are crawling through its aftermath, piecing together tragedy after tragedy.

You’re not saving the world.
You’re inheriting its ruin.
And every choice you make determines what kind of god you’ll become.

🐺 Torrent. Melina. Ranni. Iron Fist Alexander.

This game doesn’t just have NPCs.
It has legends.
You’ll fall in love. You’ll betray. You’ll be betrayed.
You’ll cry over a pot, a witch, a maiden who never opens both eyes.
You’ll burn the world for them. Or to spite them.

🎵 Music That Rips Open the Sky

The orchestral maelstrom of Radagon's theme, the melancholy swell of the Main Menu, the deep dread of Underground Cities—this OST doesn’t just complement the game.

It haunts you.

💀 Final Verdict: 1000/10 – THE FLAME HAS BEEN REBORN

Elden Ring is not just the best FromSoftware game.
It’s not just the best open world game.
It is, without a shred of exaggeration, one of the greatest games ever created.

It is what every open-world RPG aspires to be.
It is what every Soulslike envies.
It is what gaming dreams of when it closes its eyes.

“Become Elden Lord?”
No.
Become Immortal.

🛐 Praise the Erdtree. Praise the Moon. Praise the Madness.
Let there be grace. 🌟

All in all though, Elden Ring is one of those games that sets a standard, that has to have been played, if you wanna talk about the genre properly. It's significance is valid and true.

I enjoyed all FromSoftware's games before Elden Ring, I really expected to love this one.
But it just isn't for me.

The open world has been carefully crafted and feels alive, but it's missing something; other activities than combat.
I know there's exploration too and I loved that part in From's another artpiece, Dark Souls... but for some reason, exploring The Lands Between wasn't all that interesting for me.

Two things that are just personal preference, the color pallette and art direction. Elden Ring was beautiful, but not visually interesting, for god knows what reason.

Well, maybe the game wasn't for me, talking from a visual standpoint, what about the amazing bosses? People praise this game and Dark Souls 3 for their bosses.
I never played these games for their bosses. Dark Souls 3 was the weakest of the trilogy imo, maybe that tells something.

TL;DR: Elden Ring is a beautiful open world game, it lacks activities but features a lot of amazing bosses.
Still, it's just not for me.


7/10.

First souls-like. Got it cause my buddies were both playing and hyped.
Loaded in, ran around a bit, saw a dude on a horse. Died...
Got a chip on my shoulder, died 20 more times.

Eventually decided to go around him, and spent the next 10 hours exploring and testing the world. Stunning visuals, new and different enemies in every area. Every corner I turned or hill I crested laid out multiple different places that drew my attention.

Finally found my favorite weapons, a pair of curved greatswords (Usually the Beast one in main for the spinny move)

Proceeded to spider-monkey through the game. Melania wasn't too tough, did some more exploring. Farmed a lot on a specific path... If you know, you know. Beat the game, and go back from time to time to remind myself it was tougher than I remember.

10/10 - I'll get around to other Souls games someday, but there's still things here I haven't found, so it may be a while.

Posted: August 25
I am still early in the game, maybe towards the end of the first 40% or so.

The good:

-- Combat/playstyles feel rewarding, and can often have immediate success. Switching from a slow greatsword to dual scythes felt fun and responsive.
-- Adventuring is fun, finding random dungeons or other things are very cool.
-- Character creation was nice, if not overwhelming at times. That's just a me problem though, can definitely see why most people would like it.
-- Audio is nice
-- Game is hard in a nice way. A character shot a long range spell at me, then when I went to dodge leaped at me with a physical attack to punish. Much better than a damage sponge.

What I didn't like:

-- A lot of combat doesn't feel very rewarding. Big bosses do, but some side quest bosses and such give almost nothing. Maybe an Ash of War and like 2000 runes, if you're lucky.
-- Most of the game is explained in the loading screen. If you have any decent computer, it loads too fast to really get anything out of it, and there's no easy way to look at that information again. Even the Steam Deck loads areas in like 2 or 3 seconds, hardly enough time to actually read some of the tips.
-- Investing in any build is not rewarding. You can respec later in the game, but it is limited. If you decide you want a new playstyle, good luck. Using Fang with 18str/18dex, and suddenly wanting to become a caster while your int sits at 9, you have to sit there and farm runes forever before you can do anything, unless you respec, which is limited and takes a while to unlock.
-- Game is gorgeous if you like green and brown

Posted: August 24
Just as critics say, this game is spectacular, the scenery, the graphics, the story telling, and most of all, the variety of gameplay. Unlike all the other soulsborne games, in Elden Ring you can play entirely the way you want to. You can explore the map before you face a challenge thanks to the open world, there's all kinds of secrets and the game contains so much content that when rushed through will be entirely missed. To get the full experience and immersion from Elden Ring, you have to take a minute, or several hours to look closely in some areas and really try to explore the whole map instead of following the path that's shown very clearly. In terms of difficulty, the game can either be the hardest souls game, or the easiest, the thing is, YOU the player have the control of that. You can play the game in any way you want, I'd be lying if I said this game didn't save my mental health. It's a nice game to play to get away from harsh things happening in your life, yes it may be frustrating when you fail or die, but something that's built into souls games passion, after every mistake and every failure, you learn more and more until you succeed. It's about not giving up.

10/10

Posted: August 24
I've never played a true soulslike game before and wasn't sure if I was gonna like Elden Ring. I'm happy to report that I actually really enjoyed it! After overcoming some initial struggles with my lack of familiarity with some of the typical mechanics in Fromsoftware games I had a great time exploring the Lands Between. I felt extremely compelled to explore every nook and cranny of the open world. Unlike for many other games in the genre, the driving force for exploration in Elden Ring usually isn't some stupid quest marker or NPC telling you to go fetch them an ice cream, it's seeing something interesting in the distance and going there. The map is designed beautifully, I rarely felt like a place was lacking purpose or sense of wonder.
My biggest initial worry, the possibility of combat being too frustrating, turned out to be largely unfounded. I was able to clear most boss encounters within just a couple tries, without having to look up other people's strategies. That way, my approach to the game felt personal. Really the only exception to that were a couple late game bosses (you'll probably be able to guess which ones) which felt weirdly out of balance with the rest of the world. That sudden spike in difficulty was very much in line with the rest of the gameplay experience at that stage of the game however. I started to feel a bit of exploration fatigue towards the end, and the map design kind of reflected that with some of the late game areas feeling kind of empty and uninspired (the more linear dungeon sections during that stage which take up most of the time in the final hours were great though). My main criticism beyond that is probably the loot variety. Having beaten the game, I'm still not sure what 90% of the crafting items you find are even for, I didn't engage with that mechanic at all aside from crafting a few antidotes. Weapon drops don't fare a whole lot better, I gave most of them a short glance before never looking at them again. I had a silly little build with two trusty pickaxes and really didn't feel like experimenting with the weapons much. It wasn't even until the final quarter of the game that I tried some of the ashes of war that the game has been throwing at me constantly until that point. Armor variety seemed really strange to me as they all seemed to have the same tradeoffs between carry weight and stats, no matter how late into the game you find them. Maybe I just didn't understand the idea behind that, I'm not sure.

Other than that, I have extremely fond memories of the game and I'm already looking forward to playing it again some day. In a world of many failed modern AAA releases, Elden RIng is a world worth experiencing.

Posted: August 24
FOUL TARNISHED

Venture forth into The Lands Between to restore order to a fallen kingdom once united by the ever sacred Elden Rings. Wield unique playstyles from dexterous assassins to brutish fighters to artillery-based mages as you face down fearsome foes all around in an immense open-world setting that will provide ample opportunity for exploration and combat.

Elden Ring is an ambitious title by Fromsoft. While still reminiscent of their classic formula of high-difficulty action dark fantasy games, Elden Ring takes an extra step to introduce an open world environment for players to utilize, obliterating any sense of linearity in favor of complete player freedom to take the story at whatever pace they deem appropriate. Elden Ring brings a lot of great improvements and quality of life features from prior Fromsoft titles, such as no stamina drain from sprinting while out of combat and nearly zero boss runbacks to have to grind through, making Elden Ring an overall satisfying game to play from a quality standpoint. There is a clear level of polish that shines through in all aspects of this game. Elden Ring's prioritization of player freedom really helped me get out of my comfort zone, as I had spent the entirety of Dark Souls 1-3 playing with a standard, unimbued broadsword, because why not, it works just fine. After only a couple hours in Elden Ring though, I was already starting to grow attached to Colossal Swords and Fists, and making plenty of use out of summonable allies and spirits. Instead of immediately going from one Named Dungeon to the next, I was taking every opportunity to explore smaller side areas and fight random mini bosses for unique items and weapons. In a similar vein to Dark Souls 3 as well, Elden Ring goes all in on making sure that its core areas and boss fights are incredibly cinematic, with jaw-dropping visuals and soundtracks to accompany them. From start to finish, Elden Ring is an intense but rewarding journey.

Elden Ring is not without flaw though, at least in my opinion. For one, despite the open world helping exemplify how vast the story of Elden Ring expands, it can't help but feel empty in a lot of areas, or at the least devoid of anything that makes it feel unique. While in past Fromsoft games they've also recycled assets and enemies to fill out areas, Elden Ring really goes to the next level of recycling. Caves and catacombs with near identical layouts, mini-bosses recycled from mob enemies that are then recycled again later on. It's a little difficult to call the game truly open world when after exploring some side areas I had a hard time deciding if it was really worth my time. Next, I had some mild frustration with how the game incentivizes certain build strategies over others. Most commonly, when I was having more difficulty than usual with a boss with my usual strategy of quick attack fist weapons, I would come to find that I could pretty handily win encounters by just using the heaviest sword I could find and hitting bosses once every rotation, which wasn't as interesting of a strategy to use for me. On the topic of bosses, I also had some issue with how a lot of boss difficulty sometimes came down to "this attack will take several years to windup and mere milliseconds to actually land", as prior Fromsoft games usually reward pattern recognition instead of reaction times in their combat. Finally, visual clarity was something that could be a pain point at times. The game is gorgeous, without a doubt, but it is also very hard to tell what I'm looking at sometimes. From bosses that are so large you can't see when they're winding up their attacks because the animation occurs above your field of view (Starscourge Radahn), to animations that make it near-impossible to intuit how to properly dodge (Waterfowl Dance), to being sent to The Yellow Dimension TM by AOE attacks with effects so bright and yellow they could risk searing your retinas. I understand the want to make things more cinematic and EPIC, but it's difficult to justify that if it makes the gameplay harder for me as the player to follow.

All that being said, I still found Elden Ring's base game to be a really rewarding and fruitful experience. Once you really understand how it ticks, it's fun to "speedrun" it for 100% achievements by completing start to finish in less than four hours. Elden Ring overall feels like a massive summation of what Fromsoft has come to be known for and how they continue to improve.

Intro
There's not much I can say about this game that hasn't already been said. Not since my childhood has a video game been this impactful to me (RIP Skylanders). I initially was afraid to even approach it due to the sheer amount of content (I tend to go for 100% completion in games), but after beating Another Crab's Treasure I figured I would try and tackle its inspiration. So began my obsession for the next several months.

*SOME SPOILERS AHEAD, INCLUDING PERSONAL THEORIES*

Obviously, this is the centerpiece of any Souls game. While the massive open world has some things to do besides fighting (like NPC quests and searching through ruins), at its core is brutally challenging combat with heavy emphasis on dodging and memorization of enemy movesets. However, unlike previous Souls games, there is an absurd variety of weapons, spells, and armors to choose from in search of your perfect build. You could go for a typical greatsword or katana, or you could instead swing around a giant finger or a bouquet of roses (the latter being absolute garbage, but I digress). And what's even crazier is that if you find enough Smithing Stones to upgrade said weapons and magic sources, you could feasibly beat the game with any weapon. Personally, I went for a Medium, STR/DEX build with two curved greatswords, and my strategy alternated between powerstanced jump attacks and Bloodhound's Finesse. As long as it isn't an archery build, you have completely free reign over your character's strategy. You can even summon ghostly allies to fight, but if you do, you'll be ripped to shreds by the fanbase (which is part of the reason I stopped using them past the starting area).

What did you expect from Miyazaki and George R. R. Martin? Everything is part of a dark, interconnected web of lore that requires heavy exploration to understand (and don't feel bad if you need to look up explanations on Youtube). The overall worldbuilding borrows heavily from human history, religion, and works of literature. What's especially interesting is that it takes a very objective lens, forcing the player to decide for themselves which path is truly the most morally sound. Speaking of characters, your favorite NPCs have a good chance of getting killed off by the end, so try not to get too attached (easier said than done).

It's hard not to appreciate how FromSoft designed realistic characters and environments without feeling like they were spat out of . The environments, both visual and auditory, are very fleshed out, and everything and everyone feels alive with shadow and mystery. It's not quite as stylized as Sekiro, but instead echoes DS3 in graphical design and soundtrack elements. All-around peak. Every moment feels, sounds, and looks cinematic and edit-worthy.

Of course, no game is truly perfect, even critically acclaimed masterpieces. Some areas of the open world feel somewhat empty, especially in the DLC (which I will probably review separately). The quest system is confusing, and there's no real way to track what you're supposed to be doing. There is also no real reason to use any ranged weapons, as they are all easily surpassed by ranged spells and require constant crafting of arrows. Oh, and some of the bosses are just straight-up unfair. By unfair, I don't mean difficult (Malenia is a nightmare, but every loss to her chalks up to user error). I mean stupidly gimmicky and obnoxious. The Godskin Duo and Mogh are good examples, with the former being an annoying tag-team and the latter having an undodgeable Bleed attack during his phase transition. I absolutely love this game, but sacred cows need to be butchered every once in a while.

What started as an impulse buy after beating a small indie game that took inspiration from it turned into one of the best gaming experiences I've ever had. When I finally got that platinum trophy and took the throne as the Third Elden Lord, I felt a painful sense of "Now what?!" If you're not ready for such a grueling gauntlet, then perhaps you should reconsider. But for those who want a sense of incredible adventure and challenge, this game is an absolute must-play.

After multiple complete play-throughs I feel like this game was a blast.. The single-player content was exceptional. Bosses and exploration and story exceeded my expectations as my first foray into this genre of game. PVP is also well done to a degree, and I am a fan of how the colosseum, duels and invades have been implemented. Some of the scenes and bossfights are so cinematic and you will be hard-pressed to find a game that can compete with this on these benchmarks.

However, co-op play is actually so far behind other contemporary games such as Lords of the Fallen. The communication is limited to gestures and you only keep a portion of the runes for helping other players and keep no progression. You are also limited to summoning players in some zones and not others which I understand is not really the biggest issue and all of this I could really overlook. What truly holds it back is that there is no cross-system play, cross-system progression or anything of that ilk.

Posted: August 22
Elden Ring is one of history's best video games.

Discalimer: 400+ hours played on PS5 not listed here.

While all FromSoftware titles come with their abrasive features, those features are most often intended, and usually benefit you more than you may think at first. My initial experience with Elden Ring was mostly rage and confusion. It challenges you in many ways and answering the question of whether or not this game is for you at all can itself be difficult. Make no mistake, this game is for you.

Some FromSoftware fans believe these games are 'not for everyone'. I don't, I think that's the opposite intention behind the design decisions that make some people feel this way. I think no matter who you are, you can overcome the challenges presented to you in Elden Ring and I believe this is exactly why said challenges exist. Ever since Demon's Souls, difficult challenges have been a fundamental element of FromSoftware games, specifically to contribute to your enjoyment of them. The harder the challenge, the more satisfying the victory. And Elden Ring is particularly amazing because in its open world, you have tools to fine tune your gameplay experience. You can and should experience victory the way you want to. From the most overpowered summons to RL1 wretch no hit dance pad runs and everything in between, there are a ton of fun ways to play.

The art, environment, lore, combat, RPG elements, and animations are all superb. For $80 (SOTE Edition), you have potentially thousands of hours of enjoyment to experience, no questions asked.

Not a huge fan of the music but Metyr's theme hits right. I could think of countless of criticisms but that's primarily because I've spent hundreds of hours with it. I feel there are many justifiable criticisms but none of them prevent me loving Elden Ring how it is. Nothing's perfect, this game is no different, but what we have here is still one of the greatest of all time. An experience you can't find anywhere else.

10/10

This game (and its DLC) is super, super huge. There are some things about that that can be/are considered negative; some enemies are reused too much and sometimes it feels like there's tons of open space that ends up being superfluous.

But I think that's offset in my personal estimation by the delight in exploration, that the game keeps going. The first time you accidentally warp to a new portion of the map and there's so much uncharted territory between you and where you just were can be legitimately jaw dropping.

The gameplay itself is probably the best that the official line of Soulslikes has been (Disregarding Sekiro, I suppose); there's tons of variety. Different flavors of spells, different flavors of miracles. Tons of different weapons. Tons of ashes of war to tune weapons you like to have moves and elements you like. Lots of armor for fashion, talismans to help tweak your build, and tons of enemies (with mostly great variety) to use it all on.

Movement feels pretty good, too. Your jump feels spry, mounting/dismounting the horse is quick. Spiritsprings are good ways to gain some verticality or to safely descend.

Music feels sparse, despite there being a lot more ambient tracks compared to the game's predecessors. That's not to say that there aren't great songs (Mogh's is one of my personal favorites), but sometimes it feels like there's not as many memorable songs.

Multiplayer modes are the same as ever. There are no covenants but that's fine, with all that's going on in the game I get not wanting to try and put another layer of game on top of it (though to be honest, I think this open world structure would be a great way to integrate a gravelord-style covenant).

Biggest problems I have with the game are actually multiplayer, I suppose. Sometimes you lose the ability to summon your partner(s) somewhat arbitrarily. The fact you can't wander across thresholds of zones with a partners is a rather large irritant (big example being the caves. Lots of little caves in Limgrave you have to take your partner to, and one of you has to hop out. This is less egregious when you and your partner are both well-acquainted with the game/style of game where you can just agree to meet somewhere, but it's still pretty irritating. Seamless Co-op fixes this, naturally, but not everybody can/wants to mod their game.)
And of course invasions are the other problem. Mind you, I don't dislike invasions in general, but I will say that one of the bigger strengths of the game is how open-ended it is. From the get-go you can go to a TON of places and get a TON of equipment. However, this also means that dedicated invaders can do the same thing, and end up invading people who are either entirely new, or are running a new save, while carting around some really strong equipment/consumables. This has always been a thing, to some degree, but I feel like this is where it's the most egregious.

Still, small gripes compared to how much I adore this game.

I know that I only have two hours in this game - but it's just because I wanted to return it, I'd play more if not for Steam's policies.

This game is just plainly boring. There ways to make hard games fun. There are ways to implement difficulty in creative ways. Does this game do it? Not at all. The difficulty comes from enemies just having really good stats. Not to add, Elden Ring punishes you for not playing as intended, while not telling you what's the intended way to play. You're forced to guess what to do.

Elden Ring could have been amazing. The atmosphere is creative, I love that the classes aren't just "Warrior, mage, archer", but are actually cool (prophet, prisoner). But in the end, it turns out to be just a cash grab with boring and unimmersive animations.

I really enjoyed playing Elden Ring because it felt like an adventure where I was free to explore at my own pace. The world is huge and full of surprises.... sometimes I’d stumble into a hidden dungeon, other times I’d just stop to admire the view of the Erdtree. The combat is tough, and I died a lot, but beating bosses like Godrick or Malenia gave me a real sense of achievement. The story is mysterious, and while not everything is explained directly, characters like Melina, Ranni, and Nepheli made the journey feel meaningful. What I loved most is that the game never forced me to follow a strict path.... I could go anywhere and make my own choices. For me, Elden Ring isn’t just a game, it’s an unforgettable experience that kept me hooked from start to finish.

Elden Ring is my favorite fantasy action rpg for a number of reasons, most of all it is the kind of game that offers a first-playthrough experience I can only sum up with "How is there still more map?" FromSoftware knocked it out of the park and while there are some minorly irritating holdovers from previous games, almost every gameplay system and aspect has been overhauled and tuned to perfection. Bosses are challenging, learnable and incredibly cinematic and the tools given to the player to solve combat are vast and can accommodate almost any play style. If you haven't played it for some reason, and enjoy action RPGs, soulslikes, or just fantasy epics set in dying worlds, this game should not be missed. Also it's gorgeous, there are several times I remember stopping and thinking "no, THIS is the most beautiful vista I've ever seen" and then it just let's you explore that area. I give it a solid 10/10.

1st play through with this game was one of the most incredible experiences I've ever had as a gamer--it started as a literal horror game as I fled in terror from literally everything and scratched my way from a pathetic insect to something that could at least hold my own if I payed attention. I never felt OP, just...tempered. Battle hardened into a warrior that could make it if I wanted it enough. Then I overleveled my character and spent another hundred hours and a second play through just exploring the world. The fear of everything around me was gone and the world was beautiful when I actually had the breathing room to look at it.

Then...convergence mod. Glorious. I constructed an absolutely OP build and experienced everything the world had to offer at a leisure pace. I got to be curious and it was awesome. Then seamless co-op mod. Elden Ring is such a different experience with someone at your side the whole way. Then convergence+seamless co-op. Again, amazing. I'll hit 1,000 hours before I'm done. That's pretty damn spectacular for a single player game. Oh, and if you like the lore, you'll spend another 100 hours just watching VatiVidya videos on YouTube and you'll be happy about it.

I just wanted to have a good time. Maybe swing a sword. Maybe feel heroic.

Instead, I met Malenia, Blade of Miquella — and I haven’t known peace since.
She heals when she hits you. She dodges your hopes and dreams. And when you finally bring her down after 72 failed attempts and some light therapy... she gets back up and turns into a literal goddess of rot.

Cool. Totally fair.

I beat her once. The controller has never worked the same. Neither have I.

Then there's Radahn, who cannonballs into the battlefield like a meteor. Oh, and Mohg, who whispers “NIHIL” and drains your soul and bank account at the same time.

Every boss has two phases. Even the ones that look dead. Especially those. I don’t trust anything anymore. The birds? Bosses. That one merchant? Probably a boss. The loading screen? Definitely a boss.

I went in a boy. I came out a broken shell wearing a pumpkin helmet and screaming “TRY FINGER BUT HOLE” into the void.

Absolute nightmare of a game.
11/10 would recommend to my worst enemy.
Do not play this. You will cry, then laugh, then cry again, then say “just one more try” until the sun rises.

I’ve beaten Elden Ring seven times. Seven. Times.
And yet somehow, every time I think I’ve learned a boss pattern, a new death comes along to humbly remind me: nope, you still don’t know anything.

The world is breathtaking, the lore is deep, and the combat is brutal but that’s exactly why I keep coming back. I die, I respawn, and I absolutely lose it screaming, cursing, and flailing wildly before I finally defeat the boss and feel like a true king… only for the next opponent to humble me in seconds. Somehow, I still enjoy every agonizing second.

You feel powerful… until a random enemy reminds you that confidence is temporary. You think you’ve mastered the game… until Elden Ring proves you haven’t.
And somehow, that’s exactly why it’s brilliant.

10/10 Would die, learn nothing, and fall in love again.

Posted: August 17
A Beautiful, Challenging Adventure Worth Your Time

Elden Ring is a really good game, maybe not perfect, but definitely something special. It takes the core of what makes FromSoftware games unique and expands it into a massive, open world that’s both visually stunning and full of secrets.

The graphics and art direction are incredible. Every region has its own distinct atmosphere, from eerie swamps to golden plains and ruined cities. It’s one of those games where you’ll constantly stop just to take in the view.

Combat is tough but satisfying. It can be frustrating at times, especially with some bosses, but overcoming those challenges feels genuinely rewarding. There’s also a ton of flexibility in how you play; magic, melee, stealth, summons, you can really shape your character the way you want.

What really stands out is the sense of discovery. There are no big arrows telling you where to go, and that makes finding new areas or hidden items way more exciting. Some of the dungeons and side quests are easy to miss, but that’s kind of the charm, it rewards curiosity.

It’s not flawless. The difficulty can be a barrier, and the story is pretty cryptic unless you go out of your way to piece it together. But if you're into games that challenge you and don’t hold your hand, this one delivers.

Overall, Elden Ring is a memorable experience, visually gorgeous, deeply atmospheric, and full of moments that stick with you. Definitely worth playing.

This is the most overrated game I've ever played in my life. So many bosses just feel unfair. I feel like it's the greatest shame that dark souls became known as the "difficult game" and not the "atmospheric fantasy game" because you can tell with every boss fight that they're just trying to make the game more and more difficult with each installment. From soft is so obsessed with "difficulty" that they forgot to make the game actually fun and rewarding.

Half of the bosses move so unnaturally (morgott is the worst offender). It feels like the bosses movesets were custom designed to catch the players rolls and heals as upposed to natural attacks that the boss would use in the setting. In other dark souls games it fell like the bosses where a natural part of the world that were hard because in their setting they are supposed to be powerful. In ER it feels like the bosses know they are video game bosses so they purposefully delay their attacks in a way that doesn't feel natural

The worst part of this game are the oversized bosses who spam range attacks, have 1 hit kill moves and love to run miles away from the player at any given time(astel, fire giant, elden beast). They just straight up aren't fun.

This game was so unenjoyable it unironically made dark souls 1 and 3 more enjoyable in retrospect, so if you want to gain an appreciation of dark souls, play this. It's so unfun other games become more fun by comparison.
