Overall Rating - 9/10
Elden Ring is a MASSIVE game. There is so much to see and do within the world that it presents a truly daunting and often frightening experience to players. The atmosphere can be overwhelming at times. There is a serious sense of dread and unease, as you explore the ravaged world of The Lands Between. Haunting strings and eerie sounds reverberate through the land, and give you the sense that you are in a world that is truly sick and dying. Personally I would recommend turning the music and effects volume down quite a bit... Because after a while, the overbearing sounds of this game will effect your well-being significantly.
This brings us to the combat. While exceptionally responsive and intuitive at times, the game can be incredibly frustrating. For obvious reasons, the souls-like experience is a punishing endeavor for gamers who aren't familiar with the genre (me).

Even after 89 hours at the time of this review, I still consider myself a novice at the game. The level of complexity in builds and gear is a slap in the face from reality as you are forced to really THINK about how you approach every encounter. If you are caught lacking in this world, you can be killed by even the smallest enemy.

The need to "git gud" is both a positive and a negative for this game. For me, my biggest gripe is the need to constantly reference guides and other information online to play the game "properly." Massive portions of the game can be skipped or missed depending on a seemingly random string of events and NPC interactions that can be baffling at times. Without the internet, this game would be literally impossible to figure out on your own, except through a rigorous process of Trial and Error.

If you do not consciously manage your build, you WILL be forced to start a new playthrough to avoid all the 1000 pitfalls of your previous game.

Not for the faint of heart. But if you have literally 500+ hours to dump into a game, this is the one to consider. Would get steamrolled by Margott again.

ELDEN RING is a great game, and there are many reasons why. In this review, I’ll explain why I think it’s a masterpiece.

First, the open world. FromSoftware has always done exploration in a unique way. The game doesn’t mark your path or tell you where to go. You have to explore, get lost, and sometimes die. ELDEN RING is the same, but bigger. The map is huge, and you can keep finding new items, dungeons, and areas even after beating the game.

Next, the bosses. FromSoftware games always have great bosses, but ELDEN RING takes it further. It’s not just the bosses themselves, but where they are and how hard they are. Take Margit, the first major boss. He’s tough, but in a rewarding way. This fight teaches you what to expect from the other bosses. The bosses in ELDEN RING are also more aggressive, which fits the open-world style. In other FromSoftware games, bosses are slower and balanced to your progress. In ELDEN RING, players might be overleveled, so bosses had to be faster and more challenging.

The gameplay is amazing. The leveling system is one of the best I’ve seen. You get runes from enemies and spend them to increase your stats. You can build your character in many ways. From a wizard casting spells to a warrior with huge weapons. ELDEN RING also lets you summon spirit ashes, which are optional companions for boss fights. Everything in the game is up to you, and that freedom makes the gameplay great.

The story and lore are also amazing. It’s the most detailed FromSoftware world yet. Almost everything you see has a story. Some things are hidden, leaving space for players to make their own theories. Figuring out the lore is like solving a puzzle. Even if you don’t know all of it, the world still feels epic.

ELDEN RING is fun, challenging, creative, and mind-blowing. Exploring the Lands Between is always rewarding. The game doesn’t hold your hand, and it changes the rules of open-world games. That’s why I give ELDEN RING a 10/10.

🌟 Elden Ring – A Brutal Beauty Worth Every Hour 🌟
⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️ (Recommended)

Elden Ring was my second Souls-like, and wow—what a ride. I’ll be honest: I still have no clue what the story was about (something something Erdtree?), but that didn’t stop me from being completely immersed in its world.

🎨 Design Excellence
The game design, world design, and art direction are absolutely top-tier. Every area feels handcrafted, mysterious, and alive. From haunting ruins to sweeping landscapes, it’s a masterclass in environmental storytelling—even if I didn’t understand the actual story.

⚔️ Challenge Accepted
Some bosses were way harder than I expected (looking at you, Malenia), and it definitely took me a while to get through. But every victory felt earned. The difficulty never felt unfair—just demanding in the best way.

🎮 Steam Deck Performance
Played mostly on Steam Deck, and it ran super smooth. Honestly felt like it was made for it. Controls were tight, visuals stunning, and no major hiccups. Portable punishment has never felt so good.

💬 Final Thoughts
If you’re into challenging gameplay wrapped in a gorgeous, mysterious world, Elden Ring is a must-play. Even if you don’t follow the lore, the experience is unforgettable.

Posted: August 15
Would actually be fun if it stopped constantly peppering the player with cheap shots, and sometimes it does stop. It's also as vague and poorly communicated as humanly possible so that the only way you'll understand some of the most fundamentally important mechanics in the game are wiki-crawling or spending dozens of hours on rote trial and error.

Ignore the reddit hivemind trying to gaslight people into thinking this is a perfect experience. I got 'good'. I understood the design language, parried and dodged bosses, beat Malenia, Elden Beast, Maliketh, etc with a melee strength build and a colossal weapon. There's potential here but it squanders those possibilities so it can pretend that it's difficult with unintuitive memorization-based gameplay, boss combos that cannot be interrupted even during extended pauses with badly telegraphed switch-ups and lackluster RPG mechanics.

You can tell a lot about a game's design by examining where the most effective tactics leave you, and the most effective tactic in elden ring is to run past enemies to grab a bunch of items, stack up a ton of buffs and effects so you deal a ton of damage, and then kill the bosses as fast as mathematically possible before they start any kind of attack sequence to minimize the time spent interfacing with boss and foe mechanics as much as possible. That's not a good sign, but it can be excusable if a game has more to it beyond the core mechanical gameplay. Elden ring does not have a whole lot more to it than the core mechanical gameplay.

Compare that with the most effective tactics available in something like Doom Eternal on Nightmare difficulty - move, shoot, dodge, swap, combo, chainsaw, stagger foes you can't kill, and so on. The most effective way to play that game is to interface with all it's mechanics as much as possible and do so constantly. It's not a perfect game, but it doesn't bury it's own strengths.

There's a good game buried in Elden Ring somewhere, where combat is a mad dance of risk, retreat, aggression and dynamic play, but the game almost never lets it come to the surface. A damn shame.

Besides the absurd and still growing number of hours that I've played Elden Ring on Steam, I've logged hundreds on the PlayStation version as well. I haven't spent nearly as many in the DLC, but that count is growing slowly.

I have many gripes about Elden Ring. I enjoy most of the FS games and Bloodborne is my favorite game of all time. Imo Dark Souls III is the best Soulsborne game overall.

But nonetheless Elden Ring is special and is still one of my favorite and most played games. It's absolutely massive, both in the scale of its world and in its systemic elements. Your usual obtuse FromSoft systems and questlines are of course present, but because of its open-endedness it isn't as critical to understand them quickly as it is in their other games. And once you do, the build possibilities are nigh-endless.

It can be frustrating how hard it tries to be the next step in difficulty for the series in its use of overly long and complex movesets and an awkward scaling curve. And good lord does it rely *way* too much on floaty delayed rollcatch attacks that just look silly. But so too is it rewarding in that it gives you a lot of agency over *when* to approach said challenges, and gives you a lot of tools with which to approach them, allowing you to feel like you have solved a puzzle beyond just overleveling or "pressing buttons good"

Now if only the community would get its collective head out of its ass and stop shaming folks for *using* those various tools to help them succeed. If anything, much of what I dislike about Elden Ring is informed by the corrupting influence of western exceptionalism and its having completely missed the point of all of the valuable elements of the series beyond the aforementioned "press buttons good". There are plenty of games for that. This *can* be one of them, but treating it as the norm eschews the delightful world of systems to explore and exploit in exchange for elitist ego stroking and misplaced emphasis of reflex and memorization over patience and curiosity. I look forward to the clown reactions that this paragraph will provoke. Thanks in advance for the points. "Git gud" at having some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ empathy.

Beyond all of that, the modding community for the PC version is incredible, and has claimed most of my hours spent on said version. From balance tweaks to entirely new experiences, it is a joy seeing the mod community flourish and the possibilities continue to grow. I would recommend playing it wholly without mods for at least the first playthrough, maybe the first few. But afterwards there is a lot to gain from seeing the creativity that folks have attached to the game. It's fascinating.

If Dark Souls was a grumpy teacher and Sekiro was that strict sensei who made you cry, Elden Ring is the chaotic college professor who throws you into the open world, hands you a stick, and says: "Go fight God."

The freedom is intoxicating — sometimes literally, because after 424 hours, I’m convinced my Tarnished is addicted to Rune farming. The combat is a spicy buffet: swords, sorcery, faith builds, bleed spam… and of course, 9/10 players eventually becoming a Rivers of Blood menace at least once.

World & Exploration
This isn’t a map; it’s an art piece.
Every cliff edge hides either a breathtaking view… or a sudden death and a “You Died” screen. The game doesn’t hold your hand — it shoves you into poison swamps and expects you to thank it.

Every time you think you’ve explored everything, bam — another underground city or a hidden boss that makes you question if you should even be here at level 150.

Bosses
Elden Ring bosses are like Tinder dates — you think you’re ready, then suddenly you’re on the floor wondering what went wrong.

Margit is your first breakup.

Maliketh is the gym trainer who pushes you until you snap.

Malenia… she’s not a boss, she’s a tax on your mental health.

After hundreds of hours, you’ll know their move sets so well you could choreograph a dance routine… and still get one-shot.

Lore
It’s George R. R. Martin + Miyazaki, so naturally, half the plot is cryptic riddles and the other half is NPCs mumbling about “destined deaths” while slowly fading into depression.
You either deep dive into lore videos at 3 AM or accept that “things happen because the Greater Will said so.”

After 424 Hours…
You’ve memorized every grace location, tested 50+ builds, killed the same dragon for runes more times than you’d admit, and you can now navigate Caelid without therapy.

Would I recommend it? Yes — but only if you’re ready for:

Emotional damage

Physical exhaustion from leaning in during boss fights

And the sudden urge to scream “TAKE MY RUNE ARC” at strangers.

10/10 — Would lose another 400 hours.

I have very mixed feelings about Elden Ring. This was my first Souls game that I truly invested time into. If I judged it purely on art direction, character design, music, and cinematic quality, it’s a great game. If judge it on gameplay mechanics, I’d honestly give it a 5/10 at best.

The difficulty in Elden Ring doesn’t come from boss mechanics themselves, but from the core game mechanics. I played a double-dagger parry build without looking up guides because I wanted a rogue-like experience. However, I ran into major hitbox issues. Even when standing right next to some bosses, my daggers simply wouldn’t hit half the time. If I’m risking everything to get up close with a non STR build, my weapons should at least be able to hit at that distance.

Parrying was another frustration. Not all enemies or bosses can be parried, which is fine in theory, but the game gives no clear indication when something is immune. If there is a visual or gameplay cue, I never understood it. This meant I often had to leave the game to look up whether a boss could be parried, otherwise I’d keep assuming my timing was off and waste attempts. It is just bad design.

The camera also caused issues. Whether locked on or not, it often felt janky and unreliable. While some might call that a “skill issue,” I’d argue that after multiple entries in this genre, wrestling my camera shouldn’t be part of the skill curve imo.

The most fun I had was fighting Malenia. She can be parried, and her fight felt like a pure test of skill rather than a battle against clunky mechanics. Unfortunately, she was the only boss that gave me that sense of satisfaction after defeating her.

Elden Ring’s story exists, but it’s so fragmented and hidden that it’s hard to care. Quests often require talking to the same character multiple times before they actually give you the quest, why not just offer it immediately?

While I don’t mind open world games, this one felt underwhelming. Outside of grinding mobs for XP, there was little reason to explore. About two-thirds into the game, I started skipping most encounters because they felt like a waste of time. This compounds when you realize almost half the game is copy pasted boss fights with 1 new mechanic. Oh this dragon breathes ice instead of fire.... woooooow!!! Or they simply throw two of the same boss at you at once. It is really boring, not creative, and not fun.

Despite my complaints, I would still recommend Elden Ring because of its impact on the gaming scene and its cinematic boss fights (when the camera behaves). But for me, it ended up feeling like a glorified boss killing simulator. I didn’t care about the story, characters, or the world, only the bosses.

7/10

Elden Ring - A Testament to the Worth of Struggle

The Lands Between are a realm in slow collapse, their grandeur half-swallowed by ruin, their lords clinging to power that is already ash in their hands. In this, Elden Ring holds to a truth rarely embraced in games of such scope: that the world will not wait for you, and that it will not yield without the full measure of your will.

The now-infamous creed “git gud” is neither jeer nor dismissal, but a charge. It is the reminder that victory is not granted; it is seized through repetition, learning, and the deliberate refinement of one’s craft. In a world where hope has no firm foothold, the discipline to persist becomes its own form of nobility. Here, the game insists that we carve our way forward from a field of imperfect alternatives, and in doing so, it mirrors life’s harder truths: we are rarely given a perfect choice, yet we must choose all the same.

The open world is a marvel of breadth and density. Sweeping vistas give way to cloistered catacombs; gentle hills mask battlefields strewn with the remnants of ancient wars. From sunlit plateaus to cities drowned in shadow, the variety never falters, each new horizon a reminder of the beauty that can survive amid ruin. The bosses, varied in form, terrible in power, stand as both trials and teachers, their defeat earned only through mastery born of repeated failure.

Quests here are not marked in bright ink. They vanish into the current if you fail to grasp them in time, because Elden Ring understands that in the real world, events move inexorably forward without our consent. Life is not a checklist. It is an experience, and one that is fragmentary, partial, and often unresolved. We act with imperfect knowledge, in imperfect ways, toward imperfect ends. And this, the game whispers, is not merely inevitable…. it is enough.

In its beauty, its depth, and its merciless clarity, Elden Ring is a reminder that hope is not the absence of despair, but the practice of striving in spite of it. That is its gift: a vision of the world not as we wish it, but as it is, and the assurance that even there, the struggle is worth the cost.

I love Elden Ring, it feels like a solid throwback to the golden age of open world RPGs like Elder Scrolls, Fable, the Witcher, Dragon Age... etc. but with a little Soulsborn infusion. This leads to a refreshing take on a classic genre that has been experiencing a drought of stand out games in recent years.

The sound design is incredible, soft strings play while you explore the detailed world and bold brass instruments blare with thundering drums when you encounter intense boss fights, the music aligns really will with what is happening in the game world. Every swing, spell and action the player makes has a unique and satisfying sound to everything that is solidly pleasing. The graphics are likewise very well done. Every environment is unique and has its own character and beauty, you never want to stop exploring this game world.

The game-play is classic Soulsborn action aligning with the Dark Souls series. I will say that it feels a little easier than the Dark Souls titles, which has led to many fans whining on the forums about how this game is a watered down version of Dark Souls. However, I feel like that's kind of the point? This is a new IP for Fromsoft, so why would it matter that the game-play has a few more elements that make the game just a little more welcoming to newcomers? Don't let the nay-sayers fool you, this game can still be unforgiving, but it never got to the point where i was throwing my controller against the wall like in the Dark Souls titles. Part of the reason things feel easier is the open world setting allows you to leave a difficult boss fight, level up a bit more and come back to it with a much stronger character and a fresher mind. The other soulsborn titles being more linear were much less enjoyable to grind compared to Elden Ring.

My only gripe with Elden Ring (and this may just be my personal lifestyle so I get that) is the game is setup in a way that makes it hard to exist "outside" of the game world. You can't pause this game, a game that is mostly single player, which makes no sense to me. I am a father with a child and have a full time job that demands my attention outside of Elden Ring. I never know when my child may need me and I have to plop my controller down and run to their aid right away, without a pause and with much going on in the game world, that means dropping all my progress (sorry but I will always choose my family over gaming haha). Likewise, there is no quest log or anything keeping track of the side quests in this game, this isn't an issue if you play regularly, there's not a whole lot of side quests in this game. However, sometimes life gets busy and I have to put the controller down for a week or so and when I finally get back to Elden Ring, I find myself forgetting where I left off.

Overall, Elden Ring is a solid 9.5/10 for me, there really isn't much that is done wrong in this title, its fun, refreshing, there's no micro transaction bloat, it runs well, its beautiful and it will hold your interest until the end with lots of replayability. The only reason its not a 10/10 title for me is just the few quality of life additions that I think every single player action RPG should have. Although I do admit again it could just be my personal lifestyle not aligning with the game wanting to keep the player hooked at all times.

I would only mildly recommend this game, which is odd, considering how many hours I've been playing it!

On one hand, it's an amazingly detailed an immersive world. The adversaries are well placed and interesting, and the locations are fascinating.

On the other hand, I found myself almost constantly consulting web sites outside the game to figure out what to do next. NPC quests, in particular, aren't well done. The NPC shows up somewhere random in the huge world, and the chances of blundering into them at the right time is pretty much nil.

There is an astonishing amount of lore underpinning the game, but really, too much to follow who was whom's consort, what battles were fought, etc. I wondered if I had played other From games if I would have known more of it.

The only other drawback was the level cliffs. I'd be pumping along, finding the game pleasantly difficult, then run into an impossible boss blocking my way to the next area. Since I'd already cleared out the previous area, then followed lots of grinding runes to get to a high enough level to take on the boss. That broke me out of the flow of the game.

Back to the plus side, I found the combat system easy to understand and straightforward.

Leveling was also straightforward and easy to use.

Another small caveat - I played as an astrologer/mage, and there weren't a lot of goodies around to motivate me, not like melee players.

Amazing game.

I played Sekiro and Nioh 1/2 as well. Comparing those titles leads to following ratings:
- Open world and exploration: ER is the clear winner.
- Combat: ER loses here to Sekiro and Nioh.
- Story: Good story overall in ER, but I liked the story in Sekiro more. Nioh is utter trash in this regard.
- Boss designs: Amazing in ER, but also in Sekiro and Nioh imo

The open world design compensates all other flaws. The most fascinating thing in ER is that you are not forced to do most of its content or follow a strict path, because you are thrown into the open world and have to decide yourself what you wanna do. Usually, I didn't like open worlds because of its repetitive nature and the uninspired world design I've seen in other games before, but ER is different. In each corner, there is possibly a dungeon with powerful items for your build or a camp of creatures protecting a treasure. Exploration is rewarded in an unique way, which I haven't seen like this before.

Amazing work, FromSoftware.

I know its been repeated countless times, but Elden Ring is truly an utterly amazing video game. Areas where it struggles are made null by the dozens of aspects it excels in. I decided to buy Elden Ring well after the DLC came out because I was initially skeptical as to whether I would enjoy it, as a new player to the souls genre, but wow, this game can captivate you with its mesmerizing storytelling and gorgeous scenic vistas. Above all else, the combat in Elden Ring is excellent, not only do many bosses and even enemies humble even the longest veterans, overcoming said challenges make you feel so incredibly powerful. That being said, the combat difficulty is still approachable to new players by providing items like spirit ashes and player summons. One of my favourite parts of this game is the customization and builds, there are so many different ways to play the game which allows players to enjoy replaying the game. My biggest complaint is the optimization when playing on PC, there are some scenarios throughout the game that can cause some lower end computers and laptops to struggle, and I feel like the graphical settings are minimal. Ultimately, still an outstanding game and I look forward to FromSoftware's next title.

Miyazaki’s masterpiece.

Elden Ring represents the pinnacle of the Soulsbourne genre. Every technological advancement and design lesson learned through nearly a decade of Dark Souls has filtered into one of the best action RPGs of all time. This isn’t just a great video game, it’s a defining work.

What I love most about Elden Ring is the sheer responsiveness of the combat. While Dark Souls often felt weighty, lending to a sense of power and intention behind your actions, Elden Ring does still lock you into animations - butit’s only for a fraction of a second. You can swing and nearly immediately roll away, and stamina regeneration is much quicker than in the Souls games. This improves gameplay by making you feel you’ve always got a chance to evade an incoming attack.

This does allow for some leniancy, since you can now course correct mistakes. But the increased speed of the game and high damage output of enemies provides a unique challenge in their own right. You rarely, if ever, feel like a fight is out of your hands. The addition of jumping also makes you feel more agile, and no longer glued to the ground while trying to clear half a metre gaps in the terrain.

Elden Ring is, artistically, one of the most striking games I’ve ever played. The exaggerated colour grading gives a feeling of tension and drama in each of the game’s biomes, and it reproduces some of most intense colours I’ve seen on my monitor. The bright yellow hues of the Erdtree rain down onto the terrain like lightening, while the dark shadows of trees violently sweep across the landscape during rain storms. I’d avoid using raytracing though, not just for the performance hit, but the softer shadows suck much of the drama out of particularly scenes.

It feels like FromSoftware left nothing behind when developing Elden Ring. There are lush forests, icey tundras, mustafar-esque lava wastelands and swamps that aren’t just constant poison damage. My favourite location is Miquella's Haligtree: essentially a giant treehouse with a castle built into it. You climb across branches to scale the huge towers, and have to fight past ant colonies working on the structure, as well as the ancient knights inside. Not to mention it culminates in a hell of a fight against Melania. I also love the royal capital, Leyndell, which features a dragon frozen in stone mid-fight casting a long shadow over the entire city. The dragon’s wings feature beautiful tesselation and texturing, and scaling up them was one of many jawdropping moments I had in the game.

My only real criticism of Elden Ring is performance. My RTX 3070m died on duty trying to run this game on lowest settings, and even on a fresh machine, I had to contend with frequent traversal stutters. The game looks brilliant for it, but frame times are all over the place while moving around the world. Reducing shadow quality can cause a distracting flickering too, meaning I’d struggle to advocate people playing Elden Ring on bottom spec machines - which is a shame. Even the consoles struggle to hit a consistent 60fps, which is detrimental to the game’s unrelenting pace.

Elden Ring is packed full of content, and while some bosses do repeat, the context is always changed. One early game boss fight will sometimes lead to a late-game fight against two of them, or an adjusted version or movesight. You’re rarely fighting the exact same boss twice (unless they’ve become the dreaded common enemy later down the road.). Besides, some of these bosses are so magical that it’s a pleasure to fight them twice.

Judging by the consistently superb quality of Elden Ring, I pray the developers are getting to see their families now, comforted by the fact they’ve created one of the best (and now highest selling!) games ever made. I cannot possibly envision this being topped.

I’ve spent 104 hours in Elden Ring, completed all the endings, and earned all 42 achievements—and what a journey it has been. Each ending had its own charm and weight, but the Ranni ending truly stole my heart. It felt poetic, bittersweet, and perfectly fitting for the kind of Tarnished I became over the course of my adventure.
One thing that really stood out to me was how farming runes often felt more difficult than fighting some bosses. I can’t count how many times I died to fall damage compared to actual boss fights—it became almost comical after a while. That said, the difficulty spikes were real, especially at the start. I’ll be honest: the very first boss felt brutally unfair, not because of its stats, but because I had no clue about the game’s mechanics, attributes, or which weapons would serve me best. Those early hours were a humbling lesson in patience and persistence.
As I progressed, though, the combat, builds, and systems started to click, and I found myself enjoying the challenge more and more. Surprisingly, by the time I reached the final bosses like Radagon and even Malenia—yes, that Malenia—they felt way easier than expected. I’m still not sure if it was better gear, more skill, or just sheer familiarity with the game’s flow, but it made those climactic fights feel less intimidating and more like a well-earned victory lap.
Elden Ring isn’t perfect—some pacing quirks and that brutal early learning curve stand out—but it’s easily one of the most rewarding experiences I’ve had in gaming. From breathtaking landscapes to unforgettable battles and stories, it’s a journey I’ll remember for a long time. Highly recommended.

If you're experiencing ~1 second stutters in the game, this review has a fix for it.
Basically, to get rid of it, you need to launch the game in offline mode:
1. Right-click Elden Ring in your library -> Manage -> Browse Local Files -> Game folder.
2. Rename start_protected_game.exe to start_protected_game.bak.exe.
3. Copy eldenring.exe and rename the copy to start_protected_game.exe.

Now the game will run in offline mode, and the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Easy Anti-Cheat won’t boot up.

Elden Ring is everything I have wanted in an open-world game and it sure as hell delivers.

The world is massive to the point where there are secret zones within secret zones within secret zones the game just never ends despite finishing the main storyline. The world is beautiful, I must say I am very impressed with the art direction of this game. Every area feels unique, and exploration is constantly rewarded. Combat is challenging but fair, with tons of build variety, but we all know there is one answer when it comes to build variety (blasphemous blade).

Boss fights are epic and unforgettable. I will not forget entering Radagon's arena and feeling maximum glory as his amazing OST plays. The replay value in this game is also very notable as I have now finished it 3 times and there are different endings with different pre-requisites in terms of questlines and boss runs(newgame plus really works wonders). 76 hours 3 playthroughs and the 100% acquired. Probably my favourite game of all time? I cannot wait to play the DLC!

A Journey That Leaves You Hollow and Whole All at Once

I didn’t expect Elden Ring to stay with me the way it did.
When I stepped into the Lands Between, I wasn’t thinking about fate or endings or who I would become. I just walked. And somehow, somewhere along that long road of blood and grace and quiet nights, the game stopped being a game. It became a place—one I inhabited the way you inhabit a memory: unconsciously, then completely.

My first ending was the Perfect Order.
Even now I’m not sure why I chose it. Maybe it was because Melina asked—not with force, not with grand speeches, but with that calm, unwavering voice of hers. She never demanded destiny from me. She simply stood beside the fire, offered her strength, and walked until her steps ran out. When she burned, something in me steadied, like my purpose finally had weight. The throne wasn’t a prize. It was a debt. A quiet repayment for a life that carried me further than I knew how to walk alone.

And the journey there…
It wasn’t just bosses and runes. It was people who lived their brief lives alongside mine—Alexander bursting open with a laugh so loud it seemed to echo after he was gone; Juno Hoslow, chasing honor until honor hollowed him out. These figures should have been small in a world that large, but their absence left entire regions feeling colder. That’s the kind of loneliness Elden Ring understands: the kind that arrives after the noise fades.

My final ending was nothing like the first.
No law, no throne, no dazzling tree holding the sky together. Just Ranni’s hand in mine, and the two of us walking into a night that felt bigger than the world we left behind. There was no triumph in it—only an unfamiliar quiet. The Golden Tree shrank behind us until it became a star, and then a memory of a star. I didn’t feel like a ruler anymore. I felt like someone who, for once, chose his own story.

That’s the strange beauty of Elden Ring:
it tells you everything without telling you anything.
It shows you a world collapsing in on itself, then trusts you to understand it in your own way. There were moments when I rested at a Site of Grace and Melina sat across from me, the silence between us somehow heavier than any boss fight. There were nights in Nokron that felt like I had fallen into someone else’s dream. There were winds across the Altus fields that made the ruins look alive.

But what stays the longest are the small things—the graves hidden under a golden leaf, the rusted sword leaning against a nameless wall, the footsteps you leave behind without realizing they’ll never be yours again.

When I finished the game, I didn’t feel victorious.
I felt… shifted. Like something had rearranged itself quietly inside me. I put the controller down, but the world didn’t let me go. It still hasn’t.

Sometimes I close my eyes and see Melina framed by firelight; hear Alexander’s laugh like he’s still charging forward; feel the cold starlight of Ranni’s realm wrapping around everything I thought I knew about endings. These aren’t just scenes. They’re the residue of a journey I lived.

Elden Ring doesn’t vanish when the credits roll.
It lingers—like the afterglow you see when you stare at the sun too long. It leaves you hollow in the way loss does, and whole in the way great stories do. You can replay it, yes. You can change choices. But the first time—the first scar, the first miracle, the first fire—belongs to a version of you that will never exist again.

Maybe that’s why it hurts.
Maybe that’s why it’s beautiful.

And when the screen finally fades to black and the room goes quiet, you realize the quiet isn’t empty.
It’s everything you’ve lived there, still whispering back.

“I thought it was overhyped… now I’m overleveled.”

This was my very first Soulslike game. For months, I avoided it because I thought, “It’s just internet hype, no way it’s THAT good.” Then I gave it a chance… and 200+ hours later, here I am with 100% achievements. The world is massive, the combat is brutally satisfying and every boss fight feels like a mix of pain and adrenaline. I went from barely surviving against a Tree Sentinel to casually running through late-game areas like I own the Lands Between. Shadow of the Erdtree made me fall in love with it all over again, gorgeous new locations, insane bosses and that perfect blend of challenge and reward that makes you want to keep going even after dying another 200+ times.

I used to laugh at people who said Elden Ring was the best game of all time.
Now I’m one of them.. I came for curiosity, stayed for the suffering and left as Elden Lord.

11/10 would suffer again.

EDIT: I'm coming back to play with my wife.

Completed 10 August, 2025

Man its hard to put how beautiful this game is in words but Imma try, I got Elden Ring a few years ago and wasn't really a fan of the "soulslike" genre. Watching the 1997 berserk and reading the manga is what made me want to play Elden Ring (and dark souls 3) because the world building is very similar, this game was a great journey, exploring the lands between and admiring its beauty was so fun. It's hard to believe I would've missed out on such a beautiful world and story if I hadn't decided to play this game after reading berserk. Limgrave has to be the best starting area in any game, Caelid was absolute hell, Liurnia and Leyndell were beautiful and Haligtree... was Haligtree... (I hate the spear guys). The combat and bossfights we're great too, I still remember fighting Margit the first skill-check boss because it was so well placed and the arena was memorable. I went for 3 endings: age of duskborn, lord of frenzied flame and age of stars. Something about lord of frenzied flame stood out a lot to me, "killing everything in this cursed world to free it from its existence" type shi. Anyways if yall haven't played Elden Ring yet now's a better time than any.

Posted: August 8
I want to start this review by saying I don't hate elden ring, i've played through the game plenty of times, unmodded, modded, challenges, etc, and i've had my fair share of fun (at times) throughout my time with this game.

However, Elden ring has introduced some new issues to the fromsoft series that, at least for me, are too significant for me to ignore as a long-time fan of their games.

For starters, i would like to talk about what Elden ring did well.

Elden ring marked the first souls/souls adjacent game to shift to a full open-world format, allowing the player much more freedom than their other games.

Elden ring also introduced a ton of incredibly helpful new combat mechanics like the return of powerstancing, guard counters, a dedicated jump button, and multiple new weapon types.

However, all of these new features came with their fair share of issues. Powerstancing is more limited than what it was in ds2 (and I'm not someone who will tell you that was a good game, mind you), guard counters are useful but can often become useless in the face of most bosses unless you use a spear and shield, and although new weapon types are a good addition, elden ring suffers from a lack of weapon variety if you care about the stats or weapon art as there are a few that dominate their weapon class heavily. (Moonveil,RoB for katanas, Messmer's spear for greatspears, blasphemous blade for greatswords, rellana's for light greatswords, and other similar weapons) and other weapons that have every right to be good just suck because the devs didn't give them good stats or scalings (Hand of malenia, for example)

However, the largest issue elden ring suffers from is power creep. over the years fromsoft's bosses have gotten harder, but elden ring marks a point for me where it strays from the "tough but fair" style of design i've come to know from previous games and starts to feel oppressive and unfair. Bosses give you far less time to attack, push you aggressively, punish you any time you heal by reading the animation the moment it starts, and often fall into incredibly long attack strings that prevent you from doing anything but dodge repeatedly and wait until the boss decides it's your turn to play the game. these issues only got significantly worse in the DLC and turned what should have been the best and most enjoyable dlc in fromsoft's history into an exercise in masochism and "how long can i bang my head on this brick wall before i get irreparable brain damage" Compare this to DS3 where no boss was particularly egregious and always gave you fair options to punish them.

Consort Radahn is the pinnacle of this powercreep, having all of these issues dialed up to 11, i have a review on SoTE if you'd like to hear my thoughts on that, but there's little for me to say that hasn't been said already by most in the community. the boss design they've shifted to is not fun nor fair, and i hope they can find a balance between this and their older titles when the next game rolls around.

The way i've come to describe this game is that it's a good game, just one that doesn't want you to enjoy it or experience as much of it as you may want. there are many optional areas of the game that require a lot of work to get to with steps often so complicated it'd be almost impossible to figure out how without looking it up first. this alongside the open world making fromsoft's classic linear character questlines incredibly easy to fail without realizing and the world can often feel empty unless you go out of your way to play the way the game wants you to without deviation, at least DS3 made it easy to follow questlines comparatively.

10/10 world, 10/10 Lore, 10/10 developer.
3/10 boss design, 2/10 Build variety, 0/10 design decisions

Posted: August 8
Story: 4/5 - overwhelming, extensively detailed lore. Minus one because a lot of the details are buried in item descriptions, of which many simply describe the item usage and are unrelated to lore, so why would you read all of it? Without deliberately digging into every lore pocket, it was hard for me to connect the pieces of the background without turning to the internet lore dumps.

Music: 3/5 - decent, unintrusive and mostly relevant to the context, but there's no particular theme that I can think of to put in the background and chill.

Graphics: 5/5 - STUNNING. Especially on the maximum allowed settings, the way the light hits the scenery will take your breath away. I did try playing on lower settings while streaming and it greatly detracted from the experience. Highly recommend max ray tracing settings, if possible.

Gameplay: 5/5 - beautifully crafted progression. From the beginner area where you're given unobtrusive hints on how the game and this world works, to adapting to the more intense combat zones later in the game, a flawless experience that gently guides you to success. Even for those not so adapt in combat, the open world system lets you explore optional areas to get stronger before returning to harder fights, ensuring that you can never truly get stuck.

Combat: 4/5 - hard to evaluate, since I'm not good at it. I had a lot of fun with a heavy weapon strength-based build, but didn't really get into the spells and incantations (yet). To me the bosses were solidly designed, with the optional ones truly challenging your game sense, and the main bosses being slightly more predictable and less challenging so that everyone can reach the end. This might not be so desirable for hardcore Dark Souls players, but I truly appreciated it as a complete newbie to the franchise. (Though I did clear everything eventually, even Malenia.) The only downside is that a lot of the optional dungeons had repetitive bosses, making going through them to collect unique items truly a grind.

Overall: 4/5 - strongly recommend. I've heard it's a great introduction to the souls series in terms of difficulty, and as someone inadept at combat-based games, I whole-heartedly agree. Combat aside, just walking through the sceneries of the land is such a treat. I just wish I could take a walk without getting a heart attack from a sudden dragon landing.

Posted: August 8
The memories of joy from playing this game are what caused me to buy a gaming pc (well that and other things). This is a game most studios can only dream of making. Thank you to the team behind the making of this wonderful experience.

Now let's really review this game.

Is it worth the price? Yes. Simple as that. But it's important to note that as much as love this game, I can be honest and admit the flaws it presents. I believe even 10/10 games have flaws, but their other aspects more than make up for it which is what warrants the 10/10.

So where does this game mis-step? Let's start with item # 1: The Story

The story is at best, incomprehensible. Before DS fanboys say "but that's how the story has always been!". That doesn't cut it anymore. The questilines are impossible to follow without guides unless you write things down manually, and for most gamers, they don't have time for that or it's boring, let's be honest. The giant open world is mistreated by this lack of story and quest development.

Issue #2: Enemy design. This game is filled to the brim with stellar bosses, creepy enemies and fights that truly leave you satisfied when you stomp a scumbag boss, Sadly, that feeling is more often trumped by the thought of "oh, another ulcerated tree spirit". The repeat, reskin and random duo bosses do not add to the overall enjoyment. The repeat bosses can be fun (crucible knights when not together are a blast). For all the absolute awesome enemy and boss designs, I feel like fromsoft added two boring re-skin bosses.

Issue #3: The upgrade system. For melee players, you really are stuck with one weapon for a good portion of the game. The placement of the smithing stones and gloveworts (seriously? two different gloveworts to collect?) is just, I don't know what the intent was, sometimes it feels like they placed these items so deep in the game to simulate progress.

With all that said, I love this game. It is just a blast and it's easy to say "okay I'm done....wait whats that over there?". Completing wickedly difficult dungeons and bosses will have you tensed up and then celebrating. And lastly when you finally get your character sufficietnly leveled, you can do some godly stuff. I went from Elder Scrolls (Morrowind, Oblivion, and Skyrim), and while this is a completely different experience, I can easily sink 50+ hours into both open world RPGs. Elden Ring is an achievment for FromSoft, I just hope for the next game they hone in on making it a more 'caged' experience with a gripping story, exciting questlines, and more excellent boss battles. Buy this game.

Posted: August 8
Maybe the best modern RPG / open world on the market. Period.

UPDATE(s):
- 200+ hours in, DLC done, preparing for NG+. A true spectacle from start to finish.
- 150ish hours in, got the DLC, maybe the best DLC I've ever seen.
- 130 hours in, got all the achievements. Nowhere near as grindy as, say, Dark Souls trilogy. No NG+ is required if you know what you're doing.

REVIEW
For reference, I played, completed and loved Skyrim, Zelda BOTW, TOTK, and The Witcher 3, among others. Nothing compares to this one in terms of scope, atmosphere, polish, variety, overall spectacle.
It can lean a bit too much on the diabolically hard side of the difficulty spectrum, but the visual marvel and the unfathomable amount of things to do and places to see is what keeps you going.
Only downside (for me at least) is the absence of some sort of logbook; I understand Fromsoft's decision to not include any form of quest tracker, but I wish side quests weren't so obtuse, one-time-only/blink-and-you'll-miss-it NPC dialogue with vague hints to where to go and what to do. Emphasis on "vague". I have terrible memory for names, people and places - and an even worse case of FOMO - so It's a shame I have to play this masterpiece with Fextralife opened on my phone constantly.
Also, the crafting system is quite dull and obviously an afterthought, but that's an issue in most open world games, not just this one.
That being said, I'm loving the game to death (100+ hours played and surely 100+ to come), 10/10, one of the best experiences I've ever head in gaming.

I am going into this stating that while I have played "souls-like" games before, I have never actually played a game made by FROMSOFT games.

TL;DR Version: If you are not a masochist, don't play this game. It will torture you, test your sanity, and make you incredibly stressed out. This game isn't actually 'difficult', it's rigged against you, and takes pleasure from your pain.

....
My Rating: 6 of 10
....

The game ultimately has a very uninteresting story, despite how deep the lore is for the world. You are Tarnished, your goal is to become Elden Lord, and you must slaughter everyone and their mother (literally) to achieve it. Unsurprisingly, with George R. R. Martin helping in world building, the game ends up feeling like an episode of Game of Thrones, leaving you wondering what character, that you might even have a sliver of emotional attachment to, will die next and in what horrible context.

Possibly the main reason why everyone plays this game, the emphasis on frantic combat, would be less jarring if the more complex enemy mechanics were introduced gradually over time, to allow a player to become better accustomed to what is expected to them. Admittedly this does happen to a degree, because enemies in later zones do gain access to a greater array of skills and spells, however, it's all rather unforgiving for first time players trying to learn how this game works.

Enemies, especially of the tarnished/human variety, can literally just dodge most of what you do anyways (with infinite stamina to boot), can cast however much and as far as they might like (there is a fall off, but it's waaaaaaay longer than the tarnished), and they also have an greater attack and retaliation speed than the player.

While the player is forced into a particular set of rules and limitations, the NPC's freely break those, creating an uneven playing field almost perpetually during combat, leaving the player at a constant disadvantage.

Despite environments and NPCs being a graphically beautiful, the character, and customization is left wanting. Personally, I spent about an hour trying to make a normal looking face, only to change another slider ever so slightly and end up with an abomination. Outfits are neat, but... cosmetically I cannot wear one set, and gain the bonuses of another that I'm looking for. It's kind of a minor thing, but it'd be nice.

Finally the perception that you have a choice when it comes to spells/incantations/skills/weapons, is that you actually don't have a choice. That is to say, you can make a choice about what spell/incantation/skill/weapon you wish to use, but it's likely wrong. When enemies (especially bosses, looking at you Malenia) can dodge even Night Comet (a spell that cannot be 'seen' by NPCs), then what's the point of being a spell caster. The long wind-up spells leave you vulnerable in a game where you cannot stop moving, being dodged despite having spent an enormous amount of FP to cast a spell, and being forced to use a melee weapon over a spell, because that is the only way you'll actually do damage, is, in not so many words, bullcrap.

So look, I get it. To all those who love this game, love this genre, I understand. You like putting your balls in a vice and crushing them. That is so cool. But for casual players, this game is not one I can recommend. It's incredibly stressful, the rewards are not rewarding, and while you may end up sinking 170+ hours into the game to actually get somewhere, it's not really that good of a game, and you can spend that time better elsewhere.

Best of Luck!
-Eshki

When I first moved through the misty fields and crumbling castles of Elden Ring, I felt like I’d stumbled into something truly epic. Every discovery—from hidden dungeons to cryptic lore fragments—gave me that “whoa” moment that marked it as a world-defining experience.

Then came Shadow of the Erdtree in June 2024. It’s not just an add-on—it’s a concentrated dose of everything that made the original so magical. The Realm of Shadow, with its towering ruins and vertiginous pathing, elevated exploration to a whole new level of vertigo and wonder.

Unlocking pockets of lore about Miquella was deeply rewarding, nudging me to slow down and piece together the story in my own way.

The new bosses? Brutally memorable. Their designs and difficulty felt like pure FromSoftware DNA—tough, beautiful, unrelenting. Plenty of players echo that sentiment:

“Shadow of the Erdtree is a remarkable expansion…”
“It’s a whole new game that elevates everything Elden Ring presented”

But it’s not all sunshine in the shadow. A fair number of players felt let down by empty expanses and repeated assets. Some areas felt more scenic backdrop than meaningful exploration, and a few reused bosses and gloomy zones left folks wanting more nuance.

Still, if Elden Ring was a forest wildfire of ambition and beauty, Shadow of the Erdtree is the embers refusing to die—an epilogue that feels grand without overstaying its welcome.

In short:

Elden Ring is a masterpiece that redefined open-world RPGs.

Shadow of the Erdtree takes that magic deeper, offering a rich, dense, and often breathtaking extension—flawed, perhaps, but still worth every Tarnished step.

Posted: August 7
This game was pretty much my introduction to FromSoftware; I had played a little Dark Souls III, but I failed to git gud and it kinda just frustrated me. This game is different; this game is legendary. A lot of people throw around the term "masterpiece" these days, but this game deserves the title.

The open world element in this game is what allowed me to spend so many hours in it. As opposed to the Dark Souls series, which sort of forces you down a certain path, this game allows for a ton of exploration. Is a boss kicking your ass? Just come back later and find some new weapons and trinkets and gather some runes. You'll come back and beat him no problem.

The combat is fantastic. Everything is really responsive and the attacks give great feedback. It was hard to even really be mad when I died, since it was my fault pretty much every time. This game will force you to git gud, or git out. The variety in weapons and move sets associated with them makes every fight a joy.

The graphics are incredible. Some of the views of the landscape really made me stop and appreciate the world that FromSoftware and George R.R. Martin put together. So much fantasy is just Lord of the Rings reworked, but the Lands Between feel different and unique; the aesthetic that FromSoftware has accomplished here is amazing.

The story and lore are incredibly interesting. While the storytelling takes on the usual FromSoftware format and is primarily told through the world (not a whole lot of direct storytelling), it's wildly wondrous. It can be a bit hard to know what's going on at the beginning, but once you begin to understand the state of the Lands Between, the setting is, quite honestly, enchanting.

I've long felt that many major video game companies are going downhill; the passion and love for games seems to be missing from many studios. FromSoftware is the exception. This game is phenomenal and easily the best game I've played in recent years. If FromSoftware continues on this road, they'll put out many more great releases; I look forward to seeing what they release in the years to come.

I very rarely pay full price for a game, but do yourself a favor and pick this one up; if it's on sale for any amount, it's certainly worth the price. But beware, the Lands Between are not for the faint of heart.

The greatest single player open-world game of all time, bar none. With its fun gameplay, intriguing lore, awesome scale, and beautiful aesthetic, Elden Ring is an unforgettable experience that feels fresh with every New Game+. On my fourth playthrough (I'm a Xbox to PC guy) and I'm still learning new things and uncovering new secrets in The Lands Between, let alone the sequel-sized DLC: Shadow of the Erdtree (easily top 5 DLC in history). Even though the difficulty is punishing and at times borderline unfair (cough*Promised Consort Radahn*cough), most of the big boss battles are intense, epic, and rewarding. The mini bosses aren't half bad either when their not repeats (if I see another Erdtree Avatar ever again I am going to crash out). Even if your stuck on the most difficult bosses, you can always either level up, re-spec, or cheese the ever-loving crap out of them using the right tools and techniques; the freedom this game gives you is unparalleled by any other FromSoftware game. You truly feel like a lowly Tarnish trying to make a name for yourself in an unforgiving and decaying land abandoned by a false god. By nothing else but the sweat of your brow and the help of strangers you find along your path, you rob the demigods of their great runes so that you may challenge their god, mend the ELDEN RING, and rebuild the Lands Between in your own image...but at what cost?

Truly a 2075 retirement home classic.

I didn’t like the game.
I was excited for the game so much that I pre-ordered it and haven’t completed the game until now.

The first 5-10 hours was a great experience—cool world with amazing graphics, well designed bosses, and a big map to explore.

The game was perfect until the honey phase ended. The game started to be repetitive. Too many bosses are a clone from a mob with the same moves and more health, or they will use some unique bosses as mobs that you encounter in the world when exploring or in a dungeon. I hate it so much it kills the fun for me. I already killed those bosses why do I need to fight them again????

I hate that the game tries forcing you to use the spirits in the majority of the bosses. What happened to the OGA BOGA builds and fighting the boss 1v1?

Most of the bosses are easy, not a challenge. Didn’t even need to farm souls to level up there is no need. This made me sad, cause in all the Dark Souls series I was enjoying the suffering of farming, killing hard mobs to be fit to challenge the boss who will make me memorize every move he will do.

The exploring was a waste of time. The world is boring. You have only dungeons that have copies of bosses and too many churches that are copy pasted. The map is so big and empty that I gave up on exploring and started speedrunning the game to end my suffering.

Some positives to the game:
The Ash of War system is great. Didn’t try it much myself, but I do like the idea of having a nice weapon that I like with my favorite skill equipped on it.
The NPC quests were good and easy to follow on the map.
There are some puzzles here and there that peaked my interest.
"I AM MALENIA. BLADE IF MIQUELLA. AND I HAVE NEVER KNOWN DEFEAT."


Overall, my experience was awful. That doesn’t mean the game is bad it just isn’t for me. I only completed it to play Nightreign.

6/10.

Elden Ring is legendary. But let me be clear, this is not a casual stroll. If you're not wired to grind, learn, and “get good,” if the idea of facing a boss again and again until you earn that victorious rush doesn't excite you… then this isn't your game.

This masterpiece demands more than just your time, it demands commitment. The world is stunning, the lore deep, and the combat unforgiving.

But for those who thrive on challenge and live for that moment of triumph after hours of struggle, Elden Ring delivers something rare, pure and satisfying.

I'd play it a million times over. But fair warning, this is a test of perseverance and passion. Not for the faint of heart, and certainly not for the impatient.

Only the truly committed will know its beauty.

Elden Ring wasn’t just a game to me — it was a life-changing experience.

I’ve always been the type to play only competitive games or casual titles with friends. Single-player games, open worlds, RPGs? Never appealed to me. Even GRRM’s works never clicked... But when the Elden Ring DLC was announced, I decided to give the base game a shot after all, it was all anyone talked about.

And what a decision that was.

There’s a version of ‘me’ before Elden Ring, and another after. It was my first completed single-player game, my first 100%, and the game that made me understand the magic of non-competitive experiences. What a masterpiece. Every detail hooked me: the immersion of its open world, the boss fights that felt like a deadly dance, the incredibly rich lore...

I’m not exaggerating when I say Elden Ring rewrote my taste in games. Thanks to it, I now love the soulslike genre and even other types of single-player games. Elden Ring didn’t just ‘entertain’ me it taught me what it means to truly love playing something.

Elden Ring is fundamentally Skyrim a game about riding a bulldozer horse around a beautiful fantasy theme park world covered in lewd helpful messages left by other players. You'll generally wander the world following the so-called guidance of grace until you find a Dark Souls-esque dungeon area ultimately containing a boss with something you need to advance. Or you can wander out into the wilderness and murder sheep or get eaten by a dog the size of an elephant, it's largely up to you!

Good points!
It's fun to explore.
It's beautiful. Some angles and routes are definitely made to showcase the Erdtree or some of the cool scenery.
The combat is generally enjoyable dark-souls style fare. If you've played From's games, you'll largely be comfortable here.
There's quite a variation in areas.
The background lore is weird.
You can fight from your horse/bulldozer.
There are loads of weapons and you can largely play as you want.
You can summon help (or players if you're feeling brave) for most bosses or difficult areas.
There is the possibility of respec, but it's locked behind a fairly hefty boss.

Bad points!
If you're very bad at From's combat, you'll find no love here, particularly in the bosses, and particularly at the , which feels like a massive jump in difficulty. The push to the second mandatory boss through the castle also feels like it's more difficult than any other part of the game. If, like me, you're just quite bad at it, you can defeat every boss in this game with some effort and help from your trusty ashes.
Some of the bosses are miserable experiences if you're not built for them. Fortunately it's mostly the optional bosses, and coming back with some levels, upgrades or new ashes can completely turn the tide for most of them.
The final end game is essentially five bosses in a row. (Six if you count that horrible dragon horse knight, though that jackass does not get a formal boss bar). There are seven mandatory bosses before that, so if you're making the shortest run, nearly half of the bosses you fight will be bunched up at the end.
I'm not even sure how you can play this game as an archer, which incidentally, they said you could before it was released. You'll be hunting sheep or spending runes on ammo all day at the very least, and contending with the absolutely hilarious arrow dodging for the second half of the game.

Bad yet hilarious points!
The arrow dodging! When you release the button to fire a ranged attack, quite a few of the game's enemies will immediately do a dodge move. For arrows, you will most likely miss. Spells meanwhile often take a bit longer to go off, and many track anyway, so the dodge is already finished by the time the spell fires and they stand there and eat it like a doofus. You can use this to your advantage, though, with this one simple trick: The twin marionette ashes fire arrows like its going out of fashion. Any boss or enemy that dodges arrows every time will spend the majority of the fight rolling about instead of attacking, allowing you to wallop them at your leisure. Even better, they generally somehow end up as pincushions.

Overall it's great and I love it, but there is definitely some of the usual From Software nonsense in here.

Game of the Decade I feel it's hard to give a truly comprehensive review with the character limit I instead just made a bulleted list of what I think. I have played every Fromsoft game from demon souls up to nightreign and they continue to not only impress me but show innovation of a genre they created.

The build crafting is at its absolute best in this game. There is an unreal amount of customization with each build that you can truly make if you feel unique to you and what you like. The ways of play are incredibly varied as well making anything that comes to your head possible to a degree after awhile the game can almost feel like a sandbox to just play around in

The world is incredibly detailed; the level design that they are known for is found throughout the entire game; the open world does not allow this to take a hit at all. The world is filled to the brim with locations to explore for all kinds of loot

this game has some of the best bosses in the series While my favorite is ds3 in terms of bosses Elden ring is no slouch either I would put it second to that game in terms of boss design and enjoyment

The questlines that are found within the game are also fantastic every npc feels like a real genuine person in this world

Fromsoft's very iconic art style is also on full display here the worlds lore and art direction is absolutely incredible the atmosphere of this game is truly unmatched as im sure many people already know the world design of this series is top notch

To talk a bit more about game play the game is incredibly fluid. The combat feels fantastic and satisfying to learn and shines the absolute most within its variety and bosses. The game offers incredible depth within its combat as well with not just technical skill but build crafting as well allowing truly creative ideas to shine.

I cannot understate how great it feels that right when you start this game you can really go to 80% of the game. There are a few areas locked behind some progression but you still get to them rather quickly if you gun for em

there is far more positives to give and even more to dive into about why this game is so fantastic but it is important to point out some flaws as I only wish for the game to be better

There still not being a boss rush in this game is a giant missed opportunity, a boss rush mode that gives rewards or even cosmetics. I feel it could add so much to the game they could even make the bosses even more challenging exclusively within this mode to truly test you and your build. This is honestly my biggest complaint with the game; it's such a missed opportunity.

Elden Ring is a massive and epic game with so much to see and do, so many choices, and so many possibilities, that every experience will be different. If you get on and play as if it is a solo Dark Souls style experience, then Elden Ring is almost perfect. That being said, one thing that makes you think about the quality of a game is whether it succeeds at what it sets out do. In that sense, Elden Ring stumbles. One large component of the Elden Ring experience is it's online multiplayer. I got this wanting to play it in a co-op style with my brother. I had an idea of what I was in for, having played Dark Souls type games before, so I knew that summoning an ally would also create the potential for an enemy player to invade my world. This is where the game faltered for me. 80% of the time when I want to fight my way through the areas and bosses with my brother, our game gets invaded. If the invader being a much higher level than us and having a build specifically for PvP weren't bad enough, the game is not good at stopping players who willfully cheat. Many times between my myself, my brother, and a "hunter" player character summoned to help surrounding our enemy and throwing all sorts of attacks at them, they manage to pull off impossible dodges, but more often all of our attacks from all sides simply go through them with no effect, and then they soundly defeat all of us as if completely impervious to all damage. Some of this could have been the unfortunate result of poor netcode and lag; but more often than not it was pure cheating. Something about being forced into involuntary PvP, in which there is no matchmaking based on skill or level, where most opponents obviously cheat greatly ruined much of the enjoyment.

The last straw came when we realized that people can "hack" your game. We noticed something strange happen after getting killed by yet another invader. Afterwards certain, key bosses would not spawn in my brother's game. And we realized there was a problem when after fighting two other bosses, he would not receive key items or equipment drops. So we put in some 400 hours into this game only to have a majority of it spent dealing with one-sided, forced PvP with opponents who are clearly cheating, only to end up with a corrupted, hacked game. Seriously, how do you enjoy it after that? The GM's/ customer service is not particularly speedy or helpful if you make a claim.

I like that this is the most accessible Dark Souls-type of game. The unique areas were truly memorable and their corresponding boss fights were epic. In that sense the game itself is great, my only criticism is some of the generic repetition that you run into with regards to lesser bosses and dungeons. Outside of that the biggest let down has been the multiplayer which has led to my brother's game being hacked. It is just weird how what was supposed to be a great feature about ruins an otherwise epic game.

I want to like this game, I really do. It offers a big chunk of areas to explore, a ridiculously large amount of bosses to fight and challenge yourself with, and an incredible amount of weapons or spells to experiment with. I dumped a lot of hours into this, beat the content, did what I did with the game. But I felt very empty and after getting my butt handed to me over and over by multiple bosses that spam combos killing you in 3 hits even with like 60-70 vigor, the extremely exhausting climb and search to find items in the DLC so you don't get instantly clapped, as well as the PVP being absolutely appalling to play with constant gank squads destroying you each time you wanna honorably duel, I just... stepped away from this game and I do not see myself coming back to it. I played and seen what I wanted to with my 110 hours and that's that. Is the game good by design? Nope, everything is vague and you have to look up lots of YT videos to figure out where to go to next. Yes you can beat the bosses but that also doesn't mean they are well designed, just inflated HP mobs that require DPS checks as well as give you very little to no room to actually engage in fighting with swords.

Like I said there's spells and yadda yadda you can use to breeze through the game, but honestly I seen what I needed to and I cant recommend it for the normal person. You will not have fun with this game unless you got friends joining you and you run the most overpowered spells with ashes to help you win fights. Otherwise I rather just go back to dark souls.

5/10, the DLC is a 3/10 cause you gotta waste time finding items in hidden corners of the map, and if you dont get these items you just get 2-3 shotted by every single boss. So it requires you using YT videos to locate them. Have fun with that nonsense.

Product received for free

For those who don't know anything (and I mean *anything*) about this game, it is hard as hell. It's a Soulsborne game, of course it's gonna be hard. If you're on the edge about it, that's okay. Games like these are not meant for everyone, unlike what some people in the community might tell you.

If that is your only concern though, then just give it a shot. I will not lie when I say that this is probably the most beautiful game I have ever played, both visually and musically. I don't even have the most cutting edge computer to run this on (my processing core is an Intel i3 12th gen), but it is optimized so well that every time I open the game, I am stunned with how beautiful it is.

I'm 28 hours into the game and I still haven't beaten it by myself yet. I have beaten it, but I had a friend practically carry me all the way through via Seamless Co-op (a mod acquired through Nexus Mods, I highly recommend it + installation was incredibly easy). To be honest, I really don't think I would've been motivated enough to try the game by myself if it weren't for him because I'm probably like you, the person reading this review, and was on edge about the difficulty. Once again, if that's all you're worried about, then give it a shot anyway.

This game has taught me so much. Not just how to recognize attack patterns easier and when to dodge. I've learned patience with this game and with myself. This game will force you to change your mindset. If this is your first Soulsborne game, you will (most likely) not beat a story boss on your first try, but you will get better the more you fight. You get better each time. It has also taught me that taking breaks is a great way to help your mind reset so you can jump right back into the fray with a clear mind.

In summary, this game is truly an experience that I'm glad my friend carried my unconscious body through. I have seen growth in my own skills since when I first started the game and will continue to grow as I play, and so will you.

I'll say it once again, if it's the difficulty that scares you, don't be afraid. Pick up your blade and traverse the mist, Tarnished.

Elden Ring :A Visual and Exploratory Epic Feast
Masterpiece of Art Design, Pinnacle of Open Worlds

FromSoftware has once again proven themselves as masters of video game artistry. The artistic design of Elden Ring is nothing short of a textbook masterpiece—from the eerie decay of the Weeping Peninsula to the resplendent grandeur of the Erdtree Capital, every frame is as rich and vivid as a classical oil painting. The collision of Lovecraftian horror in creature design and Gothic architectural aesthetics, coupled with boss battle spectacles that blend brutality and elegance (e.g., Radahn's meteor charge), makes for an unforgettable experience.

The map design completely subverts the mediocrity of typical open worlds. No cookie-cutter markers here—only secrets hidden in cliffside ruins, decaying dragons lurking in mist-covered depths, and countless vertical exploration moments that make you exclaim, "I can go there?!" Even in death, you’ll find yourself marveling, "This place is too beautiful—worth dying for."

Yes, the difficulty remains punishing, but "cheese" strategies are more accessible than ever (spirit ashes, sorceries, horseback combat). If you’re willing to embrace the challenge, this is a visual pilgrimage that easily demands 300+ hours of your life.

### **Elden Ring Review – A Timeless Masterpiece (11/10)**

There are great games, unforgettable games—and then there's **Elden Ring**, a once-in-a-generation experience that transcends genre, expectation, and even the medium itself. FromSoftware didn’t just raise the bar with this title—they launched it into the stars.

As someone who has earned the **Platinum Trophy**, explored the world across **four different endings**, and still finds new details on every revisit, I say this without hesitation: **Elden Ring is the greatest game I have ever played.**

---

#### 🌌 **Story & Worldbuilding: A Myth Unfolding**

Elden Ring doesn't hand you a story—it whispers it, buries it, hides it in shattered ruins and item descriptions. And that’s the brilliance. The narrative is both intimate and cosmic, echoing tragedy, ambition, corruption, and fate.

Each ending offers not just closure, but a distinct worldview:

* The *Age of Fracture* clings to the old order.
* The *Age of Duskborn* invites decay and rebirth.
* The *Lord of Frenzied Flame* tears it all down.
* And *Ranni’s Age of Stars* offers a cold, distant liberation.

Every path feels earned. Every choice leaves you thinking long after the credits roll.

---

#### ⚔️ **Combat: A Symphony of Skill and Style**

Combat in Elden Ring is nothing short of *exhilarating*. Every weapon, spell, and build feels finely tuned. The freedom is staggering: dual-wield katanas, cast ancient sorceries, summon spectral allies, or just punch a dragon in the face.

Bosses aren’t just obstacles—they’re *epic duels*. Facing Malenia, Maliketh, Radagon, and countless others is a test of patience, strategy, and will. And when you finally triumph? That feeling is pure, undiluted glory.

---

#### 🌍 **The World: A Monument to Exploration**

The Lands Between is the most beautifully haunting open world ever created. It doesn’t lead you—it *beckons* you. Every cliff hides a secret. Every door might open to an underground empire.

And just when you think you’ve seen it all, the game reveals *another* hidden legacy dungeon, *another* forgotten god, *another* place that makes your jaw drop. There are no filler quests here. Everything feels deliberate. Everything rewards curiosity.

---

#### 🔁 **Replayability: Infinite Possibility**

With **multiple story branches**, **countless builds**, and **layered lore**, Elden Ring isn’t a game you just play once. It’s a game you live in. After four endings, I’m still discovering new NPC interactions, lore ties, and alternate strategies.

And New Game+ isn’t just harder—it’s *smarter*. It gives you new reasons to reforge your character and reapproach old battles with new eyes.

---

### ⭐ **Final Verdict: 11/10**

**Elden Ring isn’t just a game—it’s a myth you survive, shape, and remember.**
It challenges you. It humbles you. It rewards you with moments of staggering beauty, triumph, and discovery that most games never come close to offering.

I’ve seen its endings. I’ve claimed its throne. I’ve earned its platinum.
And still... I yearn to return.

If you haven’t played it yet, prepare yourself. You’re not just entering a game.
**You’re stepping into legend.**
