One play through the game only, sitting at 288 hours on Steam. I would recommend this game as long as you know what you are getting into. For me, unlike Demons Souls and the Dark Souls games, Elden Ring was (in comparison) much longer, and had a far less cohesive world, but overall was fun to play.
Regarding the length of the game, most of the time it was fun, but the same areas and "things" felt like they were taking way too long. I love the idea of a catacomb being very lengthy if it is filled with lots of different things, but hallway after hallway was filled with the same enemies, over and over. It felt similar above ground, with lots of open areas, but it was as if the game was made large in scope, simply for the sake of saying "there is lots of everything." I mean to say it felt as if they put more of everything, everywhere, but there was no purpose for it, except to be able to say "the game is big" and the game felt like a real slog at times. They even had to giv eus a horse.
The second negative is that the world did not make sense/was not very cohesive at times. The enemies felt as if they were picked from different games, and put into this world with areas, also from different games. You have a stone cat boss with a jerky animation, set in a stone dungeon. Then maybe next you fight another boss in a dungeon that looks the same (more "sameness") but they have a very fluid animation, with beautiful particle effects and flowing light all around. At times it felt as if I was playing a different game, and I don't know how else to describe it.
Now those are negatives only because I felt that this game that just went on more, and more, and more, ultimately kept me outside. Like I could never become part of it.
Positives are many. The sounds and music are superb, as usual for Fromsoft. The player's animations were great. I was always able to pay attention to the enemy and learn from their movement/animations, to defeat them. The game felt well balanced, with just a few uber-enemies sprinkled around, that I think you can almost always ignore/run past if you want. I ran into almost no bugs which is great, and the game's stuttering performance (very unpopular upon release) seem to have been taken care of. It is From Soft, so no microtransaction nonsense, battle passes, loot boxes, or corporate vomit. I did enjoy the game overall, but man... almost 300 hours just to finish it, and I skipped a bunch because of the grind. There is a grind here, hidden behind a game. A good game over all, but still a grindy game. I never felt this with Bloodborne, Demons Souls, Dark Souls, or Sekiro. So, this might be a good game to play for a while, then when you feel like you can't play anymore, just play something else for a while before coming back to this. Either that, or a good weekend coop game with friends. I would give this one an 8/10, minus one for a world that is fragmented/not cohesive, and the grindy length of some areas.

How Elden Ring felt is like .. picture this:

Your plane crashes in the Amazonian jungle. You're maybe hundreds of miles from any form of civilization. You're unhurt but alone and empty-handed. With no other choice, you walk towards where you think safety is. Along the way you are forced to learn quickly how to forage for food and drinking water without dying. You sometimes meet friendly locals who unfortunately don't speak much English and are possibly insanel; what they say is both cryptic and seems unhelpful. You are attacked by or see various dangerous animals and unfriendly locals - failure to defend yourself means you die.

As you walk, you start finding small communities. Most of these people are insane and savage, they want to kill you, some to eat you or worse. They know the lay of the land so their small villages and fortifications will be in the way. Sometimes, if they know about you, they won't leave you alone. You have no choice: you must kill to survive, you must take what's theirs to make yourself stronger. Nothing will stop you from your purpose.

In time, you lose your original clothing and replace it with the clothes of defeated foes and animal pelts. You begin searching for and attacking hostile groups to obtain weapons and gear (or you find a really nice rock at one point and you tie to a large stick - this turns out to be the best weapon you've used). You learn how to hunt and kill efficiently, you start to enjoy yourself, but mistakes you've made and innocents you've caused suffering to because of poor choices, sometimes caused by not listening or understanding the cryptic advice from the friendly madmen, eats at you; deep inside you are still human, you still feel, you would do almost anything to be able to fix those mistakes but you can't. You find new purpose beyond just survival; now you're here to clean this land of sins from the past in a hope to also wash away your guilt.

After months of travel, having defeated all your foes but finding no solace, no conclusion, no clarity, you finally reach civilization. As you vaguely describe your experiences to your rescuers, somebody offers you a bottle of water. As you take your first sip of water that does not taste like mud, moss, or worse things in months, you begin to cry. You realize are no longer someone meant for and able to live in the world you spent so much time looking for.

Posted: August 3
Unexpectedly addictive!
As someone new to soulslikes, I was nervous — the franchise’s brutal reputation precedes it — but Elden Ring surprised me quite pleasantly. The game gradually builds in difficulty — just don’t yeet yourself into Caelid right after Margit — making it easier for less experienced players to adjust to the challenges of the Lands Between — yes, even the crucible knights. The open world system is arguably the biggest asset of the game, making it possible for the tarnished to grind runes or find more suitable equipment, helping players avoid or overcome roadblocks, whether in tough areas or brutal boss fights. On top of that, the world, vast as it is, never feels empty. There are so many dungeons and areas to explore (and suffer in), apart from the main storyline. The quests and characters do a great job of easing you into the sombre world. The graphics and atmosphere of the Lands Between are unmatched in both beauty and in their ability to captivate the player.
The bosses are well-balanced, fun and (mostly) fair. Despite their difficulty, learning their movesets never feels like a grim chore.
My only grievance with this game lies with the fashion souls choices provided for feminine characters, as it would be nice to have a wider variety of pretty gowns.
Overall: 10/10, would suffer again!

I remember the thrill of finally slaying Starscourge Radahn on my Xbox, the controller vibrating in my hands as the world exploded into fireworks. Switching over to Steam felt like slipping a well-worn cloak into a new, more comfortable cut. Every step through Liurnia is smoother, the draw distance makes distant towers loom even more ominously, and the mouse-and-keyboard precision has me deflecting those archdrake fireballs with a confidence I never had before.

It’s the little moments that still get me: sneaking past an ill-tempered ogre to find a hidden chest, the sun filtering through broken cathedral windows and casting shifting shadows on the cracked stone floor, or the way the audio swells when you glimpse a boss arena for the first time. On PC, those details feel sharper—like I’m discovering every nook and cranny all over again, even though I’ve already beaten the game once.

I’ve lost count of how many times I’ve retooled my build in the character menu, experimenting with spells and ashes of war I missed the first time around. The vibrant Steam community is already pumping out mods that spice up my journey—new armor skins, extra challenges, even entire questlines. It’s like Elden Ring has grown its own heartbeat beyond what I thought possible.

Finishing the story felt monumental on Xbox, but here on Steam, it’s less about “finishing” and more about falling in love with the Lands Between all over again. I’m chasing hidden endings, summoning friends for co-op duels, and carving out fresh memories in places I thought I’d already mastered. If you’re on the fence about taking your Xbox save to PC, trust me: the adventure only deepens with every frame and every mod you embrace.

The game feels like an evolution of Dark Souls into an open world format, which is why I jokingly refer to it as Dark Souls 4. That being said, it does have a unique feel to the gameplay and enough fresh tools and tactics that you could get a ton of replayability out of it. Another gem from FromSoftware. The only things I would gripe about are the quality of the PC port and the overall graphical quality of the game. Character models in cutscenes look low quality on the highest settings, "ray tracing" barely seems to add anything, and the game is unfortunately capped at 60 FPS. I wish FromSoftware would treat PC players as first class citizens for once, though I know that's a shortcoming of most Japanese studios. All in all, this was a fantasticly fun game, and I will probably NG+ it at some later date (hopefully with some graphics mods next time).

I had a pretty interesting experience with it. Around 50 hours into the game, I went online to look up more about the story, the lore — just to understand what’s going on, what kind of ending I might be heading toward, maybe what optional things I should do. And I ended up getting sucked in for several hours, diving deep into the lore of this game, its universe, its characters. I took notes, tried to really understand everything, as much as I could, because honestly, I think that’s the most amazing and interesting part of the game.

It’s incredibly well done. The story, the lore they built — I feel like I’ve never seen anything like this in any video game before. Nothing even close. It’s something almost unimaginably high-level. They created a piece of art filled with a mix of huge, fascinating ideas and unbelievably cool characters. I would describe it as an epic anime-style story, but with heavy references to Nietzschean philosophy and smart, powerful feminist ideas. It’s like a distilled essence of everything that’s great in the world.

And when the credits rolled and I saw that George R. R. Martin had contributed to the writing — oh, suddenly everything made sense.

I got this game in 2022, when it first came out, but my old potato PC couldn’t run this at all, so I waited until recently I finally got a new PC to finally be able to play this game I’ve looked forward to for so long. And I have to say it DID NOT DISAPPOINT, but SURPASSED all my expectations.

Dark souls 2 has been my favorite souls game out of all the fromsoft releases on PC, but after playing and getting the platinum for this, I have to say these two are my all time favorite souls games or just games in general now.

It is a huge step for souls games to take on a open world approach, and I think elden ring did really well on this aspect, there are really cool dungeons and items you can find by exploring, and that was something we never had in previous souls games. But there are still minor issues like fall damage and certain platforming sections I feel like could be made better, but overall the open-world is such a great addition of flavor into the souls series.

The art and music is breath-taking, just like all the other souls games. Combat-wise is smooth, and I feel like this is the easiest souls game so far, since spirit ashes are available for most of the boss fights, but it is up to the player to decide whether to use it or not, I think is quite beginner friendly. Also, bleed and dragon incantations are so broken but I absolutely love it, dragonmaw is so OP.

The boss designs are fantastic, I’ve enjoyed most of the bosses in this game. Rennala’s second phase arena is magical, and Radahn’s festival boss fight is so epic and I love it.

This game felt like a fever dream, I can’t forget the nights I stayed up late trying to finish another dungeon or finishing off a boss, it’s so addicting.

Highly recommend this game to all souls lovers.

There's really nothing else to say, it's a story away from the greatest game of all time.

Elden Ring – The Souls GOAT

Story
From the first step into the Lands Between, Elden Ring doesn’t simply tell a story. It immerses you in a myth that feels older than time, written in broken statues, buried secrets, and whispers carried by the wind. There are no lengthy cutscenes holding your hand. Instead, the story is pieced together through exploration, cryptic dialogue, item descriptions, and sheer curiosity. You become the story.

You're not a hero. You're not the chosen one. You're a Tarnished, cast out, forgotten, crawling back toward the light... or deeper into the dark. Your fate is not written. That freedom, that mystery, that dread—it's exhilarating.


Graphics
This isn't just about high-resolution textures or ray-traced shadows. It's about atmosphere. Every inch of the map breathes with artistic intent. The golden trees looming in the distance. The ruined castles lost to rot. The grotesque beauty of Caelid’s red sky. From the gentle snowfall of the Mountaintops to the silent echoes of Nokron, Elden Ring is a masterclass in environmental storytelling.

Even the enemies tell stories with their bodies—how they move, what they wear, the way they scream.

Soundtrack
Every track in Elden Ring carries the soul of a broken empire. Boss themes range from melancholic orchestras to pulse-pounding, god-slaying symphonies. The main theme alone could bring tears if you close your eyes and just listen.

But sometimes, the silence speaks louder. The moment you’re exploring and all you hear is the crunch of your boots, the distant howl of wind, or the sudden screech of something that wants you dead—that’s immersion.



Gameplay
This is where Elden Ring transcends. Tight combat, vast build variety, and unmatched freedom. Want to be a sorcerer who rains down stars? A katana-wielding blood god? A naked dude with a club? Go ahead. The game won’t stop you. It might laugh at you, but it won’t stop you.

The open world is dense, rewarding, and genuinely dangerous. Every corner hides a surprise. Some are treasures. Most are death. The boss design is brutal but fair—if you die, it’s your fault, not the game’s.

Summoning spirits, discovering secret walls, dueling invaders, collecting absurdly cool weapons—everything feels meaningful. Progress isn't just leveling up. It's learning. It's getting better. It's becoming legend.

Cons
The UI could use some quality-of-life improvements.

Certain late-game bosses feel more like tests of patience than skill.

Performance issues at launch (mostly fixed now).

Quest tracking is vague, which is great for immersion but frustrating for some players.


Summary
Elden Ring is not just a game. It’s an experience. A place to get lost, to suffer, to grow, to discover stories untold and monsters unimaginable. It’s a triumph of world design, a new peak in open-world gaming, and a spiritual successor to everything FromSoftware has been building toward.

Conclusion
There are games that entertain. Games that challenge. Games that immerse. Elden Ring does all of that and more. It respects your intelligence, rewards your curiosity, and punishes your arrogance. It made me feel small, then made me fight gods. It whispered mysteries and let me chase them. It kicked my teeth in and made me say "thank you".



10/10. Not just Game of the Year. One of the greatest games ever made.

Elden Ring is an absolute triumph of game design. FromSoftware has taken the best elements of the Soulsborne formula and expanded them into a vast, open-world sandbox.

The build diversity is phenomenal. Magic, melee, ranged, summons—everything feels viable with the right approach, and there's a staggering number of weapons, spells, and tools to experiment with. Elden Ring lets you play it the way you want to. My first playthrough I went with a Guts from Berserk build. My second playthrough I am now trying a Sorceress build and loving every moment of it.

The boss fights are brutal, beautiful, and unforgettable. I'll never forget how excited I was after beating Malenia. If you enjoy FromSoftware games and open world games, this game is the perfect marriage of those two genres. This game is a 10/10, masterpiece, and must play.

A true double edged sword, and not the STR+INT build kind.

The barrier for entry is very unforgiving, but it does stand as a very strong test of your willpower and perseverance - something you'll need to get to the end of the game. The difference between this and something like Bloodborne is the open-world. If you don't like or can't beat a boss - just go elsewhere and do something else, there's no shame in that.

That's where the double-edged sword part comes in. Once it clicks - this game becomes so good, you can't help but compare anything else you play to it, and immediately think "why am I bothering with this if Elden Ring is better". It's a curse.

And when you're bored of the game...
..install Seamless Coop and now you're going through it all over again :)

Posted: August 1
If you like Souls games, then this is more of the same - just bigger and more expansive than ever. For the most part it's a game that rewards learning from your mistakes and generally that feels satisfying to overcome the trials and tribulations you will encounter in the Land's Between. I love some of the new mechanics like Stakes of Marika that more or less eliminate pointless "runback" times you'd find in previous Souls games when trying to overcome a boss. But I do believe it drops the ball on the way to the Endgame, and loses sight of the core "Punishing, but fair" philosophy and replaces that with "Punishing with OTT anime visual flair". If I could offer a "Mixed Review" I think I would because by the time the final boss was felled and the end credits rolled, I didn't feel triumph, only relief that I was done. That's not a nice feeling to end a game on, especially one that's so large in scale it can easily command >100hrs of time for your first play though.

But given my options are "Yes - Recommend" or "No - Don't", I feel compelled to choose "Yes", because from the moment you step into Limgrave all the way to the foot of the Erdtree, it was of the greatest adventures I've had in video games, and towards the endgame I feel the cracks starting to show and the bosses are too cheaply dialled up in difficulty, but I marched through that gauntlet because I was so besotted by how magnifcient the early and mid game were.

Liurnia and Siofra were some of the most beautiful areas to explore. Stormveil and Raya Lucaria are contenders for the best 'legacy dungeons' in the Souls universe, and the Weeping Penisula was such a well-designed "Tutorial Island" to help set you up for the rest of the game. There was plenty of catacombs, caves and other freaky places to explore which helped keep things interesting as you explored around but as the game goes on you start to see just how much content they recycled: not just enemies but exact same rooms in many of these optional dungeons, and the rewards for clearing them didn't feel worth their time investment. I'd probably skip these in subsequent playthroughs unless I needed to clear them for quests / specific items. That sense of recycling continues throughout the game with bosses, and you'll meet clones of earlier bosses and enemies all the way through the game, and not always in arenas suitable for them, either. It's almost like the incredible scale of the world they created massively outgrew their designs to fill it, and they had to use a lot of ctrl c ctrl v in order to meet their release deadline. I know From is well known for recycling content and assets in their games, but this game set a new bar for them.

Was it worth the time? Absolutely. Would I come back? Maybe. I think a lot of the awestruck moments this game offers whilst exploring will wither and wain after the first time. Some things will still excite me, others will make me say never again. Some bosses will always be hoot to duel, others will have me rolling my eyes and I think that's the fundamental flaw this game has: it rollercoasters between exceptional highs and god-awful lows, and unlike a well balanced ride that distributes them evenly throughout the experience: Elden Ring is guilty of frontloading the good stuff.

P.S. I've only just learned you can ride Torrent in the final boss fight. I'm not sure if that would have made the fight better but it would certainly have made it more bearable, and that only fuels my speculation that the endgame received the least QA testing and feedback.

finished base game, SotE and played a bit with Ng+, time for a review...
Not a big souls game expert, I'm trying to remediate that now by playing most of the classics, ER + dlc just happened to be my 1st title (at least full 3d one, I've already did Mandragora and No Rest for the wicked which kind of sparked my interest in the genre)
Let me start with the bad, and as You will quickly notice, it's mostly nitpicking:
- no item filter - for a game that kind of expects You to hoard items (especially weapons and talismans) an item filter would go a loooong way, of course after tens/hundreds of hours you kind of know the icons by heart..but still
- another QoL I would like to see and connected to the previous point is the lack of item sets - I wish I could bind a full set with a hotkey (it was so painful for me to juggle between talismans for different encounters, or quick slot items like pots etc - again no filter which meant every time I needed to scroll tens of icons to reequip something I was looking for)
- tooltips/item descriptions - it actually took me a good amount of hours into the game to notice that some items do actually have bonuses (like stat helms and such) and the only way to find it is to read the descriptions (which again are kind of hidden...), I mean You already have the status effect for weapons mentioned separately so why not mention everything else also?
- talking with NPCs / quests - I understand that part of the appeal of Dark Souls and ER is the obscurity (previous point regarding the item description kind of proves it also) but it seems to be a bit much sometimes...exploring a grace locking You out of a quest or expecting to click the same npc couple of times to finish the dialog proper is a strange design choice, again not a big deal, but I quickly noticed that if I actually want to see everything I probably have to follow a guide so that I don't screw up my playthrough.

And that is about it, now the good...well I'm not sure what to write other than the game is a modern MASTERPIECE.
All the important bits (music, ambiance, game-play, world-building, dialogue, enemy design, versatility of builds, combat mechanics) it's all TOP TIER.
The game holds You by the throat and doesn't let go until You commit and finish the next encounter and then the next and the next, until the end of the game.
I really found myself caring for the NPCs around me (and for obvious reasons..spoilers I guess...I was damn sad so many times).

It's worth every dollar, it's worth Your time, You have to try it even if You think You are a filthy casual and are scared of the difficulty (in my opinion heavily exaggerated, yes it has it's challenges but also multiple ways to approach a problem with the multiple tools You are given).

10/10

Summary: Elden Ring is a masterpiece of open-world action RPG, seamlessly blending challenging combat with a vast and captivating world. While the difficulty can be punishing, the rewards are immeasurable, making it a must-play for fans of the genre.
Pros:
Vast and Immersive Open World:
The Lands Between is a breathtakingly detailed and expansive world, ripe for exploration and brimming with secrets.
Challenging but Rewarding Combat:
Elden Ring's combat system is demanding, requiring precision and strategic thinking, but the sense of accomplishment after overcoming a difficult boss is unparalleled.
Deep and Engaging Story:
The lore, crafted by Hidetaka Miyazaki and George R.R. Martin, is rich and mysterious, drawing players into a compelling narrative.
Character Customization:
A wide array of classes, weapons, and armor allows for diverse playstyles and builds.
Stunning Visuals and Soundtrack:
The game's art direction and musical score are both exceptional, creating a truly immersive atmosphere.
Cons:
Steep Learning Curve:
The game's difficulty can be a barrier to entry for new players, requiring patience and persistence.
Performance Issues (on some systems):
While generally well-optimized, some players have reported performance problems, particularly on PC.
Can be overwhelming:
The sheer size and scope of the game can feel daunting, even for experienced players.
Overall:
Elden Ring is a triumph of the action RPG genre, delivering a challenging and rewarding experience that will stay with players long after they've put down the controller. Its vast open world, intricate combat, and compelling story make it a must-play for anyone seeking a truly epic adventure. Highly recommended.
Rating: 5/5 Stars
Note: This review is tailored for a Steam audience, highlighting key features and potential concerns. You can adjust the tone and focus based on your specific preferences.

I would never recommend this game to anyone because I think any one of the Dark Souls trilogy is a better experience. Is Elden Ring a bad game? No. Is it a good game? Also no. It has got a lot of flaws that really hold it back. The most egregious I would say are the unusual interactions things have with uneven ground in this game. Player actions can get cancelled by what look to be very minor changes in elevation e.g. You were on a small branch and then went on the grass. I've seen larger enemies partially clip under the ground and with the amount of uneven ground in the game it feels really sloppy. The game also feels very padded out. It is so long and you fight so many of the same enemies and bosses. Some areas also struggle a bit with having a distinct identity due to this. I think if the game had been a more condensed and focused experience it would be a lot better.

Elden Ring has become a classic by now, and it's clearly deserved, but why is it such a cult?

Dark Souls was already a cult, and it would be dumb to not expect its brother to become famous and well-liked, but Elden Ring widened the horizion of Souls-like games, making them more accessible.

Elden Ring is a good middle ground in regards to difficulty and it has a gigantic open world that grants different ways to level up based on one's skills, experience and patience. The map has vastly different types of sceneries, making it always stimulating to the player; farming is not so boring after all, especially when each place has its own hidden gems.

The typical characteristics of a souls-like remain, and are even enhanced; the lore is hidden in items and hushed words, in incantations and names... but one can also play by ignoring the story. The player has countless kinds of freedoms.

The developers had their heads up their asses when they made some bosses, but well, this is also part of the souls-like experience.

I think Elden Ring is the perfect first game for an initiate; it might feel overwhelming, but there's no need to rush it. The game gives time and methods to learn. Skill comes with practice. :)

Don't get intimidated and try again, and again until you make it. That's the best part! Especially my girlies, try it, ♥♥♥♥ what anyone else says!

I never expected a video game to fundamentally shift the way I see art, storytelling, or even myself. But Elden Ring did exactly that. It isn’t just a game. It’s an experience — one that challenged me, humbled me, and ultimately transformed me.

From the moment I first set foot in the Lands Between, I was completely immersed. The world is vast, hauntingly beautiful, and filled with secrets that beg to be discovered. Every crumbling ruin, every eerie dungeon, every horizon painted in gold — it all feels alive, almost sacred. Elden Ring doesn't hold your hand, and that’s exactly why it’s so powerful. It trusts the player. It dares you to explore, to fail, to learn, and to grow.

The combat is tight and deliberate, yet incredibly varied. I could approach situations however I wanted — with brute strength, precise spells, stealth, or even just running away and coming back stronger later. Every death felt fair, and every victory felt earned. There’s something deeply satisfying about conquering a seemingly impossible boss after hours of trial and error. It teaches perseverance. It teaches patience. And most of all, it teaches you to believe in yourself.

But what truly sets Elden Ring apart is its storytelling — subtle, fragmented, and profound. The lore, much of it hidden in item descriptions and environmental clues, weaves a dark, beautiful mythos that I still think about daily. George R.R. Martin's influence is unmistakable, but it’s FromSoftware’s enigmatic style that gives the story its soul. It’s not spoon-fed to you. You uncover it, piece by piece, and you become the author of your journey.

I’ve laughed, cursed, cried, and sat in stunned silence because of this game. It rekindled a sense of wonder I hadn’t felt since I was a kid — that feeling of true adventure, of stepping into the unknown and becoming something more than you were. It taught me that beauty often lies in struggle, and that the hardest paths often lead to the most rewarding destinations.

Elden Ring changed how I see games, but also how I approach challenges in life. It made me more resilient, more curious, and more grateful for art that dares to challenge the player instead of just entertaining them. It is, without a doubt, the greatest game ever made.

10/10. A masterpiece beyond compare.

10/10. I love this game so much and stuff related to this game. I love the Lands Between, I love the boss fights, I love the dragon fights, I love Ranni, her mother & her aunt, I love watching Vaatividya lore videos after I finish boss fights or certain areas in the game.

Honestly this might just have the most accessible and dynamic playstyle of all the SoulsBorneKiro games. You have a plethora of playstyles, lots of different types of builds and since it's open world, you can explore other areas before progressing in the game if things look very difficult. A lot of fun boss fights, both in base game and DLC. Combat is simple like all previous souls games, but Spells, Incantations and Ashes of War make way for many variations.

Visually the game is gorgeous, but stutters expected in few areas, a few more in DLC sadly. I had to play at the lowest 720p settings and I didn't get the good graphics but its still a good looking game imho. The audio experience in Souls games remains more or less same, each entry having their own iconic bgm along with the similar sfx when spells are cast or swords clash.

This game has tons of QoL changes from previous Souls games but the camera & enemy locking can still be annoying to some people. Being someone who's played the Dark Souls games and Sekiro, I'm just used to avoiding those issues altogether. Also, a lot of optional minidungeons/bosses/mini bosses can be lackluster with how often you'd find recycled stuff here and there.

I’ve always been a completionist, and before even buying Elden Ring back in 2023, I had already looked up a 100% guide, even though it turned out to be a YouTube playlist. After a while, I realized I was just copying what the person in the video was doing, without really understanding what I was doing or why. It didn’t even cover everything properly, so I eventually dropped the game.

Almost a year later, I decided to give Elden Ring another chance, this time using a really well-made spreadsheet that organized everything in a way that made sense. Once I got the hang of the guide, I stopped feeling overwhelmed and finally started to fall in love with the world and the characters.

This time, I truly did 100% of everything + DLC: every boss, questline, and hidden area, and honestly there wasn’t a single part that I found boring or poorly designed. It was a completely enjoyable experience from start to finish, one of those games that once you finish it, you immediately wish you could feel that sense of discovery and accomplishment all over again. It was also my first real Souls-like, and thanks to the insane variety of possible builds, I was able to find a playstyle that really suited me. Something I noticed though, was that Elden Ring gave me a kind of anxiety while playing. Not in a bad way, but in that tense, heart-pounding way that keeps you fully immersed. I even avoided playing it late at night because it made it harder to relax afterwards. Strangely enough, that tension became part of what made the experience so memorable.

In the end, Elden Ring became one of my favorite games ever, and that’s coming from someone who never really cared about Souls-likes before. It’s nice to find a game that feels this complete, alive, and rewarding to explore, and give you the feeling to replay it again and again.

Elden RIng was a disappointment. DOn't get me wrong, I loved playing it.
I had a lot of fun with ER, probably still will.
I just think that; from the asset flipping with no changes other than visual to the poorly design and utterly boring boss designs (In most cases. Godrick, Radhan, Margit, that one boss in the church of the bud, and Mohg if the game rng is alright, are amazing and I like fighting them) the game is just shovelware and/or just visual spectacle. Which I don't mind, but I didn't pay 160$, for the whole game+dlc for a mediocre story, a bunch of flashy visual effects, 99% of the sounds being unoriginal and literally ripped from royalty free sfx library that EVERYONE has used. I was hoping for something reminescent of Dark Souls but not just a texture pack with the worse open world gameplay I have ever experienced.

Demon's Soul was a great start.
Dark Souls 1 was the more truly achieved vision.
Dark Souls 2 was where a lot of great things were done, with the exception of some questionable additions, and is a charming sequel; Yet it was mocked because people can't fathom understanding that DS2 was not meant to just be a copy pasted experience (this will be relevant in a bit).
Bloodborne was an amazing step in a very good direction for a faster paced game while staying similar to Dark Souls in many aspect.
Dark Souls 3 was the start of Fromsoftware not willing to take risks and doing the bare minimum while selling their fans a product they had already delivered many years ago.
Sekiro (I cannot speak for this one, never played it. I would say it's definitely the most creative and unique one of their """Souls like"""" games)

And then Elden Ring; A trend chasing, hollow asset flip, visually interesting, creatively bankrupt gameplay wise, game that has nothing to offer. Not even the open world is good, despite being the main selling point of that game. Open world without direction is cool but not when 90% of the locations punishes you for even exploring by "rewarding" you something insanely useless. THe 10% is mostly mediocre finds with barely anything worth noting.

I've had this game since day one (started on PlayStation when it came out) and I don't necessarily regret the time I spent on this game but I do regret ever buying it. I would have rather spent those dollars on better and more passionately developped games than this measly excuse of a game.

This game is a "difficult game" in the same ways horrors filled with scare-jumps are "scary". It's not a good design; it is a design based on a concept that someone will be too stubborn to leave, even if it's bad.

The game is poorly balanced and way overhyped. This is a game from 2022 and gives a vibe of 2010.
Graphics are poor, keybinding for PC is a nightmare, and the entire game feels clunky (dodge rolls feel heavy and delayed).
Worse of all, the game is not giving you that natural feedback - if you do something wrong in a difficult game, you should know what it is - let's take parry mechanic for an example - you try, you fail. And what now? How can you improve? You don't know. Because it gives no damn clue if it was too fast, too slow, that it was an unblockable attack, or maybe you have the wrong shield for that type of attack. You either look ♥♥♥♥♥ up on YT or just ram your head into the wall, maybe figuring something out after a few hours. I guess it can be pleasurable for some, but the game itself isn't that good.

On the good side, tho: the vibe is amazing and exploration feels satisfying.

Posted: July 29
I'm not sure how I feel about the open world in this game. On the one hand, it is incredible how much there is to find, and how good it feels to explore. There are ruins and NPCs and little bits of lore and items to find everywhere, and it definitely feels like you're being rewarded for exploring. The visuals -- the vistas! -- were incredible. The view from the Lakeview grace looking into Liurnia, the first entry into Caelid, seeing the Erdtree for the first time; there's just so many awe-inspiring views in this game. Everything looks incredible, and each biome is beautifully crafted, with believable transitions.

However, all that freedom comes at the price of purpose -- I had a lot of trouble understanding where I was supposed to go. Usually when this happens in a game, the difficulty of a given area will tell me, but this isn't just any game. It's a FromSoft game. Which means that a spike in difficulty can be because I've gone the wrong way, or simply because I need to get good. There's that guidance from the grace, but sometimes there's two that point in different directions, and even if there's only one, it might just lead you into a brick wall like , when you're actually supposed to explore more. So I did explore more. And probably too much, because I overshot the average playtime quite a bit, and I was massively overleveled. Luckily I suck at the game, so it didn't trivialise the game for me, but I did do some areas way out of order, which led to some parts being incredibly hard and others pretty easy.

Also, the mini dungeons were cool initially, but they started to repeat a bit too much, and the lack of narrative cohesion and flow made them more administrative than exciting. It was nice to be able to find them, but all of them being so similar kind of sucked the joy out of it; I eventually stopped exploring them much, and just went for the main dungeons, which feel handcrafted (sometimes by a sadistic madman, but handcrafted nonetheless) and has that narrative cohesion and flow that makes it feel real and alive.

As for the combat and bosses, I really enjoyed it. The combat feels smooth and responsive, and I like the pace. My biggest complaint is the arbitrary waiting in the middle of attacks the bosses and enemies do to catch you out. I suppose it just takes some getting used to, but it was annoying. I much prefer the enemies that just attacks you straight with natural feeling movements, so I can intuitively dodge, rather than just rote learning the pattern and wait times. (However, I should say that learning to look for the actual attack definitely improved my ability to sightread the micro movements that gave away the incoming attack, which felt pretty cool.)

I beat most of the main bosses, and some of the major optional ones, and though beating was certainly my highest achievement, my favourite encounter was , whose armour I eventually donned. When I finally beat him, I got a Sekiro-esque feeling, where it felt like I actually understood his moveset and could sightread his attacks. Dodging and blocking and attacking at the right moments, like a dance. The feeling after beating him was maybe the best moment I had in this game.

So, while I think the open world loses a bit of the magic of the linear dungeons I've come to love, this is still an excellent game, and an excellent open world. It has all the combat and bosses I love from previous Soulsborne games, with an absolutely massive open world that is a delight to explore (for the most part).

I should start off by staying I generally don't play Medieval Fantasy games but I can't get enough of this one. I figured I 'might' because of all the hype but I was ready to not like it. Took a long time to figure out how to do things like Level Up. I think I was level 24 when I figured it out. Played for many... MANY hours without having any actual objective, just rode around looking for dungeons for loot and weapons. Was probably about 48 hours in when I found the first map section. It wasn't until shortly after I realized I hadn't been in Stormveil Castle yet, and it was quickly after that when I realized I needed better weapons and combat skills. Having said that, there is a conspicuous amount of content online to help you speed things along. I'm only what I assume is about half way through and I'm already planning my second play through. This game definitely falls in line with the other Fromsoft games I've played: dark, slightly twisted concepts supporting the plot, an incredibly in-depth world with its own history, mythology, culture, and physics. You need to pay attention to the mechanics around HP, FP, and Stamina, and how those in turn affect your weapons. I spent a LOT of effort upgrading a shield only to find out later that I had never stunned an enemy in combat, because THE SHIELD didn't have the "parry" ability. When I finally started using one with "parry" it was like the game got a little easier. I only knew about this because I watched a video about parrying in which the narrator said, "some shields have it and some don't." The game is challenging, to put it mildly, and to enjoy the game I believe you must be ready to spend an inordinate amount of time learning the games mechanics, those specific to each unique opponent. The story is engrossing and game play is addictive and very enjoyable, and I recommend this game to anyone who is willing to put in the time an effort required to follow through.

I’m not sure if adding yet another positive review among so many makes much difference, but here are my two cents.

On the positive side, there is impressive world-building in a dark fantasy setting, with striking visuals that are both dark and vibrant, even on the Steam Deck, where I experienced almost the entire game. The bosses are interesting and memorable, and the challenge is well balanced, although it is quite easy to overlevel and make the game significantly easier. There is also a wide range of combat mechanics and equipment, allowing you to play the game in your own way, adapt, and overcome.

On the negative side, although the pacing and exploration are excellent, certain dungeons felt repetitive in terms of their design. The storytelling is also somewhat lacking, with snippets of narrative delivered sporadically through short dialogue or item descriptions. It often requires close attention to piece together what is happening, or turning to YouTube videos to gain a clearer understanding and become fully invested in the story.

Overall, one of the best games I have had the pleasure to experience!

This is quite a unique game. It had me playing non-stop for days, despite having so many flaws and quirks in it, but maybe that's what adds to the experience. Despite being a best-seller AAA game, having incredible art direction and really good music, it's a very janky experience and a lot of things about it feel are so far away from what you would expect from a modern game. Things are not explained, quests can break easily, the triggers for them can be jank af, and some things really feel like they don't even make sense.

And yet, the exploration feels amazing, the combat eventually feels natural, and there is just so much to discover in a super dense world filled with things to do. Also, don't be scared of the "difficult" reputation of these games, it's more of a "This game is as difficult as you want it to be" deal honestly. You can just build in so many ways, and have a lot of options to help you in battles that you can trivialize the game if you really want.

I guess I really understand now when people call it a flawed masterpiece, but in the end, guess what really comes through is that it's a game that you can still feel that it was made with passion and care, and focused and made with the desires and needs of the developer than trying to cater too much to a demographic or something, for better or worse. It will certainly stay in my memory for being a unique experience and I feel like that's what's really valuable sometimes.

Posted: July 27
This is my first Souls game. I finished it without the DLC (yet). The early to mid-game is super hard as you're learning a lot (Margit is the real teacher here. Avoid summons, learn from him), but once you figure out your build and enjoy exploring, you can easily out-level much of the map. Toward the end, I found myself craving bosses, but they were eventually easier than I expected. Malenia was the absolute winner in terms of boss design. The world design is phenomenal, with lots of armor sets and weapons.

What I didn’t like about the game were the platforming, quest design (no one says anything that makes sense), the sheer amount of items with unclear explanations, 'hidden' traits in weapons like the Sacrificial Axe restoring mana, and how confusing is the last room towards the ending (to not spoil anything). A lot of these are things considered normal in Souls games, but they feel outdated nowadays.

I’ll definitely play again now that I understand most things. This is a solid 9/10 game.

The only contact i had with a FromSoftware game was when a friend showed me Dark Souls 3. And he was so good at that game he literally made me believe it can`t be /THAT/ hard. When I started Elden Ring (literally 2 weeks or so ago) I was full of hope and whimsy. 'I will breeze through this game like there is no tomorrow'. I told myself full of confidence.

Now, 40 hours into the game i can wholeheartedly say that i was so naive. And i am so glad i was. This game is a testament of my patience and also ambition. I think i fought Godrick over 30 times. The Magma Wyrm that gives Moonveil Katana even more times. I was screaming, crying, i was so ready to give up. But I didnt. With the help of my trusting Torrent, not only i got to explore such a beautiful world, but also get stronger!!! No boss could make me give up such a immersive world. Even if they make me lose 20k runes, even if by many people`s standards i should just quit!

What i love the most if that i can truly see the progress! Even though Rennala isn`t that hard of a boss, i managed to beat her second try. I can dodge some attack patterns of some enemies without the panic rolling. It is a huge improvement. Not to mention how good the lore is. I want to know EVERYTHING about this world!Everything!

I am looking forward to seeing what this game has prepared for me. ( i am NOT looking forward to the Fire Giant tho)

open world darksouls. Just as it says.

Downside? There is. Navigation is a pain in the butt and resources are practically depends on your time consuming especially on your first time playing. There's no really objective markers aside of custom ones and it is up to you to know where to go if you listen to the NPC dialogs let alone if you even know the map at all. That being said. There ARE limitation on the exploring part. Despite being open world, this game do put you in a lot of situation where you really have to go pass through certain area that's full of no-no and trying to go around or explore elsewhere will lead you to a cliff or massive mountain walls if the path isn't already filled with annoying dangers which no rewards whatsoever. Getting lost and then spending times not knowing where to go can lead to boredom. So be mindful of that.

Otherwise. Yes. Open world darksouls fun.
